Disease_description:
Treatment Task: I am a 52 year old woman and I have a terrible stomach ache. I had something like this 2 years ago! I also feel sick, sweaty and have a fever at 38.2 degrees. The pain is more in the left side of the stomach, more down - kind of a pressure pain. I have been suffering from hypertension and widespread athrosis for a long time. The only medications I take are Ramipril and HCT. My doctor has diagnosed me with Diverticulitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Diverticulitis
 
  Model Output:Oral antibiotics (if mild case) – First-line treatment for uncomplicated diverticulitis includes antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin + metronidazole or amoxicillin/clavulanic acid to target gut bacteria.
 
 Hospitalization and intravenous (IV) antibiotics (if severe case) – If the pain worsens, fever rises, or complications (e.g., abscess, perforation) are suspected, hospitalization for IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone + metronidazole or piperacillin/tazobactam) may be required.
 
 Dietary modifications – Initially, a low-fiber or liquid diet can help reduce bowel irritation. After recovery, a gradual reintroduction of fiber is recommended to prevent future episodes.
 
 Pain management (avoid NSAIDs!) – Since you have widespread osteoarthritis, you may be tempted to take NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen), but these can worsen diverticulitis. Instead, acetaminophen (paracetamol) is the preferred pain reliever.
 
 Surgical consultation (if recurrent or complicated cases) – If you experience repeated episodes or develop complications (abscess, perforation, fistula), elective sigmoid resection may be considered to prevent future attacks.

Treatment Task: I am a 67-year-old woman, a farmer by profession. I have had muscle pain all over for 4 months. I am exhausted and can hardly sleep. At first it was more the neck. Then it moved to the hands. But most of all in the upper arm. But it also goes from my back to the back of my knees. I could hardly move my legs. For example, I have to lift my leg out when I get out of the car. When I get up from a sitting position, I first bend down until I can walk. That's how much it hurt my back. Also in the knees. It was especially bad in my arms at night. I couldn't stretch because there was always a pulling sensation at the bottom. I thought that it came from the stomach. My doctor has diagnosed me with Polymyalgia rheumatica. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Polymyalgia rheumatica
 
  Model Output:Glucocorticoids (Prednisone, initially 15–25 mg daily) – First-line treatment to rapidly reduce inflammation and relieve pain and stiffness, followed by a slow taper over months to prevent relapse.
 
 Calcium and Vitamin D Supplementation – Helps prevent osteoporosis due to long-term steroid use, which increases fracture risk, especially in postmenopausal women.
 
 Methotrexate (or other steroid-sparing immunosuppressants, if needed) – Can be used in cases requiring prolonged steroid therapy to reduce glucocorticoid side effects.
 
 Physical Therapy and Gentle Exercise – Helps maintain mobility, reduce stiffness, and prevent muscle wasting due to decreased activity from pain.
 
 Regular Monitoring (Blood Tests and Bone Density Checks) – CRP/ESR levels should be monitored to track inflammation, and DEXA scans may be needed to assess bone health during steroid therapy.

Treatment Task: My one-year-old daughter had a persistent high fever up to 40 degrees. I have already been to the doctor's office and there it was classified as "uncharacteristic fever". Other than a reddened throat, I didn't notice anything else. We were prescribed an antibiotic two days ago (clarithromycin) because the fever was still persistent. She responded poorly to paracetamol. Now the fever is finally gone, but a rash has appeared on the abdomen. My doctor has diagnosed me with Roseola/Sixth disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Roseola/Sixth disease
 
  Model Output:1. Supportive Care & Hydration
 Ensure adequate fluid intake (breast milk, formula, water, or electrolyte solutions) to prevent dehydration.
 Offer small, frequent meals if appetite is reduced.
 2. Fever Management (If Needed)
 If the fever returns or is high enough to cause discomfort, use:
 Paracetamol (Acetaminophen): Given according to the weight-based dosage (avoid overdosing).
 Ibuprofen (if older than 6 months): Can be used as an alternative if fever is high or paracetamol is ineffective.
 Avoid aspirin (due to the risk of Reye’s syndrome).
 3. Skin Care for Rash
 No specific treatment is needed for the rash, as it typically resolves within a few days.
 Keep skin cool and comfortable with loose, breathable clothing.
 If itching occurs (though rare), calamine lotion or a gentle moisturizer may help.
 4. Rest & Comfort
 Ensure your child gets enough rest.
 Keep the room cool and well-ventilated to avoid overheating.
 Gentle cuddling and soothing can help with post-fever irritability.
 5. Monitor for Complications
 Although rare, febrile seizures can occur in some children due to high fever.
 Watch for signs of dehydration (e.g., dry mouth, fewer wet diapers, extreme lethargy).
 If symptoms worsen (persistent fever, difficulty breathing, lethargy), return to the doctor.

Treatment Task: I am a 63 year old male and have had a cough for over 3 months. At first I thought it was a cold, but it can't last that long! I have been suffering from high blood pressure and coronary heart disease for a long time. I have also had 2 stents in this context. I take Ramipril, Atorvastatin and ASS as medication. Otherwise I am symptom-free. My doctor has diagnosed me with bronchial asthma . What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: bronchial asthma 
 
  Model Output:Switching from Ramipril to an ARB (Angiotensin II Receptor Blocker)
 Why?
 Ramipril (ACE inhibitor) is a common cause of chronic cough due to bradykinin accumulation.
 Since you have hypertension and coronary artery disease, an ARB (e.g., losartan, valsartan, candesartan) would provide similar cardiovascular benefits without causing cough.
 2. Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS) + Long-Acting Beta-Agonists (LABA)
 Why?
 First-line therapy for asthma, especially in older adults with cardiovascular disease.
 Options:
 ICS alone (if mild asthma):
 Budesonide (Pulmicort)
 Fluticasone (Flovent)
 ICS + LABA (for more persistent symptoms)
 Budesonide + Formoterol (Symbicort)
 Fluticasone + Salmeterol (Advair)
 Note: ICS reduces inflammation, while LABA helps open the airways.
 3. Short-Acting Beta-Agonists (SABA) as Rescue Therapy
 Why?
 Provides quick relief for sudden coughing or breathlessness.
 Option:
 Salbutamol (Ventolin) inhaler, used as needed.
 4. Leukotriene Receptor Antagonists (LTRA)
 Why?
 Can be useful for cough-variant asthma and is an alternative for patients who cannot tolerate ICS.
 Option:
 Montelukast (Singulair), taken once daily.
 5. Pulmonary and Cardiac Monitoring
 Why?
 Since you have a history of coronary artery disease, it's crucial to monitor for any worsening heart symptoms.
 Some asthma medications (like high-dose beta-agonists) can increase heart rate and should be used with caution.
 Follow-ups:
 Spirometry or FeNO test to confirm asthma diagnosis.
 Echocardiogram or cardiac workup if there are any new symptoms like chest pain or shortness of breath.

Treatment Task: My husband is 78 years old and since last night he can no longer lift his arm properly and he can no longer walk. My son and I take care of him (care level 2). 3 months ago he was in the hospital because of bleeding with prostate cancer. A certain therapy was discontinued at that time. My doctor has diagnosed me with Stroke. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Stroke
 
  Model Output:Acute Stroke Treatment (if within the time window for intervention)
 
 For Ischemic Stroke: Thrombolysis (alteplase/tPA) if within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, or mechanical thrombectomy if a large vessel occlusion is present and within 24 hours.
 For Hemorrhagic Stroke: Blood pressure management, reversal of anticoagulation (if applicable), and neurosurgical evaluation if needed.
 Stroke Unit Monitoring & Secondary Prevention
 
 Hospital admission for neurological monitoring, blood pressure control, and prevention of complications (e.g., aspiration pneumonia, deep vein thrombosis).
 Medications: Antiplatelets (e.g., aspirin, clopidogrel) for ischemic stroke, statins for cholesterol control, and anticoagulation if atrial fibrillation is present.
 Rehabilitation & Physiotherapy
 
 Early mobilization, physical therapy, and gait training to prevent complications such as muscle atrophy and joint contractures.
 Occupational therapy for arm movement and coordination improvement.
 Speech and Swallowing Therapy (if needed)
 
 If speech or swallowing is affected, a speech therapist can help with communication and dietary modifications to prevent aspiration pneumonia.
 Long-Term Stroke Care & Supportive Measures
 
 Home care adjustments (wheelchair, walker, adapted bathroom) to improve mobility.
 Supportive care for prostate cancer history, as previous bleeding events could influence further stroke prevention (e.g., anticoagulant decisions).

Treatment Task: I am a 68 year old man and I always have such a twinge in my chest. It is a feeling of pressure in the chest that has been there for 6 weeks. Especially when I exert myself, it stings in there under the breastbone. I am slightly overweight, but otherwise healthy. My doctor has diagnosed me with Coronary artery disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Coronary artery disease
 
  Model Output:Anti-anginal medication (symptom relief) – Nitroglycerin (sublingual or spray) can be used to relieve chest pain episodes. Beta-blockers (e.g., metoprolol, bisoprolol) or calcium channel blockers (e.g., amlodipine, diltiazem) can help reduce the heart’s oxygen demand and prevent angina.
 
 Antiplatelet therapy (prevent clot formation) – Aspirin (low-dose, e.g., 75–100 mg daily) is essential to reduce the risk of heart attack. If high risk, clopidogrel may be added.
 
 Cholesterol-lowering therapy (atherosclerosis management) – Statins (e.g., atorvastatin, rosuvastatin) are crucial to lower LDL cholesterol and reduce plaque buildup in the coronary arteries.
 
 Lifestyle modifications (long-term prevention) – Weight management, regular exercise, smoking cessation (if applicable), blood pressure control, and a Mediterranean diet (rich in vegetables, healthy fats, and lean proteins) significantly improve CAD outcomes.
 
 Coronary intervention (if symptoms worsen or high risk) – If symptoms persist despite medication, or if a stress test or angiography shows significant blockages, coronary angioplasty with stent placement or bypass surgery may be necessary.

Treatment Task: Our neighbor is an avid motorcyclist and was stung by a wasp under his leather suit on his way home today. He has quaddle-shaped skin rashes all over his body. My doctor has diagnosed me with Anaphylaxis/Anaphylactic shock. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Anaphylaxis/Anaphylactic shock
 
  Model Output:ntramuscular Epinephrine (Adrenaline, 0.3–0.5 mg IM) – First-line emergency treatment to counteract anaphylaxis by reducing airway swelling, increasing blood pressure, and stabilizing the cardiovascular system.
 
 Airway Management (Oxygen, Intubation if Needed) – If breathing difficulties or airway swelling occur, oxygen therapy or mechanical ventilation may be required.
 
 Intravenous Fluids (Ringer’s Lactate or Normal Saline) – Rapid IV fluid administration is crucial to counteract hypotension and maintain circulation.
 
 Antihistamines (e.g., Clemastine or Diphenhydramine, IV/Oral) – Helps reduce hives (urticaria), itching, and swelling but should only be used as a secondary measure after epinephrine.
 
 Corticosteroids (e.g., Methylprednisolone IV/Oral) – Prevents delayed or biphasic reactions but has a slower onset of action, making it a supportive rather than primary treatment.

Treatment Task: I am a 28 years old woman and I came back from Thailand 5 days ago. There I celebrated the graduation with some girlfriends. Shortly before leaving, I noticed a greenish discharge, but I ignored it for now. However, in the last few days I have been feeling increasingly worse. I have an elevated temperature of 38 degrees, my entire lower abdomen hurts and I have pain when urinating. My preliminary examinations have so far always been unremarkable. I use contraception with the pill. I met a man in Thailand and had unprotected sex with him after a party. I don't know anything about him except that his name is Pablo and he is from Mexico. I read on the Internet that a fresh HIV infection could also be accompanied by fever and exhaustion. My doctor has diagnosed me with Gonorrhea. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Gonorrhea
 
  Model Output:1. First-Line Therapy (Recommended by WHO & CDC)
 Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM (intramuscular injection) in a single dose
 PLUS Azithromycin 1 g orally in a single dose (if Chlamydia is not ruled out)
 This combination is recommended to cover both Gonorrhea and potential Chlamydia coinfection.
 2. Alternative for Ceftriaxone Allergy
 Gentamicin 240 mg IM in a single dose
 PLUS Azithromycin 2 g orally in a single dose
 This is used if you are allergic to cephalosporins.
 3. Dual Therapy with Oral Option (Less Preferred)
 Cefixime 800 mg orally in a single dose
 PLUS Azithromycin 1 g orally in a single dose
 Cefixime is an oral alternative but is less effective than intramuscular Ceftriaxone.
 4. If Mycoplasma genitalium Coinfection is Suspected
 Moxifloxacin 400 mg orally once daily for 7-10 days
 This is used if persistent symptoms remain after standard gonorrhea treatment and Mycoplasma genitalium is suspected.
 5. Post-Treatment Testing & HIV Prophylaxis Consideration
 Test for Chlamydia, Syphilis, HIV, Hepatitis B & C
 Repeat STI testing in 3 months to check for reinfection
 If high HIV risk (e.g., exposure from a partner of unknown status within the last 72 hours), Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) should be discussed.

Treatment Task: I am a 34 year old woman expecting my first child. I am in the 36+2 SSW. Since yesterday I have been experiencing increasing upper abdominal pain, mainly localized in the right upper abdomen. I also have mild nausea. My husband must have also eaten something bad 2 days ago and is also feeling bad. I am still worried about the child. It is an absolutely desired child. The pregnancy has gone without complications so far. My doctor has diagnosed me with HELLP syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: HELLP syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. Immediate Delivery (Definitive Treatment)
 Since you are beyond 34 weeks of gestation, delivery is the only definitive treatment.
 Vaginal delivery may be attempted if conditions allow, but C-section is often necessary in cases of worsening maternal or fetal conditions.
 2. Corticosteroids for Fetal Lung Maturity (If Needed)
 If delivery is slightly delayed (e.g., for stabilization), betamethasone or dexamethasone may be given to enhance fetal lung maturity.
 3. Magnesium Sulfate for Seizure Prophylaxis
 To prevent eclampsia (seizures), magnesium sulfate is administered intravenously.
 4. Blood Pressure Management (If Hypertensive)
 Labetalol, Hydralazine, or Nifedipine may be used if severe hypertension (>160/110 mmHg) is present.
 5. Supportive Care (Fluids, Blood Products, Monitoring)
 IV fluids for hydration (carefully managed to avoid fluid overload).
 Blood products (platelets, plasma) if severe thrombocytopenia or coagulopathy occurs.
 Close maternal and fetal monitoring in an intensive care or high-risk obstetric unit.

Treatment Task: I am a 32 year old woman and have had cycle related lower abdominal pain for over 15 years. In the beginning, the pain was not that severe and I believed it was normal during my period. However, for about 7 years the pain has been almost unbearable. In addition, for about 6 months I have also noticed rectal bleeding during my period. Pain during urination or bowel movements is something I have had almost all the time. I have been treated several times, changed my gynecologist, but no one has been able to help me. They always tell me that I have too much stress. In the meantime, the complaints are also putting a strain on my relationship. Sexual intercourse is extremely unpleasant and our desire to have a child has been unfulfilled for over three years. My doctor has diagnosed me with Endometriosis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Endometriosis
 
  Model Output:Hormonal Therapy
 
 Combined oral contraceptives (COCs) – Can help regulate the cycle and reduce pain.
 Progestins (e.g., dienogest, norethisterone, or medroxyprogesterone acetate) – Reduce estrogen stimulation of endometrial implants.
 GnRH agonists (e.g., leuprorelin, goserelin) – Temporarily induce menopause to shrink endometriotic tissue.
 GnRH antagonists (e.g., elagolix, relugolix) – A newer option that suppresses endometriosis activity with fewer side effects than GnRH agonists.
 Laparoscopic Surgery (Diagnostic and Therapeutic)
 
 Laparoscopic excision or ablation of endometriotic lesions – Can reduce pain and improve fertility.
 Removal of deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) lesions – Especially necessary if bowel or bladder involvement is present (due to rectal bleeding and painful urination).
 Pain Management & Symptom Relief
 
 Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs, e.g., ibuprofen, naproxen) – To help manage pain and inflammation.
 Neuromodulators (e.g., amitriptyline, gabapentin, pregabalin) – For chronic pain management if necessary.
 Fertility Treatment (If Pregnancy Is Desired)
 
 Assisted reproductive technology (ART) – In vitro fertilization (IVF) may be recommended if conception does not occur naturally after surgery.
 Ovulation stimulation (e.g., letrozole, clomiphene citrate) – If fertility is affected but surgery is not an option.
 Multidisciplinary Support & Lifestyle Modifications
 
 Pelvic physiotherapy – Helps with muscle tension and pain relief.
 Psychological support or counseling – Chronic pain and infertility can be emotionally challenging.
 Anti-inflammatory diet & stress management – May help reduce flare-ups and pain severity.

Treatment Task: I am a 42 year old woman and for the past few months I have noticed light vaginal bleeding after sexual intercourse and at times pain. This has never happened before. I also have a watery, brownish discharge that kind of irritates me. I had an abortion when I was 18. At that time I was not ready for a steady partnership, let alone family. I partied a lot back then, sometimes took drugs and smoked. In the meantime, I lead a very bourgeois life. I am married, have a daughter and a good job. I work a lot and therefore have not been to the gynecologist for 5 years. I have never had such complaints. Otherwise I have always been healthy and my cycle is regular. My doctor has diagnosed me with Cervical carcinoma. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cervical carcinoma
 
  Model Output:Surgical treatment (if early-stage disease) – If the cancer is detected at an early stage (IA1-IB1), surgical options such as conization (for very early lesions), simple or radical hysterectomy (removal of the uterus), and lymph node dissection may be considered. If fertility preservation is desired, a radical trachelectomy (removal of the cervix but keeping the uterus) might be an option.
 
 Radiotherapy (if locally advanced disease) – In more advanced cases (IB2-IVA), external beam radiotherapy combined with brachytherapy (internal radiation) is a standard treatment approach.
 
 Chemoradiotherapy (if advanced or high-risk) – If the cancer is beyond an early stage, a combination of radiotherapy and chemotherapy (cisplatin-based regimens) is often used to improve survival and reduce recurrence risk.
 
 Targeted therapy or immunotherapy (for metastatic or recurrent disease) – In cases of metastatic or recurrent cervical cancer, Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF therapy) or immunotherapy (e.g., pembrolizumab for PD-L1 positive tumors) may be beneficial.
 
 Regular follow-up and HPV vaccination for your daughter (prevention for the next generation) – Even after treatment, regular gynecological follow-ups with Pap smears and HPV testing are crucial to detect recurrences early. Since high-risk HPV is a leading cause of cervical cancer, vaccination for your daughter could help prevent future disease.

Treatment Task: I am a yoga instructor and a participant just felt an extreme lower abdominal pain after an exercise and could not get up. She says she is actually healthy, just that her last period missed. She has a lot of stress, her boyfriend broke up with her 4 months ago and I guess that still bothers her a lot. She could not be pregnant because she has not had sexual intercourse for several months. She has never experienced this kind of pain before. My doctor has diagnosed me with Adnexal torsion / ovarian torsion. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Adnexal torsion / ovarian torsion
 
  Model Output:Emergency Laparoscopy (Minimally Invasive Surgery) – First-line treatment to untwist the ovary and preserve its function; should be performed as soon as possible to prevent tissue necrosis.
 
 Oophoropexy (Ovary Fixation Surgery, if Recurrent) – In some cases, the ovary may be surgically fixed to prevent future torsion, especially in patients with predisposing factors like ovarian cysts.
 
 Pain Management (IV Analgesics, NSAIDs, or Opioids if Severe) – Needed to control extreme pain before or after surgery, but definitive treatment is surgical.
 
 Salpingo-Oophorectomy (Ovary Removal, if Necrosis Present) – If blood flow is not restored in time and the ovary is non-viable, it may need to be surgically removed.
 
 Hormonal Therapy (If Underlying Cystic or Hormonal Condition Identified) – If ovarian cysts or other hormone-related factors contributed to torsion, birth control pills or hormonal therapy may help prevent recurrence.

Treatment Task: I am a 52 year old woman, actually athletic, slim and fit. But I haven't been able to sleep properly for months. I wake up all the time. In addition, my cycle is completely unpredictable. Sometimes the period stays away for several months and then it suddenly comes three times in quick succession and so strongly, as I don't know it at all. In addition, I am often depressed, quickly irritable and sweat very often. I have always been healthy, do not smoke and drink very little alcohol. My doctor has diagnosed me with Menopause. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Menopause
 
  Model Output:Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT, if no contraindications) – Estrogen or combined estrogen-progestin therapy can help alleviate symptoms like sleep disturbances, mood swings, hot flashes, and irregular cycles.
 
 Non-Hormonal Treatment for Hot Flashes – Alternatives like SSRIs (e.g., paroxetine), SNRIs (e.g., venlafaxine), gabapentin, or clonidine can reduce hot flashes and improve mood if HRT is not an option.
 
 Lifestyle Modifications – Regular exercise, stress management (e.g., yoga, meditation), and a healthy diet rich in calcium and vitamin D can help maintain overall well-being and prevent osteoporosis.
 
 Sleep Optimization – Melatonin supplements, good sleep hygiene practices, or herbal remedies like valerian root can improve sleep quality and reduce night awakenings.
 
 Psychological Support and Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) – If depression and irritability are significant, CBT or counseling can be beneficial, along with mindfulness-based stress reduction techniques.

Treatment Task: I am a nurse and for the last 4 days I have been having severe pain at the vaginal entrance and on the left side I also feel a hardening that has become much bigger in the last few days. I went to my gynecologist yesterday but her ointment hardly helps. Today the pain is so bad that I can barely sit. I have never had gynecological problems before. My cycle is regular and I haven't had a change of partners either. My doctor has diagnosed me with Bartholin's abscess / bartholinitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bartholin's abscess / bartholinitis
 
  Model Output:Incision and Drainage (I&D) – Preferred Treatment for Large Abscesses
 
 A minor surgical procedure to relieve pain and remove infected fluid.
 Performed under local anesthesia in a doctor’s office or hospital.
 Word Catheter Placement (After Drainage) – To Prevent Recurrence
 
 A small tube is inserted into the gland to allow continuous drainage for 4-6 weeks.
 Helps prevent the abscess from re-forming.
 Antibiotic Therapy (If Severe Infection or Signs of Systemic Illness)
 
 Usually not required for small abscesses, but needed if:
 Fever, extensive cellulitis, or recurrent infections.
 Common antibiotics: Amoxicillin-Clavulanate, Clindamycin, or Doxycycline (depending on culture results).
 Pain Relief and Anti-Inflammatory Treatment
 
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Diclofenac) to reduce pain and swelling.
 Warm sitz baths (3-4 times daily) to promote drainage and comfort.
 Marsupialization (Surgical Gland Opening) – For Recurrent Abscesses
 
 If you experience repeated Bartholin’s abscesses, a long-term surgical solution may be needed.
 Prevents future blockages while preserving gland function.

Treatment Task: I am a 20 year old female and have been having pain throughout my lower abdomen since yesterday, which initially got better but worsened significantly tonight. I have a fever up to 39.5 degrees. I do not have nausea or vomiting. Bowel movements are regular and firm. I am otherwise healthy and my last period was a week ago. My last gynecological checkup 6 months ago was unremarkable. My doctor has diagnosed me with Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)
 
  Model Output:Broad-Spectrum Antibiotic Therapy (Empiric Treatment)
 
 Outpatient:
 Ceftriaxone (500 mg IM, single dose) + Doxycycline (100 mg orally, twice daily for 14 days) + Metronidazole (500 mg orally, twice daily for 14 days) (for anaerobic coverage).
 Inpatient (if severe symptoms, high fever, or worsening condition):
 IV Ceftriaxone + IV Doxycycline + IV Metronidazole, followed by oral antibiotics after improvement.
 Pain Management & Anti-Inflammatory Treatment
 
 Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs, e.g., ibuprofen, naproxen) to reduce pain and inflammation.
 Bed Rest & Hydration
 
 Avoid strenuous activity, drink plenty of fluids, and ensure adequate rest to support recovery.
 Partner Treatment & Sexual Abstinence Until Recovery
 
 Your sexual partner(s) should be tested and treated for Chlamydia and Gonorrhea, even if asymptomatic, to prevent reinfection.
 Avoid sexual intercourse until treatment is completed and symptoms resolve.
 Follow-Up & Monitoring for Complications
 
 A follow-up exam in 48–72 hours is crucial to ensure improvement.
 Monitor for abscess formation, chronic pelvic pain, or infertility risks. If symptoms worsen, hospital admission may be required.

Treatment Task: I am a 28 year old female who has had severe right sided lower abdominal pain since this afternoon. No nausea, no vomiting, no fever. I have no previous illness and no appendix anymore. I am doubled over in pain though. My doctor has diagnosed me with Ectopic pregnancy. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Ectopic pregnancy
 
  Model Output:Surgical treatment (if ruptured or unstable) – If the ectopic pregnancy is ruptured or causing severe pain and internal bleeding, emergency laparoscopy or laparotomy is needed to remove the ectopic pregnancy. Salpingectomy (removal of the affected fallopian tube) or salpingostomy (preserving the tube if possible) may be performed.
 
 Methotrexate therapy (if unruptured and stable) – In stable patients with early-stage ectopic pregnancy, a single or multiple-dose regimen of methotrexate (a folate antagonist that stops cell growth) can be used to dissolve the pregnancy without surgery. This is an option only if hCG levels are low and there is no rupture risk.
 
 Expectant management (watchful waiting in very selected cases) – If the ectopic pregnancy is very small, hCG levels are low and declining, and there are no symptoms of rupture, close monitoring with serial hCG measurements and ultrasounds may be an option.
 
 Pain management and supportive care – Acetaminophen (paracetamol) or mild opioids may be given for pain relief, but NSAIDs should be avoided if methotrexate is used, as they can interfere with its function. Intravenous fluids may be needed if there is blood loss.
 
 Psychological support and future fertility counseling – Since an ectopic pregnancy can impact fertility, a follow-up with a gynecologist or fertility specialist is recommended to assess fallopian tube health and discuss future pregnancy planning. Early ultrasound in future pregnancies is essential to rule out recurrence.

Treatment Task: My newborn, Chris (10 days old), is increasingly tired, doesn't want to drink, and keeps vomiting. We had a normal pregnancy. The birth weight was 3490g. Everything went smoothly and so we were discharged after 3 days. We did not do a newborn screening because the examination for congenital hormonal and metabolic disorders in the children from the first marriage in each case had been unremarkable. My doctor has diagnosed me with Congenital adrenal hyperplasia. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia
 
  Model Output:Hydrocortisone (Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy, IV or Oral) – Lifesaving treatment to replace deficient cortisol and stabilize metabolism, blood pressure, and energy levels.
 
 Fludrocortisone (Mineralocorticoid Replacement Therapy) – Needed in salt-wasting forms of congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) to maintain sodium balance and prevent dehydration and hypotension.
 
 Intravenous Fluids and Electrolyte Management (0.9% Saline + Dextrose) – Essential for treating dehydration, correcting hyponatremia (low sodium), and preventing hypoglycemia.
 
 Newborn Screening and Genetic Testing (Confirmatory Diagnosis) – Although clinical symptoms are suggestive, biochemical tests (17-hydroxyprogesterone levels) and genetic testing confirm the diagnosis and subtype.
 
 Parental Education and Emergency Steroid Stress Dosing – Parents should be trained in emergency hydrocortisone injections for times of illness, stress, or adrenal crisis to prevent life-threatening complications.

Treatment Task: My 2 year old son suffers from infections very frequently. I first noticed poor wound healing when he was 8 months old. An abrasion quickly became suppurative, which was treated with antibiotics as an inpatient. Since then, our son has received antibiotic therapy four times for ear infections. A week ago, he was discharged after a 10-day inpatient stay with pneumonia and already has another cold. He also has a fever of 38.3. This is not normal. My doctor has diagnosed me with Agammaglobulinemia. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Agammaglobulinemia
 
  Model Output:Immunoglobulin Replacement Therapy (IVIG or SCIG) – Regular intravenous (IVIG) or subcutaneous (SCIG) immunoglobulin therapy to provide missing antibodies and reduce the frequency and severity of infections.
 
 Prompt and Aggressive Antibiotic Treatment for Infections – Early broad-spectrum antibiotics should be administered at the first sign of infection to prevent severe complications like pneumonia or sepsis.
 
 Routine Monitoring and Specialist Care (Immunologist/Pediatrician) – Regular immune function assessments, IgG level monitoring, and lung function evaluations to manage disease progression and complications.
 
 Preventive Measures (Vaccinations and Hygiene Practices) – While live vaccines are contraindicated, killed/inactivated vaccines may be beneficial in some cases; maintaining good hygiene and avoiding sick contacts is crucial.
 
 Screening for Complications (Lung Damage, Autoimmune Disorders) – Long-term monitoring for bronchiectasis, chronic lung disease, or autoimmune conditions, which are common in primary immunodeficiencies.

Treatment Task: I am a mother of a 7 year old son. I have been annoyed for three days that toothpaste always drips out of his mouth onto his shirt when he brushes his teeth. Today I also noticed that his facial expressions on the right side of his face are almost absent and his right eye is watery. He is otherwise calm but quite tired. My doctor has diagnosed me with Lyme Neuroborreliosis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Lyme Neuroborreliosis
 
  Model Output:Intravenous or Oral Antibiotic Therapy (Depending on Severity)
 
 IV Ceftriaxone (preferred for severe cases) – 14–21 days.
 Oral Doxycycline (if mild and no CNS involvement) – 14–21 days.
 Alternative: Amoxicillin for younger children if doxycycline is contraindicated.
 Eye Protection for Facial Palsy (Prevent Corneal Damage)
 
 Artificial tears or ophthalmic ointment to prevent dry eye.
 Eye patch or tape at night if the eye cannot fully close.
 Corticosteroids (Limited Use and Controversial)
 
 Not typically recommended for Lyme-associated facial palsy, but may be used in severe cases.
 Physical Therapy & Facial Exercises
 
 Promotes recovery of facial nerve function and prevents muscle atrophy.
 Supportive Care (Rest, Fluids, and Symptom Management)
 
 Manage fatigue with proper rest and hydration.
 Pain relief with NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen) if needed.

Treatment Task: My son is 15 months old. For 2 days he has had a cold, sore throat and fever up to a maximum of 40 degrees. Due to a positive throat swab for group A streptococcus the day before at the pediatrician, we are now giving him an antibiotic treatment with penicillin. Today there was a situation in which Marcel was very quiet, he no longer reacted properly to speech, his eyes were often turned upwards and he twitched at all extremities. This condition lasted for about 60 seconds. After that, he fell asleep. There are no allergies, pre-existing conditions or permanent medication. He was also vaccinated according to STIKO recommendations. My doctor has diagnosed me with Febrile seizure. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Febrile seizure
 
  Model Output:Supportive Care During a Seizure (First Aid Measures)
 
 Place him on his side in a safe position to prevent choking.
 Do not put anything in his mouth.
 Monitor seizure duration and breathing.
 Antipyretic Therapy for Fever Management
 
 Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) or Ibuprofen to reduce fever and improve comfort, though this does not prevent future febrile seizures.
 Completion of Antibiotic Therapy (Penicillin for Streptococcal Infection)
 
 Ensure full adherence to the prescribed penicillin course to treat group A streptococcal infection.
 Observation and Follow-Up with Pediatrician
 
 Monitor for recurrence: Most febrile seizures are benign, but a follow-up visit is recommended.
 Seek emergency care if seizures last longer than 5 minutes, occur repeatedly, or if the child has difficulty recovering.
 Education and Emergency Seizure Medication (if Recurrent or Complex Seizures)
 
 If febrile seizures become recurrent or prolonged, a doctor may prescribe a rescue medication (e.g., diazepam rectal gel or midazolam buccal spray) to stop future prolonged seizures.

Treatment Task: My 14 year old daughter Mira has been complaining of abdominal pain for the past 6 months, mainly localized in the right lower abdomen. They occur especially after eating and before she goes to the toilet. This is also the reason why Mira is eating less and less and has lost 4kg of weight in the last months. Partly she also has mushy and mucous diarrhea. She also seems very weak. My doctor has diagnosed me with Crohn's disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Crohn's disease
 
  Model Output:Induction therapy with corticosteroids (to control inflammation quickly) – For moderate to severe Crohn’s disease, Mira may need a short course of corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone or budesonide) to reduce acute inflammation and symptoms. Budesonide is preferred for milder ileocecal disease due to fewer side effects.
 
 Immunosuppressive therapy (to maintain remission and prevent relapses) – Since Crohn’s disease is chronic, azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP), or methotrexate may be used to maintain remission and avoid long-term steroid use.
 
 Biologic therapy (if moderate to severe disease) – If symptoms are severe or do not respond to conventional therapy, TNF-alpha inhibitors (infliximab, adalimumab) or integrin inhibitors (vedolizumab) can be used to control inflammation and prevent complications.
 
 Nutritional therapy (to support weight gain and reduce inflammation) – Since Mira has lost weight and is eating less, a high-calorie, anti-inflammatory diet is important. In children and adolescents, exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) with a liquid formula diet may be used to induce remission without steroids.
 
 Psychosocial support and lifestyle management – Since Crohn’s disease affects growth and quality of life, regular follow-ups with a pediatric gastroenterologist, psychological support, and stress management are crucial for Mira’s well-being.

Treatment Task: My 10 month old infant may have a failure to thrive. He has barely gained weight since 6 months of age and weight has slipped from the 25th to the 3rd percentile. Stools are bulky, greasy, and smelly. At 8 months of age, Tim has gone through a persistent upper respiratory infection with whistling breath sounds. He also tastes strangely salty when kissed. My doctor has diagnosed me with Cystic fibrosis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cystic fibrosis
 
  Model Output:Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy (PERT, e.g., Creon) – Helps digest fats and proteins, improving nutrient absorption and weight gain.
 
 Nutritional Support (High-Calorie Diet + Fat-Soluble Vitamins A, D, E, K) – Ensures adequate growth and compensates for malabsorption.
 
 Airway Clearance Therapy (Chest Physiotherapy, Postural Drainage, or Oscillatory Devices) – Helps clear thick mucus from the lungs, reducing the risk of infections.
 
 Inhaled Medications (e.g., Saline Nebulization, Dornase Alfa, or Bronchodilators) – Loosens mucus, improves lung function, and prevents respiratory complications.
 
 CFTR Modulator Therapy (if Eligible, e.g., Ivacaftor or Combination Therapy) – Addresses the underlying defect in CFTR protein function, improving lung and digestive health (specific to mutation type).

disease_GT: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: My daughter, Zoe, is 5 years old and has been floppy and tired for four weeks. Since last week she has been complaining of abdominal pain, has to urinate frequently and threw up twice yesterday. She had a febrile upper respiratory infection 8 weeks ago. Zoe's appetite remains good. However, she has lost 2 kg in weight. She drinks very well, up to 4 liters a day and always asks for iced tea and lemonade. So far, Zoe has always been healthy. My doctor has diagnosed me with Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
 
  Model Output:Insulin Therapy – Lifelong insulin treatment (basal-bolus regimen or insulin pump therapy) is necessary to maintain blood glucose control and prevent ketoacidosis.
 
 Blood Glucose Monitoring – Frequent blood sugar checks using a glucometer or continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) system to adjust insulin doses and prevent hypoglycemia/hyperglycemia.
 
 Dietary and Fluid Management – A balanced diet with controlled carbohydrate intake, avoiding excessive sugary drinks, and maintaining adequate hydration to prevent complications.
 
 Education for Parents and Zoe – Diabetes education on insulin administration, recognizing hypoglycemia/hyperglycemia, and emergency management (e.g., diabetic ketoacidosis symptoms) for both parents and the child.
 
 Regular Pediatric Endocrinology Follow-Ups – Routine check-ups with a pediatric endocrinologist to monitor growth, development, HbA1c levels, and prevent long-term complications (e.g., kidney, eye, and nerve damage).

Treatment Task: My 4 year old boy has had an increasingly waddling and unsteady gait since age 2. He also has difficulty standing up. The symptoms have continuously worsened. Otherwise Peter is a healthy, agile boy. He has been walking freely since the age of 17 months. But now I always have to think of my great-uncle, who died of muscle weakness at the age of 20. My doctor has diagnosed me with Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD)
 
  Model Output:Corticosteroid Therapy (Slows Disease Progression)
 
 Prednisone or Deflazacort – Helps preserve muscle strength and delay loss of ambulation.
 Monitor for side effects (weight gain, bone health, behavioral issues).
 Physical Therapy & Stretching (Prevents Contractures & Improves Mobility)
 
 Daily stretching, bracing, and mobility exercises.
 Use of orthotics (e.g., ankle-foot orthoses) to support walking.
 Cardiac & Respiratory Monitoring (Early Intervention)
 
 Regular echocardiograms & ECGs to monitor for dilated cardiomyopathy.
 Pulmonary function tests & possible nighttime ventilation support in later stages.
 Emerging Genetic & Molecular Therapies (For Suitable Cases)
 
 Exon-skipping drugs (e.g., Eteplirsen, Golodirsen) for specific gene mutations.
 Gene therapy trials are promising but not yet widely available.
 Multidisciplinary Care (Neurology, Cardiology, Physiotherapy, Nutrition)
 
 A coordinated care team helps manage complications and quality of life.
 Nutritional support to prevent obesity & vitamin D for bone health.

Treatment Task: My 13 year old daughter is gaining an inordinate amount of weight and this is with a growth arrest. She has also been in a depressive phase for about 6 weeks. Our otherwise cheerful daughter is withdrawing more and more, reads a lot and has few social contacts. My doctor has diagnosed me with Cushing's disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cushing's disease
 
  Model Output:Surgical Removal of the Pituitary Tumor (First-Line Treatment)
 
 Transsphenoidal surgery to remove the ACTH-producing pituitary adenoma, which is the most effective treatment in confirmed cases of Cushing’s disease.
 Medications to Reduce Cortisol Production (If Surgery Is Not Possible or Delayed)
 
 Ketoconazole, Metyrapone, or Osilodrostat – Inhibit cortisol synthesis.
 Pasireotide (Somatostatin Analog) – Reduces ACTH secretion from the pituitary tumor.
 Radiation Therapy (If Surgery Is Incomplete or Ineffective)
 
 Stereotactic radiotherapy (Gamma Knife or Proton Therapy) may be used if the tumor cannot be fully removed or recurs.
 Hormone Replacement Therapy (If Necessary Post-Surgery)
 
 After surgery, temporary or permanent adrenal insufficiency can occur, requiring hydrocortisone replacement therapy until normal adrenal function recovers.
 Psychological Support & Lifestyle Management
 
 Psychotherapy or counseling for depression, social withdrawal, and emotional well-being.
 Nutritional counseling & exercise therapy to manage weight gain and metabolic effects.

Treatment Task: I am a 23 year old woman and have severe abdominal pain. At times they are also crampy. They started the night before after a pizza with mild nausea in the upper abdomen and shifted to the lower right abdominal region during the day. I vomited once in the morning. I feel listless and have no appetite at all. My bowel movements and urine are unremarkable. I can't think of any important previous illnesses. My doctor has diagnosed me with Acute appendicitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute appendicitis
 
  Model Output:Appendectomy (standard treatment) – Surgical removal of the appendix (laparoscopic appendectomy) is the gold standard for treating acute appendicitis and prevents complications like perforation or abscess formation. Open appendectomy may be necessary if complications arise.
 
 Preoperative and postoperative antibiotics – IV antibiotics (e.g., cefuroxime + metronidazole or piperacillin/tazobactam) are given before surgery to reduce infection risk. If the appendix has ruptured, longer antibiotic therapy may be needed postoperatively.
 
 Pain management (avoid NSAIDs pre-surgery!) – Acetaminophen (paracetamol) or mild opioids can help with pain relief. NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) should be avoided before surgery due to the risk of increased bleeding.
 
 Fluid and electrolyte replacement – Since nausea and vomiting can cause dehydration, IV fluids (Ringer’s lactate or saline) help maintain hydration and support recovery.
 
 Non-surgical management (if mild and uncomplicated case) – In some cases of uncomplicated appendicitis, a course of antibiotics alone may be considered instead of immediate surgery, but this has a higher risk of recurrence.

Treatment Task: I am a middle-aged man (42) and have a swelling in the left groin area. It appeared after a violent coughing attack and is painful. I noticed a small bump on the groin some time ago. But it did not hurt yet. I am a smoker (30 cigarettes/day) and therefore have a chronic smoker's cough. Otherwise I have nothing. Two years ago I had a meniscus operation on my left knee. My doctor has diagnosed me with Inguinal hernia (indirect/direct?). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Inguinal hernia (indirect/direct?)
 
  Model Output:Surgical Repair (Herniorrhaphy or Hernioplasty – Open or Laparoscopic Surgery) – The definitive treatment to prevent complications such as incarceration or strangulation, particularly since the hernia is painful and enlarging.
 
 Lifestyle Modifications (Smoking Cessation, Weight Management if Needed) – Reducing chronic coughing from smoking may help prevent hernia recurrence post-surgery.
 
 Avoid Heavy Lifting and Straining – Until surgery is performed, minimizing activities that increase intra-abdominal pressure can help prevent worsening of the hernia.
 
 Supportive Measures (Hernia Truss or Compression Garment, if Surgery is Delayed) – A temporary option in select cases, but not a definitive treatment, as it does not prevent complications.
 
 Pain Management (NSAIDs or Acetaminophen as Needed) – Helps relieve discomfort, but should not replace surgical intervention, as untreated hernias can lead to life-threatening complications.

Treatment Task: I am a 59-year-old woman and have had increasing pain in my left lower abdomen for 2 days. Since then I feel weak, have slight diarrhea and no appetite. Today I also have a fever. About six months ago I had similar complaints, but not in this intensity. My urine is normal. 12 years ago I had a hysterectomy (because of uterine fibroids) and three years ago my gallbladder was removed because of gallstones. My doctor has diagnosed me with Diverticulitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Diverticulitis
 
  Model Output:Antibiotic Therapy (if indicated for complicated or moderate cases) – Oral antibiotics like ciprofloxacin + metronidazole or amoxicillin/clavulanic acid may be prescribed for moderate cases, while severe cases may require intravenous antibiotics in a hospital setting.
 
 Bowel Rest and Dietary Adjustments – Initially, a low-fiber or liquid diet can help reduce bowel strain during an acute flare, followed by a gradual transition to a high-fiber diet after recovery to prevent future episodes.
 
 Pain Management – Acetaminophen (paracetamol) is preferred for pain relief, as NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) may increase the risk of complications like perforation.
 
 Monitoring for Complications – Watch for worsening fever, increasing pain, peritoneal signs, or signs of abscess or perforation, which may require hospital admission and possible surgical intervention.
 
 Long-Term Prevention Strategies – After recovery, lifestyle modifications such as high-fiber nutrition, regular exercise, adequate hydration, and avoiding constipation can help reduce the risk of recurrent diverticulitis.

Treatment Task: I am a 24 year old woman and have been having lower abdominal pain (especially on the right) and increasing diarrhea for a few days. At times there is also slight nausea and twice I had to vomit. I have already had my appendix removed as a child. I have no complaints/pre-existing conditions apart from lactose intolerance. My doctor has diagnosed me with Crohn's Disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Crohn's Disease
 
  Model Output:1. Anti-Inflammatory Medications (First-Line)
 Corticosteroids (e.g., Prednisolone) – Used for short-term relief during acute flares.
 Aminosalicylates (5-ASA, e.g., Mesalazine) – Less effective for Crohn’s but sometimes used for mild cases.
 Goal: Reduce inflammation and control acute symptoms.
 2. Immunosuppressants and Biologic Therapy (For Moderate to Severe Cases)
 Immunosuppressants (e.g., Azathioprine, Methotrexate) – Help prevent future flare-ups.
 Biologics (e.g., Infliximab, Adalimumab) – Target specific inflammatory pathways (TNF inhibitors).
 Used when standard treatments are not effective or in moderate-to-severe disease.
 3. Dietary and Nutritional Therapy
 Avoid trigger foods (e.g., high-fiber foods, dairy due to lactose intolerance).
 Low-residue diet during flares to reduce intestinal irritation.
 Nutritional supplements (B12, iron, vitamin D) to manage malabsorption issues.
 4. Symptom Control and Supportive Therapy
 Antidiarrheal medications (e.g., Loperamide) – Used cautiously.
 Anti-nausea medications (e.g., Ondansetron) for vomiting.
 Pain management (avoid NSAIDs, use acetaminophen if needed).
 5. Lifestyle Modifications and Long-Term Monitoring
 Stress management (yoga, meditation) to reduce flare triggers.
 Regular gastroenterology follow-ups to monitor disease progression.
 Smoking cessation (if applicable), as smoking worsens Crohn’s.
 Con

Treatment Task: I am a 45 year old woman and woke up this morning with back pain. I could only go about my daily life in pain and taking a tablet of ibuprofen. Then the pain got so bad that I was afraid my back would break apart. The pain also radiates into my right leg. It is so strong. My doctor has diagnosed me with Spinal disc herniation. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Spinal disc herniation
 
  Model Output:Pain Management & Anti-Inflammatory Medication
 
 NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen, naproxen, or diclofenac) – Reduce inflammation and pain.
 Muscle relaxants (e.g., tizanidine, baclofen) – If muscle spasms are present.
 Short-term corticosteroids (oral or injection) – Can help reduce inflammation if NSAIDs are insufficient.
 Physical Therapy & Gentle Movement
 
 Guided physical therapy to strengthen the back and core muscles.
 McKenzie exercises & stretching to improve mobility and reduce nerve compression.
 Avoid prolonged bed rest, as movement (within pain tolerance) helps recovery.
 Epidural Steroid Injections (If Severe or Persistent Pain)
 
 Corticosteroid injections directly into the affected spinal area can reduce nerve inflammation and pain, especially for sciatica or radicular pain.
 Heat & Cold Therapy
 
 Cold packs (first 48 hours) to reduce swelling.
 Heat therapy (after acute phase) to relax muscles and improve blood flow.
 Surgical Intervention (If Symptoms Worsen or Do Not Improve with Conservative Therapy)
 
 Microdiscectomy – If severe nerve compression causes persistent pain, weakness, or bowel/bladder dysfunction.
 Laminectomy or Spinal Fusion – In more severe or recurrent cases.

Treatment Task: I am a 19 year old female student and I have been having an existing headache on my left temple and behind my eye since morning. The pain is throbbing and very severe. The headache has developed over a few minutes and is accompanied by marked nausea and vomiting. Movement and bright light make it worse. Over the past 6 months, there have been five similar episodes, each lasting 1-2 days. This time, however, the headache has lasted longer and I have seen flickering for 20 minutes before the headache started. I have no pre-existing conditions and I am not taking any medications. My doctor has diagnosed me with Migraine (with/without aura). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Migraine (with/without aura)
 
  Model Output:1. Acute Attack Treatment (Pain Relief)
 Triptans (e.g., Sumatriptan, Rizatriptan, Zolmitriptan) – Most effective for stopping migraine attacks once they start.
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Naproxen, Diclofenac) – Can help if taken early in mild cases.
 Antiemetics (e.g., Metoclopramide, Domperidone) – Help control nausea and vomiting.
 2. Preventive Medications (For Frequent or Severe Migraines)
 Beta-blockers (e.g., Propranolol, Metoprolol) – Reduce migraine frequency.
 Calcium channel blockers (e.g., Flunarizine, Verapamil) – Sometimes used for migraine prevention.
 Antiepileptics (e.g., Topiramate, Valproate) – Effective for chronic migraine.
 Tricyclic Antidepressants (e.g., Amitriptyline) – Can help prevent migraines and improve sleep.
 3. Lifestyle Adjustments and Trigger Avoidance
 Identify and avoid triggers (e.g., stress, caffeine, irregular sleep, dehydration).
 Regular sleep and meal schedules to stabilize brain function.
 Exercise and relaxation techniques (yoga, meditation) to reduce stress.
 4. Neuromodulation Therapies (Non-Drug Approaches)
 Cefaly® device (external trigeminal nerve stimulator) – Can reduce attack frequency.
 Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS) – Sometimes effective for migraine with aura.
 5. Emergency Management (For Prolonged or Severe Attacks)
 Hospital treatment with IV fluids, triptans, and antiemetics for severe, persistent attacks.
 Corticosteroids (e.g., Dexamethasone) may help prevent recurrence after a severe migraine.

Treatment Task: I am a 25-year-old man and I have incredibly severe pain in my right eye. It is just 2 am and I feel like someone is stabbing me in the eye with a knife. I am unable to sit or lie still and just woke up in pain. When I look in the mirror, the right eye looks red and the eyelid hangs a bit. I had a similar event a week ago, but it was less painful and my dentist attributed it to a root infection in my upper right jaw. I have no pre-existing conditions and am not on any medications. My doctor has diagnosed me with Cluster headache. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cluster headache
 
  Model Output:1. Acute Treatment (Stopping an Attack Quickly)
 100% Oxygen Therapy (First-Line Treatment)
 
 Inhaling high-flow oxygen (12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask) for 10-15 minutes can rapidly abort an attack.
 This is the most effective and safest acute treatment.
 Triptans (Fast-Acting Injectable or Nasal Form)
 
 Sumatriptan (6 mg subcutaneous injection) – Works within 10-15 minutes.
 Zolmitriptan (Nasal spray) – Alternative if injections are not available.
 Oral triptans are not effective due to slow onset.
 2. Preventive Therapy (Reducing Attack Frequency)
 Verapamil (First-Line Preventive Medication)
 
 A calcium channel blocker taken daily to reduce attack frequency.
 Starting dose: 240 mg/day, adjusted based on response.
 Corticosteroids (Short-Term Prevention for Active Clusters)
 
 Prednisone (60-80 mg/day, tapered over 2-3 weeks) can break a cluster cycle.
 Used only for short periods due to side effects.
 Lithium or Galcanezumab (For Chronic or Resistant Cases)
 
 Lithium (300-900 mg/day) – Effective in chronic cases.
 Galcanezumab (CGRP inhibitor) – Newer option for cluster headache prevention.
 Additional Strategies
 Avoid triggers (alcohol, smoking, strong smells).
 Regular sleep schedule to stabilize attack patterns.
 Neuromodulation (non-invasive vagus nerve stimulation) may help in some cases.

Treatment Task: I am a 40-year-old woman and I have not been able to walk properly for 2 days. My legs are numb and I have pain in my abdomen. 6 months ago I noticed a visual disturbance on the left side that lasted for days, but I did not go to the doctor. Otherwise, I have only celiac disease as a pre-existing condition. My doctor has diagnosed me with Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder
 
  Model Output:Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD) is an autoimmune condition affecting the central nervous system, particularly the optic nerves and spinal cord. Given your symptoms—leg numbness, abdominal pain, and a past visual disturbance—early and aggressive treatment is crucial to prevent relapses and further neurological damage.
 
 Here are five appropriate therapies for your case:
 
 Acute Treatment (for the current episode)
 High-Dose Intravenous Corticosteroids (IV Methylprednisolone)
 
 Typically 1,000 mg IV per day for 3–5 days
 Helps reduce inflammation and suppress the immune system.
 Plasma Exchange (PLEX, also called Plasmapheresis)
 
 Recommended if steroids are ineffective.
 Removes circulating antibodies that drive NMOSD.
 Long-Term Immunosuppressive Therapy (to prevent relapses)
 Eculizumab (Soliris) or Ravulizumab (Ultomiris)
 
 Complement inhibitor that blocks the immune response driving NMOSD.
 FDA-approved and highly effective for relapse prevention.
 Satralizumab (Enspryng) or Tocilizumab
 
 IL-6 inhibitors that reduce inflammation and autoantibody production.
 Azathioprine, Mycophenolate Mofetil (CellCept), or Rituximab
 
 B-cell-targeted therapies (Rituximab is most effective).
 Reduce attack frequency and disability progression.
 Additional Considerations

Treatment Task: My 53-year-old husband has been suffering from inner restlessness for 2 years. He constantly gets involuntary, enclosing movements in his arms, legs and face. He can hardly stand still and sometimes it looks like he is dancing. In the beginning he was able to cover this up well and he simply integrated the movement with others. In the meantime, however, this is no longer possible. During physical activity or due to mental tension, his complaints increase. He also becomes increasingly aggressive and forgets things more often. Together we have two children and four grandchildren. As a pre-existing condition he suffers only from diabetes, which he treats with insulin. My doctor has diagnosed me with Huntington's disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Huntington's disease
 
  Model Output:1. Tetrabenazine or Deutetrabenazine (VMAT2 Inhibitors) → First-Line for Chorea
 Reduces involuntary movements (chorea) by depleting dopamine.
 Deutetrabenazine has fewer side effects than tetrabenazine.
 2. Atypical Antipsychotics (e.g., Risperidone, Olanzapine, Quetiapine) → For Aggression and Chorea
 Helps with aggression, irritability, and mood swings.
 Also reduces chorea but less effectively than VMAT2 inhibitors.
 3. Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs, e.g., Sertraline, Fluoxetine) → For Depression and Anxiety
 Helps with psychiatric symptoms like depression, anxiety, and emotional instability.
 4. Physical and Occupational Therapy → For Mobility and Daily Functioning
 Helps maintain balance, coordination, and muscle control.
 Reduces fall risk and improves independence.
 5. Speech and Cognitive Therapy → For Memory and Communication
 Supports cognitive function and language abilities as HD progresses.
 Can also assist with swallowing difficulties in later stages.
 Additional Considerations
 Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) is not typically effective for chorea in HD.
 If your husband has severe aggression or psychosis, stronger antipsychotic medication may be needed.
 Support groups and genetic counseling can help the family manage the disease's impact.
 Since Huntington’s disease is progressive, a multidisciplinary approach involving neurologists, psychiatrists, and therapists is key to maintaining the best possible quality of life.

Treatment Task: I am a woman (52 years old) and I have weak legs. In the last week I have noticed the problem increasingly when climbing stairs. I live on the 4th floor and have always been able to get up there without any problems. Lately, however, I always need breaks in between. This usually manifests itself as weakness and pain in the pelvic muscles, although I have no shortness of breath or pressure on my chest. In addition, there is a lilac redness on my face. This does not bother me, neither itching nor pain. For this I now take a more covering day cream. My doctor has diagnosed me with Dermatomyositis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Dermatomyositis
 
  Model Output:1. Corticosteroids (Prednisolone) – First-Line Treatment
 High-dose oral corticosteroids (1 mg/kg/day of prednisone)
 Reduces muscle inflammation and improves strength.
 Tapering is required to minimize side effects.
 2. Immunosuppressants (Methotrexate, Azathioprine, or Mycophenolate Mofetil)
 Used as a steroid-sparing agent to prevent long-term corticosteroid side effects.
 Methotrexate is often preferred for muscle symptoms.
 Mycophenolate Mofetil may be more effective if lung involvement occurs.
 3. Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG) – For Severe or Treatment-Resistant Cases
 Given IV over several days per month to improve muscle function.
 Especially effective if dysphagia or rapid progression is present.
 4. Hydroxychloroquine or Sunscreen – For Skin Involvement
 Hydroxychloroquine can help manage facial redness and skin symptoms.
 Strict sun protection (high SPF, covering clothing) is recommended to prevent worsening.
 5. Physical Therapy and Rehabilitation
 Muscle-strengthening exercises to prevent muscle atrophy.
 Helps maintain mobility and prevent contractures.

Treatment Task: I am a 35-year-old painter and have had weak hands and arms for several months. That's why I always have problems holding the paintbrush. I have also been asked about my thinner arms and legs, even though I used to do a lot of sports. Now that I pay attention to it, my hands and shoulders cramp just at the beginning of the workday. This stops after a few seconds of hand movement. However, they only hurt moderately. My father had similar complaints and then died of a heart attack at the age of 45. In addition, I suffer from the following pre-existing conditions: diabetes, unclear cardiac arrhythmia and cataracts. My doctor has diagnosed me with Myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)
 
  Model Output:1. Physical and Occupational Therapy
 Goal: Maintain muscle strength and flexibility, prevent contractures, and improve hand function.
 Approach:
 Stretching and low-impact exercises (e.g., swimming, cycling)
 Assistive devices (e.g., hand splints, adapted paintbrushes)
 Occupational therapy for grip strength and fine motor skills
 2. Myotonia Treatment (Muscle Cramping) – Medications
 Goal: Reduce stiffness and cramps to improve hand function.
 First-line medication: Mexiletine (sodium channel blocker) – effectively reduces myotonia.
 Alternatives: Lamotrigine, carbamazepine, or phenytoin (if mexiletine is not tolerated).
 3. Cardiac Monitoring & Treatment
 Goal: Prevent severe arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death.
 Approach:
 Regular ECG and Holter monitoring (every 6-12 months).
 If significant conduction abnormalities are detected, pacemaker or defibrillator implantation may be necessary.
 Avoid medications that prolong the QT interval (can worsen arrhythmias).
 4. Diabetes Management
 Goal: Control blood sugar to reduce complications and muscle deterioration.
 Approach:
 Diet and exercise: Focus on balanced meals with slow-digesting carbohydrates.
 Medications: Metformin (if tolerated) or insulin, depending on blood sugar levels.
 5. Cataract Surgery (if vision is impaired)
 Goal: Improve vision to maintain independence and work ability.
 Approach:
 Regular ophthalmology check-ups to monitor cataract progression.
 Consider early cataract surgery if vision impairment interferes with daily life.

Treatment Task: My 75-year-old wife has had slow but increasing speech disorders with word-finding problems for almost 5 years in retrospect. She is also increasingly forgetful and can hardly manage everyday tasks. She forgets the names of our own children. In addition, she continues to withdraw and has orientation problems (frequent trips, for example to the supermarket, are no longer possible). She also had problems with depression in the past, which is why she took lithium until a year ago. Currently she does not take it regularly. We have no comparable case in our family. My doctor has diagnosed me with Alzheimer's disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Alzheimer's disease
 
  Model Output:1. Medication for Cognitive Decline
 Goal: Slow cognitive decline and improve memory.
 Approach:
 Cholinesterase inhibitors (for mild to moderate AD):
 Donepezil, Rivastigmine, or Galantamine → improve neurotransmission.
 NMDA receptor antagonist (for moderate to severe AD):
 Memantine → helps with memory and reduces confusion.
 2. Structured Cognitive Training & Occupational Therapy
 Goal: Maintain daily skills and orientation as long as possible.
 Approach:
 Cognitive therapy (memory exercises, puzzles, structured routines).
 Occupational therapy (training for daily tasks, safety strategies).
 Reality orientation therapy (calendars, labeled objects, reminders).
 3. Behavioral & Psychological Support (Depression & Agitation Management)
 Goal: Reduce withdrawal, anxiety, depression, and agitation.
 Approach:
 Non-medication strategies:
 Music therapy, social interaction, reminiscence therapy.
 Creating a structured, calming environment.
 Medication if necessary:
 SSRIs (e.g., sertraline, citalopram) for depression and mood stabilization.
 Avoid benzodiazepines (increase fall risk and confusion).
 4. Safety and Orientation Measures
 Goal: Prevent accidents and ensure safe movement.
 Approach:
 Supervised walks, GPS tracking devices if she wanders.
 Home modifications (removing tripping hazards, clear labeling).
 Establishing consistent routines to reduce confusion.
 5. Caregiver Support & Future Planning
 Goal: Prepare for disease progression and reduce caregiver burden.
 Approach:
 Support groups (Alzheimer's associations, caregiver support programs).
 Advanced care planning (power of attorney, legal and financial planning).
 Consider assisted living or home nursing support in later stages.

Treatment Task: I am a 23 year old woman and I work as a bank employee. Besides a general lack of drive, I have weak muscles and am gaining weight (11 kg in 6 months). I gave birth to twins 10 months ago and they are currently in foster care. This is due to my depression, for which I am also in psychiatric treatment. I haven't gotten my menstruation back yet after delivery. During pregnancy I had high blood pressure and glucosuria with impaired glucose tolerance. Other than that, I do not suffer from any pre-existing conditions. My doctor has diagnosed me with Cushing's syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cushing's syndrome
 
  Model Output:Surgical Removal of the Source (if applicable)
 
 If Cushing's syndrome is caused by a pituitary adenoma (Cushing’s disease) or an adrenal tumor, surgery to remove the tumor is the preferred treatment.
 In cases of an ACTH-secreting tumor (e.g., lung cancer or neuroendocrine tumors), surgery or targeted treatment may be required.
 Medication to Lower Cortisol Levels
 
 If surgery is not an option or is ineffective, cortisol-lowering medications can help manage symptoms. These may include:
 Ketoconazole, Metyrapone, or Mitotane (inhibit cortisol production)
 Osilodrostat or Mifepristone (used for severe Cushing's with diabetes)
 Your doctor will select the appropriate medication based on your specific case.
 Radiation Therapy (for Pituitary Tumors)
 
 If a pituitary tumor is the cause and surgery is not fully effective, radiotherapy may be used to shrink the tumor and reduce cortisol overproduction.
 Lifestyle and Supportive Therapies
 
 Diet and exercise: A low-calorie, high-protein diet combined with strength training can help with weight management and muscle weakness.
 Bone protection: Cushing’s increases the risk of osteoporosis, so calcium and vitamin D supplements may be recommended.
 Mental health support: Continuation of psychiatric treatment for depression is crucial, as high cortisol levels can contribute to mood disorders.
 Hormone Replacement Therapy (if needed after treatment)
 
 If treatment leads to adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol production), you may need temporary glucocorticoid replacement therapy (e.g., hydrocortisone) until your body recovers normal function.
 If menstruation does not return after treatment, hormonal therapy may be needed.

Treatment Task: I am a 67 year old farmer and I have excruciating pain in my lower legs every night. It also regularly wakes me up. The pain is worse on the right than on the left and sometimes so severe that I can no longer stay in bed. When I sit up, it gets a little better. Since about 2 years I am generally limited in everyday life. When walking, I feel a burning pain in my right calf after a distance of 75 meters, which becomes so severe after 150 meters that I have to stop. The discomfort then subsides so that I can walk again after a short time. This is the reason why I don't leave my yard much anymore. I smoke regularly, about 60 pack-years, and occasionally drink alcohol. My father passed away from a heart attack and my mother from a stroke. My doctor has diagnosed me with Peripheral artery disease . What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Peripheral artery disease 
 
  Model Output:Smoking Cessation (Most Crucial Step)
 
 Continuing to smoke severely worsens PAD and increases the risk of heart attacks and strokes. Stopping smoking is the single most important step to slow disease progression and improve circulation.
 Structured Walking Therapy (Supervised Exercise Program)
 
 Regular, structured walking training can help improve blood circulation and increase your walking distance before pain sets in.
 Strategy: Walk until pain occurs, rest briefly, and then continue. Over time, this helps build new blood vessels (collateral circulation).
 Medication Therapy
 
 Antiplatelet therapy (e.g., Aspirin or Clopidogrel) to reduce the risk of heart attack or stroke.
 Statins (e.g., Atorvastatin or Rosuvastatin) to lower cholesterol and slow plaque buildup in arteries.
 Cilostazol or Naftidrofuryl may help improve walking distance by increasing blood flow.
 Revascularization (for Severe Cases)
 
 If symptoms do not improve with conservative treatments, a minimally invasive procedure (angioplasty with stent placement) or bypass surgery may be needed to restore blood flow.
 Pain and Nighttime Symptom Management
 
 Keeping your legs in a dependent position (lowered over the side of the bed) can help reduce nighttime pain.
 Painkillers may be prescribed, but addressing the root circulation problem is key.
 Warm socks and gentle massage may improve blood flow and provide symptom relief.

Treatment Task: I am a 63-year-old man and have been suffering from fatigue, headaches and aching limbs, repeated fevers up to 39 degrees, night sweats, loss of appetite and an unwanted weight loss of 5 kg since a cold 2 months ago. Suspecting polymyalgia rheumatica, I have been treated with glucocorticoids, but this has only slightly improved the symptoms. I have diabetes. In addition, I had a mechanical valve replacement 9 months ago. The reason was a high-grade aortic valve stenosis. My doctor has diagnosed me with Subacute bacterial endocarditis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Subacute bacterial endocarditis
 
  Model Output:Prolonged Intravenous (IV) Antibiotic Therapy (Cornerstone of Treatment)
 
 High-dose IV antibiotics are required for 4 to 6 weeks to eradicate the infection. The specific choice depends on blood culture results but often includes:
 Vancomycin + Gentamicin (empirical therapy for prosthetic valve endocarditis)
 Ceftriaxone or Ampicillin-Sulbactam (depending on bacterial sensitivity)
 Blood cultures will guide adjustments in antibiotic choice.
 Monitoring for Valve Dysfunction and Heart Failure
 
 Regular echocardiography (TEE - transesophageal echocardiography) to assess for vegetations, valve damage, or abscess formation.
 Close monitoring for heart failure symptoms, which may require urgent intervention.
 Anticoagulation Management (Due to Mechanical Valve)
 
 As you have a mechanical aortic valve, you are likely on warfarin or another anticoagulant.
 Endocarditis increases the risk of embolism, but anticoagulation must be carefully managed (your doctor may adjust or temporarily stop anticoagulation depending on risk).
 Surgical Intervention (If Indicated)
 
 If antibiotics fail or if you develop:
 Severe valve destruction or regurgitation
 Large vegetations (>10 mm) with embolic risk
 Signs of heart failure or abscess formation
 You may need repeat valve surgery (valve replacement or debridement).
 Management of Diabetes and Supportive Care
 
 Strict blood sugar control is essential to improve infection response.
 Nutritional support to counteract weight loss and weakness.
 Pain and fever management (e.g., acetaminophen) while treating the underlying infection.

disease_GT: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: Our 18 year old daughter is quite dazed. She had severe abdominal pain last night and vomited several times. This morning she barely responded to being spoken to. For the past 4 days, our daughter has also been taking an antibiotic for a middle ear infection. In addition, she has been complaining of weakness in general for a few weeks, is drinking noticeably, is getting thinner and thinner, and is constantly going to the toilet. We already think that she has an eating disorder. My doctor has diagnosed me with Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Insulin-dependent Diabetes mellitus / Diabetes mellitus type 1
 
  Model Output:Immediate Action: Go to the Emergency Room (ER) Immediately!
 DKA requires urgent hospitalization. Do not wait. Delayed treatment can lead to coma or death.
 
 Most Appropriate Therapies for Suspected DKA
 Intravenous (IV) Insulin Therapy
 
 Continuous insulin infusion to lower blood sugar and stop ketone production.
 Intravenous Fluid Replacement
 
 Large amounts of fluids (0.9% saline initially) to correct dehydration and electrolyte imbalances.
 Electrolyte Correction (Especially Potassium)
 
 Potassium levels need to be closely monitored and corrected to prevent life-threatening arrhythmias.
 Acidosis and Ketosis Management
 
 Insulin and fluids help resolve ketoacidosis, while bicarbonate therapy is sometimes needed for severe cases.
 Identification and Treatment of Underlying Causes
 
 Possible new-onset type 1 diabetes, infection (ear infection, antibiotic use as a trigger), or another metabolic issue.

Treatment Task: I am a 27 year old college student suffering from loss of appetite, weight loss (8 kg from last year) and have been sluggish for months. Last week I also collapsed several times. My skin is browner than it used to be even though I'm not out in the sun anymore. I was actually a gymnast since I was 12 years old with training several times a week and competitions. However, I had to give that up because of the ailments. I don't have any pre-existing conditions, I don't take any medications and I don't drink much alcohol or smoke. My doctor has diagnosed me with Addison's disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Addison's disease
 
  Model Output:Glucocorticoid Replacement (Hydrocortisone or Prednisolone)
 
 Hydrocortisone (preferred): Typically 15–25 mg/day divided into 2–3 doses (higher dose in the morning, lower in the afternoon).
 Alternatively, prednisolone or dexamethasone can be used in some cases.
 Mineralocorticoid Replacement (Fludrocortisone)
 
 Needed to maintain blood pressure and electrolyte balance (especially sodium and potassium).
 Typical dose: 0.05–0.2 mg/day.
 Adjusted based on blood pressure, potassium, and renin levels.
 Salt and Fluid Intake Adjustments
 
 Increased salt intake is often needed to prevent dehydration and low blood pressure.
 Stay well-hydrated, especially in hot weather or during illness.
 Emergency Steroid Dosing (Stress Dosing)
 
 Double or triple your glucocorticoid dose in case of illness, infection, surgery, or physical stress to prevent an adrenal crisis.
 Carry a glucocorticoid emergency injection kit (e.g., intramuscular hydrocortisone 100 mg) in case of vomiting or collapse.
 Medical Alert Bracelet and Patient Education
 
 Wear a medical alert bracelet stating "Adrenal Insufficiency – Needs Steroids".
 Learn how to recognize and manage an adrenal crisis (severe weakness, vomiting, confusion, shock).
 Close endocrinologist follow-up to adjust hormone doses and prevent complications.

Treatment Task: My 9 year old son fell at school a week ago and hit his right knee. The wound is not healing properly and large bruises have developed on his hands and elbows. This is not the first time Max has developed large bruises. This happens again and again with slight bruises, e.g. on the forehead or like now on the knee. But I have that all the time, too, probably it runs in the family. But it has never been as bad as it is now. A year ago there was also a funny situation: I lifted him up while playing and turned him in a circle, and he dislocated both arms.
