Apart from that, Max is completely healthy, has no previous illnesses and takes no medication. He is otherwise a bright and lively boy. My doctor has diagnosed me with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome
 
  Model Output:Physiotherapy and Strength Training
 
 Goal: Improve joint stability and muscle strength to reduce dislocations and injuries.
 Focus on: Core stability, proprioception, and low-impact exercises like swimming.
 Protective Measures to Prevent Injuries and Bruising
 
 Use padded clothing (e.g., knee and elbow pads) during play and sports.
 Avoid contact sports or activities that put stress on joints (e.g., gymnastics, heavy lifting).
 Use assistive devices if needed (e.g., braces, orthotics).
 Wound Care and Skin Protection
 
 Gentle wound care: Use steri-strips instead of stitches (skin is fragile).
 Moisturize skin to prevent tearing.
 Protect from bruising with soft padding and careful handling.
 Pain Management and Joint Care
 
 Over-the-counter pain relievers (e.g., paracetamol, ibuprofen) for joint and muscle pain.
 Avoid unnecessary surgeries—poor healing and complications are common.
 Regular check-ups with a rheumatologist or geneticist to monitor symptoms.
 Genetic Counseling and Family Screening
 
 If you have EDS, there’s a high chance Max has inherited it.
 Genetic testing may help confirm the type of EDS (especially if vascular EDS is suspected, which requires extra precautions).
 Education about EDS for teachers, coaches, and caregivers is essential to prevent injuries.

Treatment Task: I am a 52 year old female vet and have had unsteadiness when I walk for 18 months. In the last few weeks, there has been a continued worsening of my symptoms and I have recently fallen several times. In the meantime, I feel like I can no longer adequately control my legs. I also have recurring headaches, difficulty concentrating, and a new hearing loss. My husband thinks I am also becoming more forgetful and not speaking as clearly. I have arterial hypertension and Algerian asthma. At 17, I suffered some broken bones in a serious horseback riding accident. At the age of 29, a severe episode of headache occurred for the first time, after which an intracranial aneurysm had to be surgically treated. I take ramipril and sometimes salbutamol. I have no other allergies, but I smoke and occasionally consume alcohol. My doctor has diagnosed me with Superficial siderosis of the CNS. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Superficial siderosis of the CNS
 
  Model Output:Identification and Surgical Management of Bleeding Source
 
 Goal: Stop chronic bleeding to prevent further iron deposition.
 MRI and angiography should be used to identify a possible source (e.g., aneurysm, dural defect).
 Surgical repair or embolization may be necessary if a treatable bleeding site is found.
 Iron Chelation Therapy (Deferiprone)
 
 Deferiprone (Ferriprox) is an oral iron chelator that may help remove excess iron from the CNS.
 Clinical studies show mixed results, but some patients experience slowed progression.
 Requires regular blood monitoring due to potential side effects (e.g., agranulocytosis).
 Symptom Management and Rehabilitation
 
 Physical therapy: Helps maintain mobility and reduce fall risk.
 Hearing aids or cochlear implants for sensorineural hearing loss.
 Speech therapy if language difficulties worsen.
 Blood Pressure Control and Stroke Prevention
 
 Strict hypertension management (continue ramipril or adjust as needed) to reduce bleeding risk.
 Avoid blood thinners (aspirin, NSAIDs) unless absolutely necessary due to bleeding risk.
 Neurological and Cognitive Support
 
 Regular neurological follow-ups to monitor progression.
 Cognitive therapy or memory training if cognitive decline worsens.
 Smoking cessation to improve cerebrovascular health and prevent further complications.

Treatment Task: My 14-year-old daughter, Johanna, has severe abdominal pain. In the morning, she went to school feeling slightly unwell; during the day, she developed very severe abdominal pain with nausea and has vomited and had diarrhea several times. In time with the onset of the abdominal pain, she noticed a metallic taste in her mouth. Johanna's father has developed severe edema in the affected areas after minor bruising. Otherwise, I can't think of any hereditary diseases. Johanna used to have mild to moderate swelling of the extremities as a child, which we always treated with cold water. Johanna's condition is getting worse and worse. My doctor has diagnosed me with Hereditary angioedema. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hereditary angioedema
 
  Model Output:Most Appropriate Therapies for Hereditary Angioedema (HAE)
 Acute Attack Treatment – C1 Inhibitor Replacement Therapy
 
 Plasma-derived C1-inhibitor concentrate (Berinert, Cinryze, or Ruconest) should be administered as soon as possible to halt the attack.
 This is the first-line treatment for severe abdominal or laryngeal HAE attacks.
 Bradykinin Pathway Inhibitors for Acute Attacks
 
 Icatibant (Firazyr): A bradykinin receptor antagonist that can be self-injected for rapid symptom relief.
 Used if C1-inhibitor is not available or as an alternative.
 Long-Term Prophylaxis (Prevention) for Frequent Attacks
 
 If Johanna has frequent attacks, she may need regular prophylaxis with C1-inhibitor (Cinryze, Takhzyro [lanadelumab], or Androgens like Danazol) to prevent future episodes.
 Takhzyro (lanadelumab) is a monoclonal antibody that prevents attacks by blocking kallikrein.
 On-Demand Emergency Medication and Avoidance of Triggers
 
 Johanna should carry emergency medication (C1-inhibitor or Icatibant) and know how to self-administer.
 Avoid triggers such as trauma, stress, infections, estrogen-containing medications (e.g., birth control pills), and ACE inhibitors.
 Hospitalization if Symptoms Worsen or Laryngeal Edema Develops
 
 Severe abdominal attacks can mimic appendicitis and may require IV fluids and pain management.
 Airway involvement is an emergency—immediate intubation or tracheostomy may be required.

Treatment Task: I am a 21 year old woman and a student at the moment. For the repeated time, I now have fever up to 39.1 degrees, muscle pain and a big skin rash on both forearms, as well as I also have eye and stomach pain. The fever has been going on for a week. In the morning I vomited for the first time. Nobody else in my environment is sick. I have had weeks like this before. That's why I had to miss school more often as a child. However, the complaints were never investigated further. I have never traveled outside Europe. I have no previous illnesses. My mother suffers from renal insufficiency. I do not consume alcohol, nor do I smoke. When I was 8 years old I had similar symptoms and that is when my appendix was removed. However, in retrospect, that turns out to be unremarkable. My doctor has diagnosed me with TNF receptor associated periodic syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: TNF receptor associated periodic syndrome
 
  Model Output:IL-1 Inhibitors (First-Line Treatment) – Anakinra or Canakinumab
 
 Anakinra (Kineret): A daily interleukin-1 (IL-1) receptor antagonist that blocks inflammation and reduces fever attacks.
 Canakinumab (Ilaris): A long-acting IL-1 inhibitor that is injected once per month, often preferred for long-term control.
 These are the most effective treatments for TRAPS and can significantly reduce the frequency and severity of attacks.
 Corticosteroids for Acute Flare Management
 
 Prednisolone or Methylprednisolone can be used in low doses to quickly reduce fever, rash, and pain during attacks.
 Long-term steroid use is not recommended due to side effects, but it helps in acute situations.
 TNF Inhibitors (Alternative Therapy) – Etanercept
 
 Etanercept (a TNF-α blocker) was historically used but is less effective than IL-1 inhibitors.
 Some patients still respond well, but not all TRAPS cases improve with TNF inhibitors.
 Colchicine for Symptom Control and Amyloidosis Prevention
 
 Colchicine (commonly used in Familial Mediterranean Fever) may help in some TRAPS cases to reduce inflammation and prevent kidney complications.
 Its effectiveness in TRAPS is not as well-established as in FMF, but it can be tried in mild cases.
 Supportive Management: Pain Control and Fever Reduction
 
 NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen, naproxen) for fever and muscle pain.
 Hydration and rest during flare-ups.
 Regular kidney function monitoring (due to risk of amyloidosis, especially given your family history of renal insufficiency).

Treatment Task: I am a 47-year-old male and have been suffering from high fever and muscle pain for a few days. I first thought it was a flu-like infection. Other members of my family also have similar symptoms. However, since this morning I have had swelling on my face, especially around my eyes. A fortnight ago I was at a barbecue and had diarrhea and severe abdominal pain for several days. There were wild boar sausages that a friend of mine, an Italian, brought from his home country. Otherwise, I am actually healthy. I do not take any medication, I am only allergic to penicillin. My doctor has diagnosed me with Trichinosis or trichinellosis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Trichinosis or trichinellosis
 
  Model Output:1. Antiparasitic Medication (Primary Treatment)
 Albendazole (400 mg twice daily for 8–14 days) or
 Mebendazole (200–400 mg three times daily for 3 days, then 400–500 mg three times daily for 10 days)
 These kill the larvae in the intestines and prevent further spread.
 2. Corticosteroids (For Severe Symptoms)
 Prednisone (30–60 mg/day, tapered over 10–14 days)
 Used if there is severe inflammation, high fever, or widespread muscle pain to reduce the immune response.
 3. Pain and Fever Management
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Paracetamol) help relieve muscle pain and fever.
 Avoid aspirin if there is suspicion of gastrointestinal bleeding.
 4. Nutritional and Supportive Therapy
 Electrolyte and fluid replacement if dehydration occurs due to diarrhea.
 Protein-rich diet to help with muscle recovery.
 5. Hospitalization (For Severe Cases)
 If there are severe complications (e.g., myocarditis, encephalitis, severe weakness), hospital monitoring is required.
 Additional Advice
 Family members may also need evaluation, as they consumed the same wild boar sausage.
 Avoid consuming raw or undercooked wild game meat in the future (cook to ≥71°C).
 Since you have a penicillin allergy, ensure that alternative antibiotics are used if a secondary bacterial infection develops.

disease_GT: Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: I am a 66 year old man and have severe feelings of illness with weakness, abdominal pain, loss of appetite, chills and aching limbs. I went to the doctor 4 days ago because I had a high fever overnight (38.8), chills, severe headache and aching limbs. Since it is fall, this was considered the flu. I was told to stay on bed rest, take ibuprofen and drink enough. I did all of that, however, the symptoms got much worse from yesterday to today. In addition, I have severe pain in my stomach, I am no longer hungry and my eyes are sensitive to light and constantly watering. Even if I think back, nothing special has happened. I winterized the garden 2 weeks ago, with everything that goes with it: cleaned the pond from dead plant remains and fetched firewood for the fireplace from the shed. On top of everything, today I have a dizzy feeling when I try to get up. My doctor has diagnosed me with Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. Hospitalization and Supportive Care (Essential)
 Close monitoring in a hospital is required due to risk of renal failure, hemorrhage, and shock.
 IV fluids to maintain hydration and electrolyte balance.
 Monitoring urine output and kidney function (creatinine, urea levels).
 2. Pain and Fever Management
 Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) for fever and body aches (avoid NSAIDs like Ibuprofen due to risk of kidney damage and bleeding).
 Cooling measures (lukewarm compresses) if fever is high.
 3. Blood Pressure and Shock Management
 If blood pressure drops: IV fluids, vasopressors (e.g., Norepinephrine) may be needed.
 If blood pressure rises: Medications like Calcium Channel Blockers (e.g., Nifedipine) may be required.
 4. Dialysis (If Kidney Failure Develops)
 Hemodialysis may be necessary if there is severe kidney impairment, oliguria (low urine output), or electrolyte imbalances.
 5. Antiviral Treatment (Experimental)
 Ribavirin (used in early stages in some cases) may reduce disease severity but is not always standard for all Hantavirus types.
 Additional Advice:
 Avoid nephrotoxic drugs (e.g., NSAIDs, certain antibiotics).
 Strict bed rest and avoid dehydration to prevent worsening kidney function.
 Rodent control measures to prevent future infections.

Treatment Task: I am a 70-year-old man and have been suffering from nausea and vomiting for a week. My wife noticed yellowing of the skin in the previous two days. In addition, I have lost about 7 kg in the previous month. My doctor has diagnosed me with Cholangicarcinoma. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cholangicarcinoma
 
  Model Output:1. Surgical Resection (Curative Option in Early Stages)
 If the tumor is localized and operable, surgery is the best chance for cure:
 Hepatectomy (Liver Resection) – If the tumor is in the intrahepatic bile ducts.
 Whipple Procedure (Pancreaticoduodenectomy) – If the tumor is in the perihilar or distal bile ducts.
 Surgery is only an option if the cancer has not spread significantly.
 2. Biliary Drainage (To Relieve Jaundice and Improve Symptoms)
 Endoscopic Biliary Stent Placement (ERCP with stenting) – A stent is placed to unblock bile flow and relieve jaundice, nausea, and vomiting.
 Percutaneous Biliary Drainage (PTBD) – If ERCP is not possible, bile drainage is done through the skin.
 3. Chemotherapy (For Unresectable or Metastatic Disease)
 Standard first-line chemotherapy:
 Gemcitabine + Cisplatin – Improves survival in advanced cases.
 If Cisplatin is not tolerated, Gemcitabine + Capecitabine may be used.
 4. Targeted Therapy (For Specific Mutations)
 If genetic testing shows IDH1, FGFR2, or HER2 mutations, targeted therapies may be an option:
 Pemigatinib (FGFR2 inhibitors).
 Ivosidenib (IDH1 inhibitors).
 5. Palliative Care (For Advanced Stages)
 Pain management (Opioids for severe pain).
 Nutritional support (Small, frequent meals).
 Management of ascites (fluid in the abdomen) or infections.
 Radiotherapy may help reduce tumor burden and pain in some cases.

Treatment Task: I am a 53 year old woman and have had problems for some time that I can no longer hold my bowel movements completely. In addition, when I have a bowel movement itself, there is always blood and something bulges, which I then have to push back again. I have had 3 vaginal births with two stitched perineal tears. My doctor has diagnosed me with Rectal procidentia or rectal prolapse. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Rectal procidentia or rectal prolapse
 
  Model Output:1. Surgical Repair (Definitive Treatment)
 Indicated if prolapse is persistent or affects quality of life.
 Types of surgery:
 Abdominal Rectopexy (Preferred for Younger, Fitter Patients)
 The rectum is lifted and fixed to the sacrum.
 Can be done laparoscopically or robotically (less invasive, quicker recovery).
 Perineal Surgery (For Older or High-Risk Patients)
 Altemeier Procedure (Perineal Proctosigmoidectomy) – Removal of the prolapsed section.
 Delorme’s Procedure – Mucosal stripping and plication (for smaller prolapses).
 2. Pelvic Floor Therapy (For Mild to Moderate Cases)
 Pelvic floor muscle training (Kegel exercises) to strengthen muscles.
 Biofeedback therapy to improve bowel control.
 Useful in early stages or post-surgery to prevent recurrence.
 3. Dietary and Bowel Management
 High-fiber diet (fruits, vegetables, whole grains) to prevent constipation and straining.
 Adequate hydration to keep stools soft.
 Avoid straining during bowel movements (use stool softeners if needed).
 4. Stool Management and Medications
 Bulk-forming laxatives (e.g., Psyllium, Methylcellulose) for soft, formed stools.
 Anti-diarrheal agents (Loperamide) if fecal incontinence is an issue.
 Topical treatments (e.g., hydrocortisone suppositories) if inflammation or irritation is present.
 5. Pessary or Support Devices (Temporary Relief)
 A vaginal or rectal pessary may help support the pelvic floor.
 More commonly used in women with both rectal and vaginal prolapse.

Treatment Task: I am a 30-year-old auto mechanic and have been experiencing increasing pain in my left shoulder. At first, it only occurred when I was working overhead. In the meantime, however, I have constant problems. In addition, I have numbness in my left forearm and hand. The left hand is also pale and feels cold. My doctor has diagnosed me with Neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome (TOS)
 
  Model Output:1. Physical Therapy (First-Line Treatment)
 Postural training to correct shoulder positioning.
 Stretching exercises to improve flexibility of the scalene muscles and pectoralis minor.
 Strengthening exercises (especially for the trapezius and serratus anterior) to improve shoulder stability.
 Nerve gliding exercises to relieve brachial plexus compression.
 2. Pain and Inflammation Management
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Naproxen) for pain relief and reducing inflammation.
 Muscle relaxants (e.g., Tizanidine, Baclofen) if muscle tightness is contributing to compression.
 Ice or heat therapy depending on pain triggers.
 3. Activity Modification and Ergonomic Adjustments
 Avoid prolonged overhead work or heavy lifting.
 Use ergonomic tools or modify work techniques to reduce strain.
 Frequent breaks and postural awareness to minimize compression.
 4. Botox or Local Anesthetic Injections (For Severe Cases)
 Scalene muscle injections to temporarily reduce muscle tightness and relieve compression.
 Botox injections may be used if conservative therapy fails.
 5. Surgery (Only for Severe or Refractory Cases)
 First rib resection and scalenectomy (removal of a portion of the first rib and scalene muscles) if nerve compression is severe and non-surgical treatments fail.
 Indicated if symptoms persist for 6+ months despite therapy or if significant weakness develops.

Treatment Task: I (40-year-old female) have increasing pain and tingling in the thumb, index finger and middle finger of my right hand. These occur mainly at night. My doctor has diagnosed me with Carpal tunnel syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Carpal tunnel syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. Wrist Splinting (First-Line Treatment)
 Wearing a wrist splint at night keeps the wrist in a neutral position, reducing pressure on the median nerve.
 Recommended especially for mild-to-moderate CTS.
 2. Anti-Inflammatory Medications (For Symptom Relief)
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Naproxen) may help reduce pain and inflammation.
 Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) for pain management.
 3. Corticosteroid Injections (For Persistent or Severe Symptoms)
 Lidocaine + Corticosteroid injection directly into the carpal tunnel to reduce inflammation.
 Provides temporary relief (weeks to months) and may delay the need for surgery.
 4. Physical Therapy and Nerve Gliding Exercises
 Carpal tunnel stretches to improve flexibility.
 Median nerve gliding exercises help reduce nerve compression.
 Ergonomic adjustments (e.g., correct hand positioning at work).
 5. Surgery (For Severe or Refractory Cases)
 Carpal tunnel release surgery (open or endoscopic) involves cutting the transverse carpal ligament to relieve pressure.
 Recommended if symptoms persist despite conservative treatment or there is muscle weakness.

Treatment Task: My 14-year-old daughter has been complaining of headaches, nausea and vomiting for several days. In addition, she has increased salivation and can not really drink anything despite thirst. She has no previous illnesses but was bitten by a cat last week. My doctor has diagnosed me with Rabies. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Rabies
 
  Model Output:1. Immediate Hospitalization and Intensive Supportive Care
 Rabies is nearly always fatal once symptoms appear, so ICU admission is necessary.
 Symptom management includes sedation, pain relief, and seizure control.
 Mechanical ventilation may be required if respiratory muscles are affected.
 2. Milwaukee Protocol (Experimental Therapy)
 An experimental approach that includes inducing a coma and using antiviral drugs like amantadine and ribavirin.
 Only a handful of people worldwide have survived rabies with this protocol, and its effectiveness is debated.
 3. Passive and Active Immunization (Only if Post-Exposure Prophylaxis Is Still an Option)
 If your daughter is still in the incubation period (before severe symptoms fully appear), she may benefit from:
 Rabies Immune Globulin (HRIG) around the wound site.
 Rabies Vaccination (4-5 doses over 14 days).
 Unfortunately, once neurological symptoms appear, this is unlikely to be effective.
 4. Antiviral Therapy (Experimental, Limited Effectiveness)
 Some antiviral drugs like ribavirin and interferon-alpha have been tried, but they have not consistently shown benefit.
 5. Symptomatic Treatment and Palliative Care
 If rabies is too advanced for curative treatment, palliative care focuses on relieving pain, anxiety, and seizures.
 Sedation, pain relief (opioids), and artificial nutrition may be necessary.

Treatment Task: I am a 51-year-old man and have been suffering from pain and itching at the anus for some time. Maybe they are hemorrhoids, because sometimes there is also blood on the toilet paper.  Used in metastatic or recurrent cases with high PD-L1 expression or MSI-H/dMMR tumors.
 Helps the immune system recognize and attack cancer cells.
 Targeted Therapy
 
 Investigational for cases with specific genetic mutations (e.g., EGFR inhibitors like Cetuximab).
 Used when standard treatments are not effective.
 Palliative Care
 
 For advanced, inoperable cases or to relieve symptoms.
 Includes pain management, symptom control, and localized radiation.

Treatment Task: I am a 41-year-old man and have stomach pain. My family doctor has already put me on proton pump inhibitor therapy for 3 months due to two diagnosed duodenal ulcers.  Typically used in cases of pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (pNETs).
 Chemotherapy (e.g., Streptozocin, 5-FU, Doxorubicin, or Temozolomide-based regimens)
 
 For metastatic or aggressive gastrinomas.

 Helps reduce tumor size and hormone secretion.

Treatment Task: I am a 17 year old girl and I am having increasingly severe pain in my lower right abdomen as well as nausea and vomiting. My doctor has diagnosed me with Acute appendicitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.

  Model Output:Appendectomy (Surgical Removal of the Appendix) – Standard Treatment

 Open Appendectomy (Used for severe cases, such as perforated or complicated appendicitis).
 Surgery is the most effective and definitive treatment.
 Pre- and Postoperative Antibiotic Therapy
 
 Pre-surgery: To reduce infection risk (e.g., ceftriaxone + metronidazole).
 Post-surgery: Needed if the appendix was perforated or an abscess is present.
 In mild cases, antibiotics alone might be considered, but surgery is preferred.
 Pain Management (Analgesics, IV Fluids)
 
 NSAIDs or acetaminophen for pain relief.
 IV fluids to prevent dehydration, especially if vomiting is severe.
 Anti-nausea medications (e.g., ondansetron) if needed.
 Non-Surgical Antibiotic Treatment (For Selected Cases)
 
 Used in uncomplicated cases when surgery is not immediately available or if the patient prefers non-surgical treatment.
 High-dose IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole).
 Risk of recurrence is higher compared to surgery.
 Drainage of an Abscess (If Present)
 
 If the appendix has ruptured and formed an abscess, a percutaneous drainage procedure may be required before surgery.
 Performed under ultrasound or CT guidance.
 Delayed appendectomy may follow after infection control.

Treatment Task: I am a 32 year old male and I have severe pain during stool. This pain is accompanied by itching and secretion of mucus. In addition, blood is found more often on the toilet paper.  Relieves pain, relaxes the sphincter, and promotes healing.

 Botulinum Toxin (Botox) Injection

 Injected into the internal anal sphincter to relax it and reduce pain.

 Lateral Internal Sphincterotomy (LIS) (Surgical Option for Chronic or Refractory Cases)

 Small incision in the internal anal sphincter to reduce pressure and promote healing.

 Would you like additional information on healing time, pain managem

Treatment Task: I am a 52 year old woman and have dysphagia that occurs mainly with liquid foods. In addition, I have pain behind the breastbone after eating. I have had this pain for several years, especially at night it has become unbearable as I often wake up and have to cough. My doctor has diagnosed me with Achalasia. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Achalasia
 
  Model Output:Pneumatic Balloon Dilation (Endoscopic Treatment)
 
 A balloon is inserted endoscopically and inflated to stretch the lower esophageal sphincter.
 Effective in many cases but may require repeated dilations.
 Risk of esophageal perforation (small but possible).
 Laparoscopic Heller Myotomy (Surgical Treatment)
 
 A minimally invasive surgery where the LES muscle is cut to allow better passage of food.
 Often combined with a fundoplication (partial wrapping of the stomach around the esophagus) to prevent acid reflux.
 Considered the gold standard for long-term symptom relief.
 Peroral Endoscopic Myotomy (POEM)
 
 A newer, minimally invasive endoscopic procedure that cuts the LES internally without external incisions.
 Effective for both classic achalasia and spastic esophageal disorders.
 May have a higher risk of reflux compared to Heller myotomy.
 Botulinum Toxin (Botox) Injection into the LES
 
 Temporarily relaxes the sphincter by blocking nerve signals.
 Less invasive but provides only short-term relief (effects last ~3–6 months).
 Used in patients who are not candidates for surgery.
 Medications (Less Effective, Used for Symptom Management)
 
 Calcium channel blockers (e.g., nifedipine) or nitrates (e.g., isosorbide dinitrate) to relax the LES.
 Generally used when other treatments are not possible.
 Limited effectiveness and can cause side effects like low blood pressure or headaches.

disease_GT: Proximal biceps tendon rupture, more specifically of the long head biceps tendon (LHBT rupture)
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: I am a 45-year-old male. I was lifting a sink onto a truck at work yesterday and suddenly felt a sharp pain in my upper left arm. After that, I could not continue working. Now the pain is still there for more than 12 hours. My doctor has diagnosed me with Proximal biceps tendon rupture, more specifically of the long head biceps tendon (LHBT rupture). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Proximal biceps tendon rupture, more specifically of the long head biceps tendon (LHBT rupture)
 
  Model Output:Conservative Management (Non-Surgical Treatment)
 
 Rest and activity modification (avoiding heavy lifting or overhead activities).
 Ice application (15–20 minutes every few hours for the first few days to reduce swelling).
 Pain management (NSAIDs like ibuprofen or naproxen to reduce inflammation and discomfort).
 Physical therapy (to strengthen surrounding muscles and improve function).
 Recommended for older patients, less physically active individuals, or those who can tolerate mild weakness.
 Physical Therapy and Rehabilitation
 
 Range-of-motion exercises to prevent stiffness.
 Strengthening exercises for compensatory muscles (especially the rotator cuff and remaining short head of the biceps).
 Helps restore function and minimize muscle imbalances.
 Biceps Tenodesis (Surgical Treatment - Recommended for Active Individuals)
 
 The torn long head of the biceps tendon is surgically reattached to the humerus.
 Provides better strength and endurance than non-surgical management.
 Recommended for younger, active patients or those with persistent pain and functional limitations.
 Biceps Tenotomy (Surgical Treatment - Simple Release)
 
 The torn tendon is cut and left unattached, eliminating pain but leading to some loss of biceps strength.
 Less invasive than tenodesis, but may result in the Popeye deformity.
 Suitable for older, less active patients who prioritize pain relief over cosmetic appearance.
 Sling Support and Gradual Return to Activity
 
 Short-term use of a sling (to limit movement and reduce pain in the acute phase).
 Gradual return to daily and work activities, avoiding heavy lifting for at least 4–6 weeks.
 Important to follow a structured rehabilitation plan to avoid complications.

Treatment Task: I am a 60-year-old man and have increasing swelling in the right groin, and since a few days also of the right testicle. At first, the swelling was completely gone when I was lying down and only worsened when I was standing and coughing. In the meantime, however, the swelling is always present. Otherwise, however, I'm fine. My doctor has diagnosed me with Inguinal hernia. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Inguinal hernia
 
  Model Output:1. Surgical Hernia Repair (Definitive Treatment)
 Recommended since your hernia is always present and could worsen over time.
 Two main surgical options:
 Laparoscopic (Minimally Invasive) Hernia Repair
 Small incisions with a camera-assisted procedure.
 Faster recovery, less pain, lower risk of recurrence.
 Preferred for bilateral hernias or recurrent cases.
 Open Hernia Repair (Hernioplasty or Herniorrhaphy)
 Larger incision with direct repair and mesh reinforcement.
 Suitable for larger or complicated hernias.
 2. Watchful Waiting (For Mild, Asymptomatic Cases)
 Not ideal in your case since the swelling is persistent.
 Can be considered if the hernia is small and causes minimal discomfort.
 Requires regular monitoring for signs of worsening (e.g., pain, strangulation).
 3. Hernia Truss (Supportive Belt)
 Temporary relief option before surgery.
 Helps reduce discomfort by applying pressure to keep the hernia in place.
 Not a permanent solution and does not prevent complications.
 4. Lifestyle Modifications (To Prevent Worsening)
 Avoid heavy lifting and straining.
 Maintain a healthy weight to reduce abdominal pressure.
 Treat chronic cough or constipation, which can worsen the hernia.
 Strengthen core muscles with gentle, supervised exercises.
 5. Emergency Surgery (If Complications Occur)
 If symptoms worsen suddenly (e.g., severe pain, redness, nausea, vomiting).
 Signs of strangulation (loss of blood supply to the herniated tissue) require immediate intervention.
 Incarceration (hernia cannot be pushed back) also requires urgent surgery.

Treatment Task: I am a 32 year old woman and today I had my attitude examination. It revealed high blood pressure with values of 180/100 and blood sugar 220. In addition, I have been suffering from fatigue and muscle weakness for a year. In addition, I have gained 10 kg since a few months. My doctor has diagnosed me with Cushing's Syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cushing's Syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. Surgical Removal of the Tumor (First-Line Therapy if Tumor is Present)
 Adrenal Tumor (Adrenalectomy)
 If caused by an adrenal adenoma or carcinoma, the affected adrenal gland is surgically removed.
 Pituitary Tumor (Transsphenoidal Surgery)
 If caused by a pituitary adenoma (Cushing’s disease), the tumor is removed via a minimally invasive approach through the nose.
 Ectopic ACTH-Secreting Tumor (Lung, Pancreas, etc.)
 Requires surgery to remove the tumor if localized.
 2. Gradual Reduction of Corticosteroid Use (If Cushing's is Steroid-Induced)
 If caused by long-term steroid use, a gradual tapering of the medication is required to avoid adrenal insufficiency.
 Alternative treatments for the underlying condition (e.g., non-steroidal immunosuppressants for autoimmune diseases) may be needed.
 3. Medications to Lower Cortisol Levels (If Surgery is Not Possible or While Waiting for Surgery)
 Ketoconazole, Metyrapone, or Mitotane (inhibit cortisol production in adrenal glands).
 Pasireotide (Somatostatin Analog) (blocks ACTH production from pituitary tumors).
 Mifepristone (for severe Cushing’s with diabetes, blocks cortisol effects but does not lower levels).
 4. Radiation Therapy (For Persistent Pituitary Tumors)
 Used if surgery fails or is not an option.
 Stereotactic radiotherapy (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife) may help control ACTH secretion in pituitary tumors.
 Effects take months to years, requiring medical management in the meantime.
 5. Lifestyle and Symptom Management
 Blood pressure control (e.g., ACE inhibitors, calcium channel blockers).
 Diabetes management (metformin or insulin if needed).
 Weight management (diet, exercise, and muscle-strengthening activities).
 Bone health (Vitamin D, calcium supplements to prevent osteoporosis).

Treatment Task: I am a 42 year old male and have been having headaches for about 6 months. In addition, I suffer from sweating and heart palpitations again and again. My doctor has diagnosed me with Pheochromocytoma or catecholamine-secreting paraganglioma. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Pheochromocytoma or catecholamine-secreting paraganglioma
 
  Model Output:1. Surgical Removal of the Tumor (First-Line Treatment)
 Laparoscopic adrenalectomy (for pheochromocytoma in the adrenal gland) or resection of paraganglioma.
 Curative in most cases but requires careful preoperative management.
 Open surgery may be necessary for large or invasive tumors.
 2. Preoperative Alpha-Blockade (To Prevent Surgery Complications)
 Essential before surgery to prevent dangerous blood pressure spikes when handling the tumor.
 Alpha-blockers (e.g., Phenoxybenzamine or Doxazosin) for at least 7–14 days before surgery.
 High-salt diet and IV fluids to expand blood volume after lowering blood pressure.
 3. Beta-Blockers (Only After Alpha-Blockade)
 Used to control tachycardia (fast heart rate) and palpitations but only after alpha-blockers have been given.
 Common options: Propranolol, Metoprolol, or Atenolol.
 Beta-blockers alone are dangerous (can worsen high blood pressure if given before alpha-blockers).
 4. Medical Therapy for Inoperable or Metastatic Cases
 Tyrosine hydroxylase inhibitors (Metyrosine) reduce catecholamine production.
 Chemotherapy (e.g., Cyclophosphamide, Vincristine, Dacarbazine - CVD regimen) for malignant cases.
 Targeted therapy (e.g., Sunitinib) for advanced disease.
 5. 131I-MIBG Therapy (For Metastatic Disease)
 Used if the tumor is malignant or inoperable, and it absorbs MIBG (metaiodobenzylguanidine).
 Helps reduce tumor size and catecholamine secretion.

Treatment Task: I (67-year-old man) have increasing pain in my left hip joint when walking and the resulting limited mobility. Especially in the morning, I have a lot of difficulty walking, sometimes I limp because of it. I have not had an accident. My doctor has diagnosed me with Hip osteoarthritis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hip osteoarthritis
 
  Model Output:1. Physical Therapy and Exercise (First-Line Non-Surgical Treatment)
 Strengthening exercises for hip stabilizers (e.g., gluteal and quadriceps muscles).
 Range-of-motion exercises to maintain flexibility.
 Aquatic therapy (water exercises reduce joint stress).
 Assistive devices (e.g., cane or walker to reduce weight-bearing stress).
 2. Pain Management and Anti-Inflammatory Medication
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Naproxen, Diclofenac) to reduce pain and inflammation.
 Acetaminophen (Paracetamol) for mild pain.
 Topical pain relief (e.g., Capsaicin cream or NSAID gels).
 In severe cases: Stronger pain relievers (e.g., tramadol) if NSAIDs are insufficient.
 3. Intra-Articular Injections (For Moderate-to-Severe Cases)
 Corticosteroid injections (temporary pain relief but limited long-term benefit).
 Hyaluronic acid injections (lubricates the joint but evidence is mixed).
 Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) or stem cell therapy (experimental but promising for early-stage OA).
 4. Weight Management and Lifestyle Modifications
 Losing excess weight reduces stress on the hip joint.
 Low-impact activities (e.g., swimming, cycling instead of high-impact exercises).
 Ergonomic modifications (e.g., using a raised toilet seat, cushioned shoes).
 5. Total Hip Replacement (Hip Arthroplasty) (For Severe OA)
 Recommended if pain is severe and mobility is significantly impaired.
 Types:
 Total hip replacement (THR) (most effective for long-term relief).
 Hip resurfacing (for younger, active patients).
 Highly successful, but requires rehabilitation after surgery.

Treatment Task: I am a 22 year old woman and have been having severe labor-like pain in my lower right abdomen for the past 3 hours. My last period was only a week ago, but I have a slightly elevated temperature of 37.7 degrees Celcius. My doctor has diagnosed me with Acute pelvic inflammatory disease (PID). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)
 
  Model Output:1. Broad-Spectrum Antibiotic Therapy (First-Line Treatment)
 Empirical antibiotic treatment should start immediately before lab results are available.
 Outpatient treatment:
 Ceftriaxone (IM or IV) + Doxycycline (oral) + Metronidazole (oral) for mild to moderate PID.
 Hospitalization for severe cases or complications (e.g., abscess, sepsis):
 IV Cefotetan/Cefoxitin + Doxycycline OR Clindamycin + Gentamicin.
 2. Pain Management and Anti-Inflammatory Medications
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Naproxen) for pain relief and to reduce inflammation.
 Acetaminophen (Paracetamol) if NSAIDs are contraindicated.
 Adequate hydration and rest.
 3. Hospitalization (For Severe Cases)
 Criteria for admission:
 Severe pain, high fever, vomiting, or inability to tolerate oral antibiotics.
 Suspected tubo-ovarian abscess.
 Pregnant women.
 IV antibiotic therapy and close monitoring in hospitalized patients.
 4. Partner Treatment and Sexual Abstinence
 Sexual partners should be tested and treated (if applicable, especially for Chlamydia or Gonorrhea).
 No sexual activity until treatment is completed and symptoms have resolved.
 Consider screening for other STIs (HIV, Syphilis, HPV, etc.).
 5. Surgical Intervention (If Needed)
 If there is a tubo-ovarian abscess or suspected rupture, laparoscopy or drainage may be required.
 In extreme cases, removal of the affected organ (salpingectomy or hysterectomy) may be necessary.

Treatment Task: I am a 28 year old woman and have been suffering from extremely unpleasant smelling vaginal discharge for about a week. In addition, there is also a burning and itching of the vagina. My boyfriend also has discomfort when urinating. My doctor has diagnosed me with Bacterial vaginosis, (vaginal) trichomoniasis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bacterial vaginosis, (vaginal) trichomoniasis

 For trichomoniasis:

 3. Partner Treatment (Essential for Trichomoniasis)
 Your boyfriend also needs treatment, even if his symptoms are mild, to prevent reinfection.
 Metronidazole 2 g in a single dose is the usual treatment for male partners.
 Abstain from sexual activity until both partners have completed treatment and symptoms have resolved.
 4. Probiotic Therapy and Vaginal pH Restoration

 Boric acid suppositories (in resistant BV cases, but not for trichomoniasis).
 5. Preventive Measures to Reduce Recurrence
 Avoid douching and scented vaginal products (can disrupt normal flora).
 Use condoms consistently to prevent reinfection.
 Regular STI screenings if sexually active with multiple partners.

Treatment Task: I am a 22 year old woman and I am overweight. My last period was about 4 months ago. However, my cycle has always been quite irregular. I also have increasing hair growth on my upper lip and cheeks. I had a laparoscopic appendectomy 2 years earlier, during which a biopsy was taken from the ovary. The findings at that time said: thickened tunica albuginea, hyperplasia of theca interna, thickened cortical stroma, multiple subcapsular follicles of different stages of maturation. My doctor has diagnosed me with Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS)
 
  Model Output:1. Combined Oral Contraceptives (COCs) (First-Line Treatment for Irregular Cycles and Androgen Excess)
 Birth control pills (e.g., Ethinyl estradiol + Cyproterone acetate, Drospirenone, or Desogestrel) regulate cycles and reduce excessive hair growth.

 2. Anti-Androgen Therapy (For Excess Hair Growth and Acne)
 Spironolactone (50–100 mg/day) blocks androgen receptors and reduces unwanted hair growth.
 Finasteride (5-alpha reductase inhibitor) may be used if spironolactone is not tolerated.
 Takes several months to show results; should be combined with contraception (to avoid fetal abnormalities if pregnancy occurs).
 3. Insulin-Sensitizing Agents (For Metabolic and Hormonal Balance)
 Metformin (500–2000 mg/day) improves insulin sensitivity and may help regulate cycles.
 Helps with weight loss and reduces diabetes risk (often beneficial for overweight patients).
 Can be used alone or in combination with lifestyle changes.
 4. Lifestyle Modifications (Key for Weight Management and Hormonal Balance)
 Weight loss (even 5–10% reduction) significantly improves cycle regularity and symptoms.
 Low-carb, high-fiber diet and regular exercise (e.g., strength training, cardio) help reduce insulin resistance.
 Intermittent fasting or Mediterranean diet may be beneficial.
 5. Ovulation Induction (For Fertility Treatment, If Desired)
 Clomiphene citrate or Letrozole (first-line medications for inducing ovulation).
 Gonadotropin injections if oral medications do not work.
 Lifestyle changes often improve natural ovulation.

Treatment Task: I am a 49 year old woman and have hot flashes several times a day. When this happens, you can see a reddening of my skin, which usually starts in the head and neck area and then spreads across my chest and back to my arms. My heart beats faster and I have beads of sweat on my forehead. I can no longer concentrate at work. I have no previous illnesses and my periods are regular, although they are now 35 days long.  Estrogen-Progestin Therapy (EPT): For women with a uterus (protects against endometrial hyperplasia).

 Reduces hot flashes, night sweats, and improves cognitive function.
 Should be used at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration (typically <5 years).
 2. Non-Hormonal Medications (For Women Who Cannot or Prefer Not to Take HRT)
 Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) (e.g., Paroxetine, Escitalopram, Venlafaxine): Reduce hot flashes and improve mood.
 Gabapentin (used for nerve pain but also effective for hot flashes).

 3. Lifestyle Modifications (For Mild to Moderate Symptoms)
 Avoid triggers (e.g., caffeine, alcohol, spicy foods).
 Exercise regularly (reduces stress and stabilizes mood).
 Layered clothing (to adjust for sudden temperature changes).
 Mindfulness, yoga, or relaxation techniques (help manage symptoms).
 4. Phytoestrogens and Herbal Supplements (Mild Symptom Relief)
 Soy isoflavones (found in soy products, mimic weak estrogen effects).

 Red clover, flaxseed, and evening primrose oil (may offer mild symptom relief).
 5. Cognitive and Sleep Support (For Concentration and Fatigue)
 Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) for managing mood swings and concentration issues.
 Melatonin or sleep aids if sleep is affected.
 Brain-training exercises (crossword puzzles, memory games) to enhance focus.

Treatment Task: My wife has acute stabbing pain in her right lower abdomen. She has pain almost all the time, e.g. during sexual intercourse, during bowel movements, even two days before she gets her period. When we met, it was not like that. And we don't get pregnant either, even though she stopped taking the pill 2 years ago. My doctor has diagnosed me with Endometriosis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.

  Model Output:1. Hormonal Therapy (First-Line Treatment for Pain Relief)

 Can be taken continuously to stop periods.
 Progestin-Only Therapy (e.g., Dienogest, Norethindrone)
 Thins the endometrial lining and reduces lesion growth.
 Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone (GnRH) Agonists (e.g., Leuprolide, Nafarelin)

 2. Laparoscopic Surgery (Gold Standard for Diagnosis and Treatment)
 Removes visible endometriotic lesions and adhesions to relieve pain.
 Improves fertility by restoring normal anatomy.
 Recommended if symptoms persist despite medication, or if infertility is a major concern.
 3. Pain Management (For Symptom Relief)
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Naproxen, Diclofenac) to reduce inflammation and pain.
 Acetaminophen (Paracetamol) for mild pain.
 Nerve pain medications (e.g., Gabapentin, Amitriptyline) in chronic cases.
 4. Fertility Treatment (If Pregnancy Is the Goal)
 If pregnancy does not occur naturally after laparoscopic surgery:
 Ovulation induction with Letrozole or Clomiphene (if ovulation is irregular).
 Intrauterine insemination (IUI) for mild cases.
 In vitro fertilization (IVF) for severe endometriosis-related infertility.
 5. Lifestyle Modifications and Complementary Therapies
 Regular exercise (helps reduce inflammation and improves pain tolerance).
 Dietary changes (anti-inflammatory diet: less dairy, red meat, and processed foods).
 Pelvic physical therapy (helps with painful intercourse and muscle tension).
 Acupuncture, mindfulness, and relaxation techniques for additional pain relief.

Treatment Task: I am a 25-year-old woman and I have unbearable itching and burning in the vagina and vaginal entrance. In addition, there is increased discharge. This is pretty much the first time. My doctor has diagnosed me with Vulvovaginal candidiasis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.

 2. Pain and Itch Relief (Symptomatic Treatment)
 Cold compresses or sitz baths (soaking in lukewarm water with baking soda or oatmeal).

 Avoid scented soaps, douching, and tight clothing to reduce irritation.
 3. Probiotics and Vaginal Flora Support (Prevention and Faster Recovery)

 4. Avoidance of Irritants and Hygiene Adjustments

 Avoid using scented pads, tampons, or feminine washes.
 Wipe front to back after using the toilet to prevent reinfection.
 5. Medical Follow-Up if Symptoms Persist
 If symptoms do not improve within 7 days or recur frequently, you may need further evaluation.
 Chronic or recurrent infections (≥4 episodes/year) may require long-term antifungal therapy (e.g., Fluconazole weekly for 6 months).

Treatment Task: I am a 55-year-old woman and I feel that my clothes no longer fit me at the stomach. It certainly can't be from eating, since I have hardly any appetite lately. I also have constipation all the time, sometimes the stool is dark-blackish. In addition, there is a non-specific feeling of pressure in the lower abdomen, but no pain. My doctor has diagnosed me with (Advanced stage) Ovarian cancer. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: (Advanced stage) Ovarian cancer
 
  Model Output:1. Cytoreductive Surgery (Debulking Surgery)
 The goal is to remove as much of the tumor as possible, including affected ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus, and any other involved structures.
 If complete removal is not feasible, partial debulking can still improve the effectiveness of subsequent therapies.
 2. Platinum-Based Chemotherapy (Carboplatin + Paclitaxel)
 This is the standard first-line chemotherapy for advanced ovarian cancer.
 Usually administered intravenously in cycles over several months.
 Can be given before surgery (neoadjuvant chemotherapy) to shrink tumors or after surgery (adjuvant chemotherapy) to eliminate remaining cancer cells.
 3. Targeted Therapy (PARP Inhibitors, Bevacizumab)
 PARP inhibitors (e.g., Olaparib, Niraparib, Rucaparib) are used in patients with BRCA mutations or homologous recombination deficiency (HRD).
 Bevacizumab (anti-angiogenic therapy) is a monoclonal antibody that inhibits tumor blood vessel growth and is often combined with chemotherapy.
 4. Hormonal Therapy (for Certain Subtypes)
 Used in low-grade serous ovarian cancer or hormone receptor-positive cases.
 Options include Tamoxifen, Letrozole, or Anastrozole to slow tumor growth.
 5. Palliative Care & Symptom Management
 If the cancer is very advanced and curative treatment is no longer possible, symptom management becomes a priority.
 Includes pain control, management of bowel obstruction, nutritional support, and emotional care.

Treatment Task: I am a 20 year old female and have been experiencing acute and crampy lower abdominal pain for about 4 hours. I have slightly elevated temperature with 37.8 degrees Celsius. My last period was 5 days ago. My doctor has diagnosed me with Acute Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)
 
  Model Output:1. Broad-Spectrum Antibiotic Therapy (First-Line Treatment)
 Since PID is often polymicrobial, combination antibiotic therapy is essential.
 Outpatient Treatment (Mild to Moderate PID):
 Ceftriaxone (single intramuscular injection) + Doxycycline (oral, 14 days) ± Metronidazole (if anaerobic coverage needed).
 Inpatient Treatment (Severe PID or complications like tubo-ovarian abscess):
 IV Cefotetan or Cefoxitin + IV or oral Doxycycline.
 2. Pain Management
 NSAIDs such as Ibuprofen or Naproxen to alleviate crampy abdominal pain.
 Acetaminophen can also be used if NSAIDs are contraindicated.
 3. Partner Treatment
 Sexual partners should also be tested and treated for Chlamydia and Gonorrhea, even if asymptomatic, to prevent reinfection.
 4. Abstinence & Follow-Up
 Avoid sexual intercourse until treatment is completed and symptoms have resolved.
 Follow-up in 48–72 hours to assess symptom improvement.
 5. Hospitalization (If Necessary)
 Indications include severe illness, pregnancy, tubo-ovarian abscess, inability to take oral antibiotics, or lack of improvement with outpatient therapy.
 IV antibiotics are required in these cases.

Treatment Task: I am a 46-year-old woman and I have heavy vaginal bleeding. Actually, I have always had my period regularly every 28 days. However, in the last 7 weeks I have not had any bleeding. As a precaution, I took a pregnancy test, but it was negative. This morning I started bleeding, which is much heavier than the normal period. My doctor has diagnosed me with Endometrial hyperplasia, follicular persistence. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Endometrial hyperplasia, follicular persistence
 
  Model Output:1. Progestin Therapy (First-Line)
 Oral Progestins (e.g., Medroxyprogesterone acetate, Norethisterone, or Micronized progesterone)
 Levonorgestrel-Releasing Intrauterine System (LNG-IUS) (Mirena IUD) – provides continuous local progestin delivery
 Depot Medroxyprogesterone Acetate (DMPA) (injection) for longer-term control
 Indication: Progestins counteract estrogen stimulation and promote endometrial shedding and thinning, making this the primary therapy for non-atypical endometrial hyperplasia.
 2. Endometrial Ablation
 Minimally invasive procedure using heat, radiofrequency, or laser to destroy the endometrial lining.
 Indication: Considered for women with completed family planning who have persistent heavy bleeding despite hormonal therapy.
 3. Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone (GnRH) Agonists
 Medications like Leuprolide (Lupron) or Goserelin (Zoladex) suppress ovarian function and reduce estrogen stimulation.
 Indication: Used for severe hyperplasia or when progestin therapy is contraindicated.
 4. Hysteroscopic Polypectomy or Dilation & Curettage (D&C)
 If significant bleeding persists, D&C can remove excessive endometrial tissue to control bleeding and confirm histological diagnosis.
 Indication: Acute management of severe or prolonged bleeding.
 5. Hysterectomy (Definitive Treatment)
 Considered in cases of recurrent hyperplasia, persistent symptoms, or failure of conservative treatments.
 Indication: Typically reserved for women with atypical hyperplasia or when conservative therapies fail.

Treatment Task: I am 35 years old and gave birth to my first child 9 months ago. I have been losing increasingly large amounts of urine for a few weeks. In the beginning, I only lost a drop when I coughed or laughed. Now I lose urine every day, even when climbing stairs or going for a walk. This occurs quite suddenly and is not accompanied by a feeling of urge to urinate. Somehow I also have the feeling that something is bulging out of my vagina, something like a hemisphere. My doctor has diagnosed me with Female stress urinary incontinence. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Female stress urinary incontinence
 
  Model Output:1. Pelvic Floor Muscle Training (PFMT) - First-Line Therapy
 Also known as Kegel exercises, this strengthens the pelvic floor muscles and improves urethral closure.
 Supervised training with a physiotherapist specializing in pelvic floor dysfunction is more effective.
 Biofeedback and electrical stimulation can enhance muscle activation.
 Indication: Recommended as the first-line therapy for mild to moderate SUI, particularly after childbirth.
 2. Pessary
 A vaginal support device that helps lift the bladder and support pelvic organs, reducing both urine leakage and vaginal bulging.
 Fitted by a gynecologist or urogynecologist.
 Indication: Suitable for women with pelvic organ prolapse and stress incontinence who prefer a non-surgical approach.
 3. Urethral Bulking Agents
 Injectable fillers (e.g., dextranomer/hyaluronic acid gel) are injected around the urethra to improve closure.
 Minimally invasive procedure performed in an outpatient setting.
 Indication: Suitable for women who want a temporary improvement without surgery, but results may be short-lived.
 4. Mid-Urethral Sling Surgery (Surgical Option)
 Gold standard surgical treatment for moderate to severe SUI.
 A synthetic mesh sling is placed under the urethra to provide support.
 Indication: Recommended for persistent SUI that does not improve with PFMT.
 5. Colposuspension (Burch Procedure)
 A laparoscopic or open surgical technique that lifts the bladder neck and provides long-term relief.
 No mesh is used, making it an option for women concerned about synthetic materials.
 Indication: An alternative to the mid-urethral sling in women with severe SUI.

disease_GT: Coronary artery disease, stable angina pectoris
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: I am a 49-year-old gardener and I have a feeling of pressure behind my sternum that has been occurring at work for a few days. It then lasts for several minutes and after a short break from work (due to pain) it also disappears again. My doctor has diagnosed me with Coronary artery disease, stable angina pectoris. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Coronary artery disease, stable angina pectoris
 
  Model Output:1. Anti-Anginal Medication (Symptom Control)
 Short-acting Nitroglycerin (Sublingual or Spray) – taken during chest pain episodes for immediate relief.
 Beta-Blockers (e.g., Metoprolol, Bisoprolol) – reduce heart workload and oxygen demand.
 Calcium Channel Blockers (e.g., Amlodipine, Diltiazem) – relax blood vessels, improving oxygen supply.
 Indication: First-line therapy for controlling symptoms.
 2. Antiplatelet Therapy (Prevents Blood Clots)
 Aspirin (Acetylsalicylic Acid, ASA) 75–100 mg/day – prevents clot formation in coronary arteries.
 Clopidogrel (alternative to ASA in case of intolerance).
 Indication: Long-term therapy to reduce heart attack risk.
 3. Lipid-Lowering Therapy (Prevents Plaque Progression)
 Statins (e.g., Atorvastatin, Rosuvastatin) – lower LDL cholesterol and stabilize arterial plaques.
 PCSK9 Inhibitors (e.g., Evolocumab, Alirocumab) – for patients with high cholesterol despite statins.
 Indication: Essential in CAD to prevent worsening of arterial blockage.
 4. Lifestyle Modification & Cardiac Rehabilitation
 Smoking cessation, weight management, and dietary changes (Mediterranean diet).
 Regular physical activity (supervised exercise program).
 Blood pressure control – target <130/80 mmHg.
 Indication: Prevents disease progression and reduces future cardiovascular risk.
 5. Revascularization (If Symptoms Worsen)
 Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI) with Stent Placement – improves blood flow in significantly narrowed arteries.
 Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting (CABG) – for severe, multi-vessel disease.
 Indication: Considered when medications fail to control symptoms.

Treatment Task: I am a 34 year old woman and for the last 4 days I have had redness of the skin on my lower left leg - just above the ankle. The redness is ring-shaped and has been expanding for the last few days. I just came from a mountain vacation, but otherwise have no problems. My doctor has diagnosed me with Lyme disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Lyme disease
 
  Model Output:1. Oral Antibiotic Therapy (First-Line Treatment)
 Doxycycline (100 mg twice daily for 10–21 days) – preferred if no contraindications (not for pregnant women).
 Amoxicillin (500 mg three times daily for 14–21 days) – alternative for pregnant women or those intolerant to doxycycline.
 Cefuroxime axetil (500 mg twice daily for 14–21 days) – another alternative.
 Indication: First-line treatment to eliminate Borrelia burgdorferi and prevent progression.
 2. Pain and Symptom Management
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Naproxen) – help with local inflammation, pain, and swelling.
 Antihistamines (e.g., Cetirizine, Loratadine) – if itching or mild allergic reactions occur.
 3. Monitoring for Systemic Symptoms
 Watch for neurological signs (e.g., facial paralysis, meningitis-like symptoms) or joint pain.
 If symptoms develop, intravenous (IV) antibiotics (e.g., Ceftriaxone) may be needed.
 4. Wound Care & Skin Protection
 Keep the affected area clean and dry to prevent secondary infection.
 Avoid scratching to prevent irritation.
 5. Preventive Measures for Future Tick Bites
 Use insect repellent (DEET, permethrin-treated clothing) in tick-prone areas.
 Perform daily tick checks after outdoor activities.
 Proper tick removal (using tweezers to extract the tick without crushing it).

Treatment Task: I am a 32 year old woman and have had worsening muscle weakness for 3 months. In particular, getting up from sitting and lying down, climbing stairs and lifting my arms is becoming increasingly difficult. There is no significant muscle pain, but there are recurring pains and swellings of the finger and wrist joints. In addition, I notice increased dandruff on the hands and slightly red swollen eyelids. My doctor has diagnosed me with Dermatomyositis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Dermatomyositis
 
  Model Output:1. Corticosteroids (First-Line Treatment)
 Prednisone (1 mg/kg/day, then tapered slowly over months)
 High-dose intravenous steroids (if severe muscle weakness or difficulty swallowing/breathing)
 Indication: Rapidly suppresses immune-mediated muscle and skin inflammation.
 2. Immunosuppressive Therapy (For Steroid-Sparing and Severe Cases)
 Methotrexate (weekly) or Azathioprine – commonly used for long-term disease control.
 Mycophenolate mofetil (CellCept) – useful if lung involvement is present.
 Cyclophosphamide or Rituximab – reserved for severe, refractory cases.
 Indication: Prevents steroid-related side effects and controls disease progression.
 3. Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG)
 IVIG infusions (monthly) for 3–6 months.
 Indication: Used in severe cases, especially if muscle weakness is disabling or resistant to steroids.
 4. Physical Therapy & Rehabilitation
 Gradual, supervised physical therapy to prevent muscle atrophy.
 Occupational therapy for joint function and hand involvement.
 Indication: Maintains mobility, strength, and independence.
 5. Skin & Symptom Management
 Hydroxychloroquine (for skin symptoms, if no muscle involvement).
 Topical corticosteroids or tacrolimus (for skin rash, eyelid swelling, and hand lesions).
 Sunscreen (broad-spectrum UVA/UVB) – prevents disease flares from sun exposure.
 NSAIDs (Ibuprofen, Naproxen) for joint pain/swelling.
 Indication: Essential for managing rash, joint symptoms, and preventing flare-ups.

Treatment Task: I am a 67-year-old man and have pain in my left calf. They occur from a distance of 150m and disappear when I stop. This has been happening for 6 months. At rest I do not notice anything. I have known arterial hypertension and diabetes mellitus type II. I smoke about a pack a day for almost 50 years. My doctor has diagnosed me with Peripheral artery disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Peripheral artery disease
 
  Model Output:1. Smoking Cessation (Most Important Lifestyle Change)
 Complete smoking cessation is the single most effective way to slow disease progression.
 Methods include:
 Nicotine replacement therapy (patches, gums, lozenges)
 Medications (Varenicline, Bupropion)
 Behavioral therapy & support groups
 Indication: Essential to prevent worsening PAD and cardiovascular events.
 2. Antiplatelet Therapy (Prevents Blood Clots)
 Aspirin (75–100 mg daily) or Clopidogrel (75 mg daily)
 Reduces the risk of heart attacks, strokes, and limb-threatening events.
 Indication: Long-term therapy for all PAD patients.
 3. Supervised Exercise Therapy (Improves Walking Distance)
 Structured walking program (at least 3x per week, 30–60 min per session)
 Walk until symptoms appear, then rest, then resume walking (graded approach)
 Indication: First-line treatment to increase pain-free walking distance.
 4. Lipid-Lowering Therapy (Prevents Progression)
 High-intensity statins (e.g., Atorvastatin 40–80 mg or Rosuvastatin 20–40 mg)
 Reduces cholesterol, stabilizes plaques, and lowers cardiovascular risk
 Indication: Mandatory in PAD, especially with diabetes.
 5. Cilostazol or Revascularization (For Severe Symptoms)
 Cilostazol (100 mg twice daily) – improves walking distance but not for heart failure patients.
 Endovascular procedures (angioplasty + stenting) or bypass surgery – if symptoms severely impact daily life despite medical therapy.
 Indication: For patients with disabling claudication or critical limb ischemia.#

disease_GT: Diabetes insipidus (both types possible: central ~ = Arginine vasopressin deficiency, nephrogenic ~ = Arginine vasopressin resistence)
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: I am a 34-year-old woman and have been experiencing increasing thirst for months. I have to drink large amounts of fluid all the time. That's why I often have to urinate a lot during the day and at night. In addition, I sleep worse and become increasingly nervous. My doctor has diagnosed me with Diabetes insipidus (both types possible: central ~ = Arginine vasopressin deficiency, nephrogenic ~ = Arginine vasopressin resistence). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Diabetes insipidus (both types possible: central ~ = Arginine vasopressin deficiency, nephrogenic ~ = Arginine vasopressin resistence)
 
  Model Output:1. Desmopressin (First-Line for Central DI)
 Synthetic vasopressin analog (Desmopressin acetate, DDAVP) compensates for hormone deficiency.
 Available as nasal spray, oral tablets, or injection.
 Dosage: Nasal (10–40 µg/day), Oral (0.1–0.8 mg/day), Injection (1–4 µg/day).
 Indication: Only for central DI, not nephrogenic DI.
 2. Low-Sodium Diet & Thiazide Diuretics (For Nephrogenic DI)
 Reduces urine output by lowering kidney filtration pressure.
 Thiazide diuretics (e.g., Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg/day) cause mild volume contraction, reducing urine production.
 Indication: First-line for nephrogenic DI, often combined with NSAIDs.
 3. Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs, for Nephrogenic DI)
 Indomethacin (25–50 mg 2–3x/day) reduces urine volume by increasing kidney response to vasopressin.
 Indication: Used alongside thiazides for nephrogenic DI.
 4. Adequate Fluid Intake & Electrolyte Monitoring
 Essential for both types to prevent dehydration and hypernatremia.
 Maintain balanced sodium levels to avoid complications.
 Indication: All DI patients, especially when treatment is not fully effective.
 5. Identify & Treat Underlying Causes
 For Central DI: MRI to check for pituitary tumors, brain injury, or autoimmune conditions.
 For Nephrogenic DI: Check for medication-induced DI (e.g., lithium, demeclocycline) and genetic mutations.

Treatment Task: I am 24 years old, male and have had diarrhea for 4 days: up to 10 bowel movements per day. The stool is watery, discolored and unformed. I do not take any medication nor do I have any other pre-existing conditions. My doctor has diagnosed me with Acute diarrhea. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute diarrhea
 
  Model Output:1. Oral Rehydration Therapy (First-Line)
 Oral rehydration solution (ORS): Contains electrolytes and glucose to prevent dehydration.
 Homemade alternative: 1 liter of water + ½ teaspoon salt + 6 teaspoons sugar.
 Indication: Prevents dehydration, especially with frequent watery stools.
 2. Dietary Adjustments (BRAT Diet)
 Bananas, Rice, Applesauce, Toast – easily digestible, helps firm stool.
 Avoid dairy, caffeine, alcohol, fatty, or spicy foods.
 Indication: Helps reduce stool frequency and promotes gut recovery.
 3. Anti-Motility Medications (For Symptom Relief)
 Loperamide (Imodium): Slows bowel movements, reducing diarrhea frequency.
 Indication: Only for non-bloody diarrhea without fever.
 4. Probiotics
 Lactobacillus or Saccharomyces boulardii: Restores gut microbiota balance.
 Indication: Helps shorten diarrhea duration, especially after infections.
 5. Antibiotic Therapy (Only If Indicated)
 Not routinely needed, but may be used if:
 High fever, bloody diarrhea (dysentery), or travel history (possible bacterial cause).
 Ciprofloxacin or Azithromycin (for bacterial causes like Salmonella, Shigella).
 Indication: Only if bacterial infection is suspected.

Treatment Task: My mother is 79 years old, who has had chills, diarrhea, cough and shortness of breath for several hours. She has had a fever for several days, currently around 39.4 degrees Celsius. She regularly takes the tablets Diclofenac, Ramipril and Methotrexate. Because of the fever, she has also been taking daily 3-4 tablets of an antibiotic (cotrimoxazole) from my father's stock for the last few days. My doctor has diagnosed me with Drug-induced agranulocytosis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Drug-induced agranulocytosis
 
  Model Output:1. Immediate Discontinuation of the Causative Drug
 Stop Cotrimoxazole immediately (common cause of agranulocytosis).
 Methotrexate could also contribute and should be reviewed by a doctor.
 Indication: Prevent further suppression of white blood cells.
 2. Broad-Spectrum IV Antibiotics (For Infection Control)
 Empirical IV antibiotics (e.g., Piperacillin-Tazobactam, Meropenem, or Cefepime).
 Addition of Vancomycin if severe infection suspected.
 Indication: High risk of life-threatening bacterial infections due to low neutrophils.
 3. Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor (G-CSF)
 Filgrastim (G-CSF, subcutaneous injection) to accelerate neutrophil recovery.
 Indication: Shortens duration of agranulocytosis and reduces infection risk.
 4. Supportive Therapy (Fluids, Fever Control, Isolation)
 Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) for fever (avoid NSAIDs like Diclofenac).
 IV fluids and electrolytes if dehydration is present.
 Isolation precautions to prevent infections.
 Indication: Supportive care to prevent complications.
 5. Monitoring & Hematology Consultation
 Frequent blood tests (CBC, neutrophil count) to track recovery.
 Bone marrow biopsy if agranulocytosis persists or cause is unclear.
 Indication: Ensures correct diagnosis and guides further treatment.

disease_GT: Nodular Goiter with normal hormone production
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: I am a woman of 33 years. My thyroid gland has slowly increased in size over the past few years. I am more and more prone to nervousness and have gained 4 kg of weight in the past 2 years. My doctor has diagnosed me with Nodular Goiter with normal hormone production. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Nodular Goiter with normal hormone production
 
  Model Output:Watchful Waiting & Regular Monitoring – If the goiter is not causing significant symptoms, your doctor may recommend regular thyroid function tests and ultrasound exams to monitor changes.
 
 Iodine Supplementation – If iodine deficiency is a contributing factor, iodine supplementation (through diet or medication) can help stabilize or reduce goiter growth.
 
 Levothyroxine Therapy (L-T4 Suppression Therapy) – In selected cases, low-dose levothyroxine may be prescribed to suppress TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone), which can slow goiter growth. However, its effectiveness is debated, and it is typically used with caution.
 
 Minimally Invasive Treatments (e.g., Radiofrequency Ablation, Ethanol Injection) – For autonomous or growing nodules, non-surgical treatments like radiofrequency ablation (RFA) or ethanol injection may shrink the nodules without requiring surgery.
 
 Surgery (Thyroidectomy or Partial Thyroidectomy) – If the goiter continues to grow, causes compression symptoms (difficulty swallowing, breathing issues), or has a risk of malignancy, surgical removal of part or all of the thyroid gland may be necessary.

Treatment Task: I am a 23-year-old woman suffering from acute shortness of breath. Since the beginning of April, I have had attacks of shortness of breath, usually accompanied by cough and glassy, viscous sputum. At the same time, I have no fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Apart from hay fever, I have no previous illnesses. My doctor has diagnosed me with Bronchial asthma. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bronchial asthma
 
  Model Output:1. Short-Acting Beta-2 Agonists (SABAs) - Reliever Medication
 Example: Salbutamol (Albuterol) or Terbutaline
 These rescue inhalers work quickly to relax airway muscles and relieve acute shortness of breath.
 Used as needed during asthma attacks.
 2. Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS) - Controller Medication
 Example: Budesonide, Fluticasone, Beclomethasone
 Reduces airway inflammation and prevents future attacks.
 Used daily for long-term asthma control.
 3. Long-Acting Beta-2 Agonists (LABAs) - For Persistent Symptoms
 Example: Formoterol, Salmeterol
 Used in combination with ICS for better long-term control.
 Not used alone (to avoid worsening asthma outcomes).
 4. Leukotriene Receptor Antagonists (LTRAs) - Especially for Allergic Asthma
 Example: Montelukast
 Works well in asthma triggered by allergies (e.g., pollen, dust mites).
 Taken orally, often used as an add-on therapy.
 5. Avoidance of Triggers & Non-Medical Management
 Identify and avoid allergens (e.g., pollen, dust, pet dander).
 Consider antihistamines for hay fever relief.
 Use peak flow monitoring to track lung function.
 Smoking cessation & air pollution control are crucial if applicable.

Treatment Task: I am a 34 year old woman and have been fatigued and tired for months. I have also collapsed several times in the past few weeks. Otherwise I have never been ill and I do not take medication regularly. However, since a short time I have brownish hyperpigmentation, which rather does not come from the sun. My doctor has diagnosed me with Chronic primary adrenal insufficiency. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Chronic primary adrenal insufficiency
 
  Model Output:1. Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy (Cortisol Replacement)
 Example: Hydrocortisone (first choice), Prednisolone, or Dexamethasone
 Compensates for cortisol deficiency to relieve fatigue, low blood pressure, and other symptoms.
 Hydrocortisone is typically taken in divided doses (higher in the morning, lower in the afternoon/evening).
 Dosage adjustment during stress/illness is crucial to prevent adrenal crisis.
 2. Mineralocorticoid Replacement Therapy (Aldosterone Replacement)
 Example: Fludrocortisone
 Essential for sodium and fluid balance, preventing dehydration and low blood pressure.
 Needed in primary adrenal insufficiency, but not in secondary adrenal insufficiency.
 3. Emergency Hydrocortisone Injection (For Adrenal Crisis Prevention)
 Hydrocortisone 100 mg IM or IV for emergencies (e.g., severe illness, surgery, trauma).
 Patients should carry an emergency steroid card or medical alert bracelet.
 Family members should be educated on administering injections if needed.
 4. Sodium & Electrolyte Management
 Increased salt intake may be necessary, especially in hot weather or excessive sweating.
 Regular monitoring of potassium and sodium levels to avoid imbalances.
 5. Education & Lifestyle Adaptations
 Stress dosing: Increasing hydrocortisone during infections, surgeries, or extreme stress.
 Avoiding sudden medication withdrawal to prevent adrenal crisis.
 Routine endocrinologist follow-ups to monitor hormone levels and adjust treatment.
 Bonus: Consideration of DHEA Supplementation (If Needed)
 Some patients benefit from DHEA (Dehydroepiandrosterone) to improve energy, mood, and libido, but it’s not essential for all.

Treatment Task: My father is 78 years old and has been experiencing pain in his left lower abdomen for several days. He also complains of alternating constipation and diarrhea. He also has a fever of 38.7 degrees Celsius. My doctor has diagnosed me with Acute colonic diverticulitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute colonic diverticulitis
 
  Model Output:Antibiotics (if indicated):
 
 For uncomplicated diverticulitis, antibiotics may not always be required, but for febrile or symptomatic cases, a targeted antibiotic course is recommended to cover anaerobic and gram-negative bacteria (e.g., ciprofloxacin and metronidazole, or amoxicillin-clavulanate). Intravenous antibiotics may be required for severe cases or if hospitalization is needed.
 Pain Management:
 
 Analgesics such as acetaminophen (paracetamol) are preferred for pain relief, as NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) may increase the risk of gastrointestinal complications. Avoid opioids if possible due to their potential to worsen constipation.
 Dietary Modifications:
 
 Initial recommendations often include a clear liquid diet during the acute phase to reduce bowel stress. Once symptoms improve, a gradual transition to a low-residue diet may be advised. Eventually, a high-fiber diet can be introduced to prevent future episodes.
 Hospitalization and Supportive Care (if necessary):
 
 Indicated for elderly patients, those with significant fever (>38.5°C), persistent abdominal pain, or other complications. This involves intravenous fluids, bowel rest, and close monitoring for complications such as perforation, abscess, or sepsis.
 Surgical Intervention (if complications occur):
 
 Surgery, such as resection of the affected bowel segment, is typically reserved for cases of recurrent diverticulitis, abscesses, perforations, or fistulae. In severe cases, emergency surgery may be required.

Treatment Task: I am a 36-year-old male and I am having increasing difficulty swallowing. I've had this for a while, both when swallowing solid foods and when drinking. Recently, I have sometimes had to regurgitate food. Nevertheless, I have not lost any weight and I have no other pain. My doctor has diagnosed me with Achalasia. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Achalasia
 
  Model Output:Pneumatic Balloon Dilation:
 
 This involves the use of an endoscopic balloon to forcefully stretch the LES. It is a minimally invasive procedure and is effective for many patients, though repeated dilations may be necessary over time.
 Heller Myotomy (Surgical Treatment):
 
 A laparoscopic procedure where the LES muscle is cut to relieve obstruction. This is often combined with a partial fundoplication to prevent reflux. It is considered highly effective and long-lasting.
 Peroral Endoscopic Myotomy (POEM):
 
 A newer, minimally invasive endoscopic procedure where the inner muscle layer of the LES is divided. POEM is effective, especially for younger patients, and has become an increasingly popular treatment option.
 Botulinum Toxin (Botox) Injection:
 
 Botox is injected into the LES to temporarily paralyze the muscle and relieve symptoms. This is less invasive but typically provides only short-term relief, so it may be better suited for patients who are not surgical candidates.
 Medications (if surgery or invasive procedures are not immediately possible):
 
 Calcium channel blockers (e.g., nifedipine) or nitrates (e.g., isosorbide dinitrate) can reduce LES pressure temporarily. These are generally considered as adjunctive or temporary measures and are less effective than mechanical interventions.

Treatment Task: I (17 years old girl) have been suffering from high fever, sweating and joint pain for several days. It all started 10 days ago with a sore throat. In addition, my knee joints, ankles and wrists are swollen and red. I have a fever of 39.5 degrees Celsius and strange spots and nodules on my forearm. My doctor has diagnosed me with Acute rheumatic fever. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute rheumatic fever
 
  Model Output:Antibiotic Therapy (Penicillin or Alternative Antibiotics)
 
 Purpose: Eradicate the underlying group A Streptococcus (GAS) infection to prevent recurrence.
 Common Choice: Benzathine Penicillin G (IM single dose) or oral Penicillin V for 10 days.
 Alternative (if allergic to Penicillin): Azithromycin, Clindamycin, or Cephalosporins.
 Anti-inflammatory Therapy (NSAIDs or Corticosteroids if severe)
