 Purpose: Reduce joint pain, swelling, and inflammation.
 Common Choice: Aspirin (high-dose) or Naproxen.
 Corticosteroids (e.g., Prednisolone): If severe carditis or no response to NSAIDs.
 Supportive Therapy for Heart Involvement (if present)
 
 Purpose: Manage rheumatic heart disease if carditis is present.
 Diuretics (e.g., Furosemide) or ACE inhibitors (e.g., Enalapril) for heart failure symptoms.
 Bed Rest and Symptom Management
 
 Purpose: Prevent complications, especially if carditis is present.
 Hydration, fever control (Paracetamol), and adequate nutrition.
 Secondary Prevention (Long-term Penicillin Prophylaxis)
 
 Purpose: Prevent recurrent GAS infections and future heart damage.
 Benzathine Penicillin G (IM injection every 3-4 weeks) for at least 5 years (or lifelong if severe heart disease develops).

Treatment Task: I am a woman of 24 years. I have been having cramping pain in my lower right abdomen for weeks and recurrent non-bloody diarrhea, as well as a decrease in my body weight by 6 kg. I have already had my appendix and tonsils removed. I have no fever, but I have a fistula at my anus and swelling on my right knee joint. My doctor has diagnosed me with Crohn disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Crohn disease
 
  Model Output:1. Biologic Therapy (TNF-alpha Inhibitors)
 Indicated for: Moderate to severe Crohn’s disease, fistulizing disease, and arthritis
 Examples:
 Infliximab (Remicade)
 Adalimumab (Humira)
 Certolizumab (Cimzia)
 These monoclonal antibodies reduce inflammation and promote fistula healing, making them a cornerstone of treatment for perianal Crohn’s disease.
 2. Immunomodulators
 Indicated for: Maintenance therapy in moderate Crohn’s disease or in combination with biologics to prevent antibody formation
 Examples:
 Azathioprine (Imuran)
 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP)
 Methotrexate (for steroid-sparing effect and arthritis treatment)
 These drugs suppress the immune system and help in long-term remission.
 3. Antibiotic Therapy (for Perianal Disease and Fistulas)
 Indicated for: Perianal Crohn’s disease and fistulizing disease
 Examples:
 Metronidazole
 Ciprofloxacin
 Used to reduce bacterial load and control secondary infections in perianal Crohn’s disease.
 4. Corticosteroids (for Induction Therapy in Flares, Not Long-term Use)
 Indicated for: Active Crohn’s flares, especially if severe symptoms are present
 Examples:
 Budesonide (Entocort, less systemic side effects, good for ileal Crohn’s)
 Prednisone (short-term use for flares, not maintenance)
 These reduce inflammation quickly but are not for long-term use due to side effects.
 5. Surgical Treatment (for Refractory Disease or Complications)
 Indicated for: Persistent fistulas, strictures, or disease not responding to medical therapy
 Procedures include:
 Seton placement (for perianal fistulas)
 Resection of affected bowel (only in severe cases)
 Strictureplasty (if strictures develop)
 Surgery is not first-line therapy but may be required if complications arise.

Treatment Task: I am a 40-year-old man and have been physically fatigued for about 6 months. In addition, I have a dry cough and night sweats. In addition, I have been suffering from a chronic cold with frequent nosebleeds and a very crusty nose for 9 months. For about 3 weeks now, I have also been suffering from swelling of the right knee joint, the left wrist and both ankles. My doctor has diagnosed me with Granulomatosis with polyangiitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis
 
  Model Output:1. Immunosuppressive Therapy (Induction & Maintenance)
 Purpose: Suppress the immune system to stop vasculitis and prevent organ damage.
 Induction therapy (to control the disease initially):
 Rituximab (anti-CD20 antibody, preferred)
 Cyclophosphamide (if severe organ involvement, e.g., kidney or lungs)
 Maintenance therapy (to prevent relapse):
 Azathioprine, Methotrexate, or Rituximab (low-dose, periodic infusion)
 2. Corticosteroids (High-dose Prednisolone)
 Purpose: Quickly reduce inflammation and symptoms.
 Dosing:
 High-dose Prednisolone (1 mg/kg/day) for induction
 Gradual tapering over months to minimize side effects.
 3. Plasma Exchange (Plasmapheresis) – If Severe Disease
 Purpose: Remove harmful autoantibodies (used in rapidly progressing cases, especially with kidney involvement).
 Indicated for:
 Severe glomerulonephritis with rapidly declining kidney function.
 Severe lung hemorrhage.
 4. Symptom Management & Supportive Therapy
 Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX, Co-trimoxazole)
 May help prevent relapses, especially in respiratory forms.
 Nasal Care (Saline rinses, topical steroids, antibiotic ointments like Mupirocin)
 To manage crusty nose & chronic sinusitis.
 Joint pain management (Acetaminophen, physiotherapy)
 Avoid NSAIDs if kidney function is impaired.
 5. Long-term Monitoring & Preventive Measures
 Regular ANCA testing, kidney function tests, and lung imaging
 Osteoporosis prevention (Calcium, Vitamin D, and bisphosphonates if on long-term steroids)
 Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) prophylaxis if on immunosuppressants (e.g., TMP-SMX).

Treatment Task: I am a 21 year old woman with headache. If I were to describe it: existing for 5 hours, left-sided, of pulsating character with a moderate nausea. I have headaches more often, however, I have had similar symptoms 2 times a year since I was 17. Before the onset of the headache today I saw 20 minutes of wandering flashes of light. After about 30 minutes that was gone and the pain started. 1 tablet of aspirin only gave mild relief. The onset of visual and speech disturbance is a new dimension for me. My doctor has diagnosed me with Migraine with aura. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Migraine with aura
 
  Model Output:Acute (Abortive) Therapy – To Stop Ongoing Migraine
 Triptans (e.g., Sumatriptan, Rizatriptan, Zolmitriptan)
 
 Purpose: Quickly relieve migraine pain when taken at onset.
 Note: Should be avoided if you have vascular risk factors.
 NSAIDs or Combination Analgesics (e.g., Ibuprofen, Naproxen, or Aspirin with Caffeine & Paracetamol)
 
 Purpose: Reduce pain and inflammation.
 Example: Aspirin 900 mg or Ibuprofen 400–600 mg.
 Antiemetics (e.g., Metoclopramide or Domperidone)
 
 Purpose: Treat nausea and improve pain medication absorption.
 Preventive Therapy – If Attacks Are Frequent or Severe
 Beta-Blockers (e.g., Propranolol, Metoprolol) or CGRP Monoclonal Antibodies (e.g., Erenumab)
 
 Purpose: Reduce attack frequency if you have >3 attacks/month.
 Magnesium, Riboflavin (Vitamin B2), or Anticonvulsants (e.g., Topiramate)
 
 Purpose: Natural and pharmacological prevention.
 Additional Recommendations:
 Avoid Triggers: Lack of sleep, dehydration, stress, certain foods (chocolate, alcohol).
 Lifestyle Changes: Regular sleep schedule, hydration, and exercise.
 Hormonal Consideration: If you take hormonal contraceptives, discuss risks (increased stroke risk with migraine aura).

Treatment Task: My 69-year-old husband has had memory problems for about 1 year. He has increasing difficulty in retaining new information for more than a few minutes, while he remembers things from earlier times well. Thus, he often correctly recounts his school days. On the other hand, 6 months earlier he did not find his car again after a shopping trip in the city center and had to ask the police to search for it. Otherwise, my husband behaves as he always has, is friendly and courteous in his dealings with me, family and friends. A distant relative who visited us recently did not notice anything about him at first, but my husband did not recognize her at first. There are actually no pre-existing conditions and medication or alcohol abuse does not apply. My doctor has diagnosed me with Alzheimer disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Alzheimer disease
 
  Model Output:1. Cholinesterase Inhibitors (Donepezil, Rivastigmine, Galantamine)
 These medications increase acetylcholine levels in the brain, improving memory and cognitive function.
 Typically used in mild to moderate Alzheimer’s disease.
 Side effects: Nausea, diarrhea, and loss of appetite.
 2. NMDA Receptor Antagonist (Memantine)
 Protects brain cells from damage caused by excess glutamate.
 Used in moderate to severe Alzheimer’s disease.
 Can be combined with cholinesterase inhibitors for better effect.
 3. Cognitive Training & Behavioral Therapy
 Helps reinforce memory, problem-solving skills, and daily activities.
 Structured activities, memory exercises, and reality orientation therapy can slow decline.
 Cognitive stimulation therapy (CST) in group settings is particularly beneficial.
 4. Lifestyle Interventions (Exercise, Diet, and Social Engagement)
 Regular exercise (e.g., walking, swimming, or strength training) improves brain function and slows cognitive decline.
 Mediterranean diet (rich in vegetables, fish, nuts, and olive oil) may have neuroprotective effects.
 Social interaction helps maintain mental abilities.
 5. Caregiver Support and Structured Routine
 Establish daily routines to reduce confusion.
 Use reminders, labeled objects, and calendars to aid memory.
 Involve family or professional caregivers to ensure safety and emotional well-being.

Treatment Task: My husband is 65 years old and has been suffering increasingly from memory loss for half a year. In addition, he is often sleepy during the day, can rarely concentrate and he has little desire for his hobbies. 3 years ago he had a slight stroke, but the symptoms are gone except for slight walking problems. It could now be another stroke. My doctor has diagnosed me with Vascular dementia, vascular cognitive impairment. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Vascular dementia, vascular cognitive impairment
 
  Model Output:1. Stroke Prevention & Vascular Risk Management
 Blood Pressure Control: ACE inhibitors, calcium channel blockers, or beta-blockers to maintain BP below 130/80 mmHg.
 Cholesterol Management: Statins (e.g., atorvastatin) to reduce vascular risk.
 Blood Thinners: Aspirin, clopidogrel, or anticoagulants if needed to prevent further strokes.
 Diabetes Control: Tight blood sugar regulation if applicable.
 2. Cognitive Enhancing Medications
 Cholinesterase Inhibitors: Donepezil, Rivastigmine, or Galantamine (sometimes used in vascular dementia, especially if mixed with Alzheimer’s).
 Memantine: May help with cognitive function in moderate-to-severe cases.
 3. Physical & Cognitive Rehabilitation
 Regular Exercise (30–45 min/day): Improves blood flow to the brain and slows cognitive decline.
 Physiotherapy: Helps improve mobility and reduce walking problems.
 Speech or Occupational Therapy: If needed, for cognitive function and daily living activities.
 4. Lifestyle & Nutrition Modifications
 Mediterranean Diet: Rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, fish, and olive oil to support brain health.
 Cognitive Stimulation Therapy (CST): Activities like puzzles, memory exercises, and group discussions.
 Adequate Sleep & Sleep Apnea Screening: Daytime sleepiness may be linked to poor sleep or sleep apnea, which should be treated.
 5. Structured Daily Routine & Caregiver Support
 Consistent Schedule: Helps reduce confusion and improve function.
 Use of Memory Aids: Clocks, notes, reminders, and labeled objects.
 Caregiver Support & Supervision: To assist with tasks and monitor for further strokes.

Treatment Task: My brother is a 44-year-old engineer and has been suffering from increasing movement abnormalities for 3 years. For example, there are always spontaneous movements in all 4 extremities, especially hands and feet. Sometimes there are also twitches in his face. My brother is also becoming increasingly depressed, but this could also be due to his unemployment. His marriage also broke up a year ago. In addition, his alcohol consumption is significantly increased. He does not admit to drinking much alcohol or to the strange gestures and movements that occur. Overall, he is more often irritable. When he argues, these striking movements occur more often. Also his speech sometimes seems a bit blurred. My doctor has diagnosed me with Huntington disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Huntington disease
 
  Model Output:1. Symptomatic Drug Therapy
 For Chorea (Involuntary Movements)
 
 Tetrabenazine (Xenazine) or Deutetrabenazine (Austedo): Reduce excessive movements by depleting dopamine.
 Neuroleptics (e.g., Risperidone, Olanzapine): Can also help reduce chorea and irritability.
 For Psychiatric Symptoms (Depression, Irritability)
 
 Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs, e.g., Sertraline, Fluoxetine): Help with depression and mood stabilization.
 Atypical Antipsychotics (Quetiapine, Olanzapine): Help manage severe irritability and aggression.
 2. Physiotherapy & Occupational Therapy
 Movement Training & Coordination Exercises: Helps slow down motor decline.
 Assistive Devices (e.g., weighted utensils, gait supports): Helps maintain independence in daily activities.
 3. Speech Therapy
 Speech & Swallowing Therapy: Helps with slurred speech and difficulty swallowing that may occur later in the disease.
 Communication Strategies: Using alternative means of expression if speech deteriorates.
 4. Lifestyle Adjustments & Nutritional Support
 Balanced Diet & Caloric Intake: HD patients often lose weight unintentionally due to excessive movements.
 Avoid Alcohol: Since alcohol worsens HD symptoms, reducing or quitting drinking is crucial.
 Regular Exercise: Can help with mobility, mood, and cognitive function.
 5. Psychological & Social Support
 Psychotherapy & Counseling: Helps manage depression and emotional distress.
 Support Groups for Huntington’s Disease: Provides emotional and practical coping strategies.
 Family & Caregiver Training: Educates loved ones on how to assist with daily challenges.

disease_GT: Myotonic dystrophy Type 2 (DM2), formerly proximal myotonic myopathy (PROMM)
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: I am a 37 year old woman and have had slightly increasing weakness in my shoulder and gluteal muscles for 10 years. I have also been suffering from muscle soreness for 5 years. In addition, my muscles are stiff especially in the winter months. However, except for these muscle problems, I am healthy. My close relatives do not have these problems. My doctor has diagnosed me with Myotonic dystrophy Type 2 (DM2), formerly proximal myotonic myopathy (PROMM). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Myotonic dystrophy Type 2 (DM2), formerly proximal myotonic myopathy (PROMM)
 
  Model Output:1. Physiotherapy & Exercise Therapy
 Strength & Endurance Training: Light resistance training can help maintain muscle function without overexertion.
 Stretching & Flexibility Exercises: Helps reduce muscle stiffness and improve mobility, especially in winter.
 Hydrotherapy (Water-based therapy): Warm water exercises can ease muscle stiffness and soreness.
 2. Pain & Myotonia Management (Muscle Stiffness)
 Mexiletine (or Alternative Sodium Channel Blockers like Lamotrigine, Ranolazine): Can help reduce muscle stiffness and cramping.
 Magnesium Supplements: Some patients report relief from stiffness.
 NSAIDs (e.g., Ibuprofen, Naproxen): For muscle soreness or inflammation.
 3. Occupational Therapy & Assistive Devices
 Helps with daily activities if muscle weakness progresses.
 Ergonomic tools & adaptive devices (e.g., cushioned seating, walking aids) can assist in reducing strain on weak muscles.
 4. Lifestyle & Nutritional Support
 Vitamin D & Calcium Supplementation: Helps maintain bone health, as DM2 patients are at risk for osteoporosis.
 Avoid Prolonged Inactivity: Regular movement prevents further muscle atrophy.
 Cold Weather Precautions: Layering up in winter or using warming devices can reduce stiffness.
 5. Multidisciplinary Monitoring & Support
 Regular Cardiac Checkups: Some DM2 patients can develop cardiac conduction defects, so an ECG should be done periodically.
 Neurologist Follow-ups: To monitor disease progression and adjust treatments.
 Pain Management Specialist: If muscle pain becomes severe

Treatment Task: I am a 34 year old male and have been having unilateral pain behind my right eye every night for weeks. This one wakes me up almost always at the same time, around 1:00 am. It feels like a knife behind the eye, so severe that the eye is watery and all red. When I get up and walking around in my appartment, it is better after about 1 hour. Eye and ENT doctors have not found anything. My dentist has also not been able to get any improvement with a root canal. I have also tried to take aspirin or diclofenac against the pain, but unfortunately neither brings anything. Apart from that, I am a smoker and have higher blood pressure more often. My doctor has diagnosed me with Cluster headache. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Cluster headache
 
  Model Output:1. Acute Attack Treatment (Fast Relief)
 High-Flow Oxygen Therapy (100% Oxygen at 12–15 L/min via Non-Rebreather Mask)
 
 The first-line treatment for cluster headache attacks.
 Works within 15 minutes for most patients.
 Needs to be administered immediately when the attack begins.
 Sumatriptan (Subcutaneous Injection 6 mg)
 
 Fast-acting and effective within 10–15 minutes.
 Alternative: Zolmitriptan nasal spray (useful if injections are not available).
 2. Preventive Therapy (To Reduce Attack Frequency)
 Verapamil (First-line Preventive)
 
 A calcium channel blocker that helps prevent attacks.
 Starting dose: 240 mg/day (can be increased under medical supervision).
 Requires ECG monitoring due to potential heart rhythm effects.
 Alternative Preventives:
 
 Lithium (in chronic cluster headaches).
 Topiramate or corticosteroids (for short-term prevention).
 3. Corticosteroids (For Cluster Headache Breakthrough)
 Prednisone (60–100 mg/day for a short period, then taper)
 Can temporarily stop cluster cycles.
 Used while waiting for other preventive medications (like verapamil) to take effect.
 4. Lifestyle & Behavioral Adjustments
 Avoid Triggers
 
 Nicotine & Alcohol: Known to trigger attacks, so smoking cessation is recommended.
 Irregular Sleep Patterns: Maintaining a consistent sleep schedule may help.
 Cold vs. Warm Applications
 
 Cold packs on the head may help in some cases.
 5. Neuromodulation (For Chronic or Drug-Resistant Cases)
 Sphenopalatine Ganglion Stimulation or Vagus Nerve Stimulation
 Used when medications do not work.
 Involves electrical stimulation of specific nerves linked to headache pathways.

Treatment Task: I am a 55-year-old saleswoman and have been suffering from muscle pain for 8 weeks. In addition, I have had increasing weakness in my extremities for 2 weeks. I also find it increasingly difficult to swallow. Lately I have also had joint pains, but they are better now. I have always been healthy and do not take any medication. I also drink alcohol very responsibly, but smoke about 30 cigarettes a day. My doctor has diagnosed me with Polymyositis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Polymyositis
 
  Model Output:I am a 55-year-old saleswoman and have been suffering from muscle pain for 8 weeks. In addition, I have had increasing weakness in my extremities for 2 weeks. I also find it increasingly difficult to swallow. Lately I have also had joint pains, but they are better now. I have always been healthy and do not take any medication. I also drink alcohol very responsibly, but smoke about 30 cigarettes a day. My doctor has diagnosed me with Polymyositis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.

Treatment Task: I am a 45-year-old woman and have been seeing double vision for a few days. Although only slight and are not always present, but still disturbing. Especially when driving a car. Then I usually just close my eyes. I also keep noticing a feeling of pressure behind my left eye. My doctor has diagnosed me with Third cranial nerve (oculomotor nerve) palsy. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Third cranial nerve (oculomotor nerve) palsy
 
  Model Output:Identify and Treat the Underlying Cause
 
 Urgent brain imaging (MRI/MRA or CT angiography) is essential to rule out an aneurysm, which is a medical emergency.
 If due to microvascular disease (e.g., diabetes, hypertension), blood sugar and blood pressure control are crucial.
 Prism Glasses or Eye Patch for Double Vision
 
 Temporary prism lenses can help align vision if recovery is expected.
 An eye patch over one eye can reduce the discomfort from double vision while healing.
 Corticosteroids (if Inflammatory Cause)
 
 If the palsy is caused by inflammation (e.g., vasculitis, Tolosa-Hunt syndrome), a course of steroids may reduce swelling and improve nerve function.
 Botulinum Toxin Injections (if Persistent Double Vision)
 
 If the nerve does not recover fully, Botox injections into overactive muscles can improve alignment and reduce double vision.
 Surgical Correction (if Long-Term Deficits)
 
 If the nerve palsy does not improve after 6-12 months, strabismus surgery may be needed to realign the eye.

Treatment Task: My 56-year-old wife had an epileptic seizure for the first time. Her hand twitched involuntarily, then the whole right arm shook. This happened while she was fully conscious. Somehow she has also changed a bit in the last weeks. She has been complaining of constant headaches for about 1 month, which she fights with many painkillers. Maybe the pills are to blame that she behaves so strangely. Somehow she also moves slower. My doctor has diagnosed me with Glioblastoma. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Glioblastoma
 
  Model Output:Glioblastoma (GBM) is an aggressive brain tumor that requires a multimodal treatment approach. Given your wife's symptoms—focal seizures, persistent headaches, behavioral changes, and slowed movements—prompt treatment is crucial. The five most appropriate therapies include:
 
 Surgical Resection (Maximal Safe Tumor Removal)
 
 The first and most critical step is surgery to remove as much of the tumor as possible without causing significant neurological deficits.
 Even partial resection can relieve pressure and improve symptoms.
 Radiotherapy (Fractionated or Stereotactic)
 
 Standard treatment after surgery to slow tumor progression.
 Typically delivered as fractionated radiotherapy (6 weeks, 5 days a week).
 Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) may be considered for smaller residual tumors.
 Chemotherapy (Temozolomide, TMZ)
 
 Temozolomide (TMZ) is the standard chemotherapy drug used with radiation.
 Increases survival when combined with radiation therapy.
 If the tumor is MGMT promoter-methylated, TMZ is particularly effective.
 Anti-Seizure Medication
 
 Since she has experienced focal seizures, anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) like levetiracetam (Keppra) may be prescribed.
 Helps prevent further seizures and improves quality of life.
 Supportive & Palliative Care
 
 Dexamethasone (steroids) to reduce brain swelling and improve symptoms like headaches and weakness.
 Pain management and psychological support for symptom relief.
 Physical therapy to maintain mobility.
 Important Next Steps
 A biopsy or surgical resection is crucial to confirm the diagnosis.
 Molecular testing (e.g., MGMT methylation, IDH mutation) helps guide therapy.
 Consider a neuro-oncology center for the best treatment options.
 Clinical trials may provide access to new experimental treatments.

disease_GT: Giant cell arteritis/temporal arteritis, Horton disease
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: I am 73 years old and female. I have had headaches for about 4 weeks. Since yesterday, however, I have had visual disturbances for the first time. Maybe the visual disturbances are side effects of the tablets. My doctor has diagnosed me with Giant cell arteritis/temporal arteritis, Horton disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Giant cell arteritis/temporal arteritis, Horton disease
 
  Model Output:High-Dose Corticosteroids (Prednisone or Methylprednisolone) – Immediate Treatment
 
 First-line therapy to prevent vision loss.
 Oral prednisone (40–60 mg/day) is started immediately, even before biopsy confirmation.
 If vision loss is already present, IV methylprednisolone (1000 mg/day for 3 days) may be given.
 Gradual tapering over months to prevent relapse.
 Tocilizumab (IL-6 Inhibitor) – Steroid-Sparing Therapy
 
 Used if long-term steroids cause side effects.
 Reduces relapse risk and allows faster tapering of steroids.
 Administered as weekly subcutaneous injections.
 Low-Dose Aspirin (100 mg/day) – Prevents Stroke & Vision Loss
 
 GCA increases the risk of stroke and blindness due to vessel inflammation.
 Aspirin may reduce vascular complications.
 Used cautiously if no contraindications (e.g., stomach ulcers, bleeding risk).
 Calcium and Vitamin D – Bone Protection
 
 Long-term steroid therapy increases osteoporosis risk.
 Calcium (1000 mg/day) + Vitamin D (800 IU/day) recommended.
 Bisphosphonates (e.g., Alendronate) may be needed if osteoporosis risk is high.
 Regular Monitoring & Follow-Up
 
 Frequent blood tests (ESR, CRP) to monitor inflammation.
 Ophthalmologic check-ups to assess vision risk.
 Gradual steroid tapering under supervision to prevent relapse.

Treatment Task: I am a 37 year old woman and have had a mild gait disorder for a few days. However, I also have problems with urination. And when I have to, I have to significantly more urgently. I have also missed a few drops in the past. My doctor has diagnosed me with Neuromyelitis optica (spectrum disorders). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Neuromyelitis optica (spectrum disorders)
 
  Model Output:1. High-Dose Corticosteroids (First-Line Therapy)
 IV methylprednisolone (1000 mg/day for 3–5 days) to reduce inflammation.
 Oral prednisone may be used for gradual tapering after IV therapy.
 This is crucial to halt progression and prevent worsening symptoms.
 2. Plasma Exchange (PLEX) – If No Response to Steroids
 Used if severe or steroid-resistant cases.
 Removes harmful autoantibodies from the blood.
 Typically 5–7 sessions over 2 weeks.
 3. Immunosuppressive Therapy (Long-Term Prevention)
 To prevent future attacks, since NMOSD relapses can cause irreversible damage.
 Common options:
 Rituximab (anti-CD20 therapy) – Most commonly used.
 Eculizumab (complement inhibitor) – FDA-approved for NMOSD.
 Azathioprine or Mycophenolate Mofetil – Alternative for long-term suppression.
 4. Symptomatic Treatment for Urinary Problems & Gait Issues
 Bladder dysfunction:
 Anticholinergics (e.g., oxybutynin, solifenacin) for urgency/incontinence.
 Catheterization if urinary retention is severe.
 Gait disorder & weakness:
 Physiotherapy & rehabilitation to maintain mobility.
 Spasticity management (e.g., baclofen, tizanidine) if stiffness develops.
 5. Vitamin D & Supportive Care
 Vitamin D supplementation to support immune health.
 Pain management (neuropathic pain is common).
 Mental health support, as NMOSD can be emotionally challenging.

Treatment Task: My 6-year-old son suddenly developed a fever of 39 to 40 degrees yesterday, which cannot be lowered by calf compresses and paracetamol. In addition, he has severe headaches and neck pain and has already vomited several times. My doctor has diagnosed me with Bacterial meningitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Bacterial meningitis
 
  Model Output:1. Immediate IV Antibiotic Therapy (Lifesaving Priority)
 Empiric IV antibiotics must be started immediately before waiting for test results.
 First-line antibiotics for children (6 years old):
 Ceftriaxone (or Cefotaxime) + Vancomycin (for Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, H. influenzae).
 If Listeria is suspected (rare in children but possible if immunocompromised), Ampicillin is added.
 Treatment duration: Usually 7–14 days, depending on the pathogen.
 2. Corticosteroids (Dexamethasone) – Reduces Brain Swelling & Hearing Loss Risk
 Given before or with the first dose of antibiotics to reduce inflammation.
 Helps lower the risk of neurological complications and hearing loss.
 Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 2–4 days is commonly used.
 3. Intensive Supportive Care in Hospital (ICU if Severe)
 IV Fluids to prevent dehydration from fever and vomiting.
 Fever Management: Acetaminophen (Paracetamol) or Ibuprofen.
 Oxygen or Ventilation Support if breathing is affected.
 Seizure control: If seizures occur, benzodiazepines (e.g., Lorazepam) may be needed.
 4. Lumbar Puncture & Blood Tests for Diagnosis & Monitoring
 CSF Analysis (Lumbar Puncture) to confirm bacteria, measure glucose & protein levels.
 Blood Cultures to identify the specific bacteria causing the infection.
 Monitoring of inflammatory markers (CRP, procalcitonin) to track treatment response.
 5. Long-Term Monitoring & Prevention (After Recovery)
 Hearing Tests: Meningitis can cause permanent hearing loss (audiological screening is needed post-recovery).
 Neurological Follow-Up: To check for developmental or cognitive issues.
 Vaccination:
 Pneumococcal vaccine (PCV13, PPSV23) – prevents Streptococcus pneumoniae meningitis.
 Meningococcal vaccine (MenACWY, MenB) – prevents Neisseria meningitidis meningitis.
 Hib vaccine (if not received in infancy) – prevents Haemophilus influenzae type B meningitis.

Treatment Task: My 3 year old daughter is convulsing like she is having an epileptic seizure. It lasted about 3 minutes. My daughter seems tired, but otherwise responds adequately. She appears to have a fever of 39.8 degrees Celsius. This is the first time we have seen anything like this in her. The fever keeps going up despite calf wraps and acetaminophen suppositories. My doctor has diagnosed me with Febrile seizure. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Febrile seizure
 
  Model Output:1. Fever Management (Antipyretics)
 Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) or Ibuprofen
 Helps reduce discomfort but does not prevent future seizures.
 Paracetamol: 15 mg/kg every 4–6 hours (max 60 mg/kg/day).
 Ibuprofen: 10 mg/kg every 6–8 hours (max 40 mg/kg/day).
 Avoid aspirin due to risk of Reye’s syndrome.
 2. Monitoring and Supportive Care
 Observe your child closely after the seizure.
 If she remains alert and responsive after the seizure, hospitalization is usually not necessary.
 Ensure hydration (fluids, electrolyte solutions).
 Let her rest after the seizure.
 3. Emergency Management for Prolonged or Recurrent Seizures
 If a seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, give a benzodiazepine rescue medication (if prescribed by a doctor).
 First-line options for prolonged seizures:
 Diazepam rectal gel (0.5 mg/kg)
 Midazolam buccal/oral (0.2 mg/kg)
 Seek emergency medical attention if:
 Seizure lasts longer than 5 minutes.
 Another seizure occurs within 24 hours.
 Child remains unresponsive after the seizure.
 4. Identifying and Treating the Underlying Cause of Fever
 Common causes: Viral infections, ear infections, strep throat, pneumonia.
 Doctor may prescribe antibiotics if a bacterial infection is suspected.
 Hydration and rest help speed recovery.
 5. Education & Prevention of Future Febrile Seizures
 Febrile seizures are usually harmless and do not indicate epilepsy.
 Cooling methods:
 Light clothing, lukewarm baths (no cold water!).
 Avoid excessive wrapping in blankets.
 Discuss with your pediatrician if febrile seizures recur.

Treatment Task: My 10-year-old boy has pain and swelling on his right knee. He does not have a fever. Last week he complained of joint and muscle pain, but always in different locations. We thought it might be growing pains. I also noticed an annular rash that spread and then subsided. There is an additional small redness and swelling on the earlobe. My doctor has diagnosed me with Lyme disease. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Lyme disease
 
  Model Output:1. Oral Antibiotic Therapy (First-Line Treatment)
 Amoxicillin (For children under 8)
 50 mg/kg per day (divided into 3 doses) for 14–21 days.
 Doxycycline (For children over 8)
 4 mg/kg per day (max 200 mg) for 14–21 days.
 Preferred if there is neurological involvement (e.g., facial palsy).
 Cefuroxime (Alternative)
 30 mg/kg per day (max 1000 mg) for 14–21 days.
 This treatment effectively eliminates the infection in most cases.
 2. NSAIDs for Pain and Swelling
 Lyme arthritis can cause significant joint inflammation.
 Ibuprofen (10 mg/kg every 6–8 hours) helps reduce pain and swelling.
 Avoid corticosteroids, as they can worsen Lyme-related symptoms.
 3. Monitoring and Follow-Up
 Symptoms may take weeks to fully resolve, even after antibiotics.
 Regular joint examinations to track improvement.
 If arthritis persists after antibiotic treatment, longer therapy (up to 28 days) or IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone) may be needed.
 4. Treatment of Additional Skin Lesions (Ear Swelling)
 Borrelial lymphocytoma (swelling/redness on the earlobe) also responds to antibiotics.
 Usually resolves within weeks after starting treatment.
 5. Preventing Future Tick Bites
 Wear long sleeves and pants in tick-infested areas.
 Use insect repellent (DEET or permethrin-treated clothing).
 Perform daily tick checks, especially behind the ears, scalp, and joints.
 Remove ticks promptly with fine-tipped tweezers.

Treatment Task: My 15-year-old daughter has been increasingly suffering from sore throat, difficulty swallowing, fatigue and exhaustion as well as fever between 39 and 40 degrees Celsius for several days. She has a feeling of pressure especially in the right upper abdomen. Yesterday she also had a fine- to medium-spotted skin rash, but it has now faded again. My doctor has diagnosed me with Infectious mononucleosis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Infectious mononucleosis
 
  Model Output:1. Symptomatic Treatment (Pain and Fever Management)
 Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) or Ibuprofen for fever and sore throat.
 Avoid aspirin (due to the risk of Reye’s syndrome).
 2. Hydration and Rest
 Encourage plenty of fluids (water, herbal tea, soups) to prevent dehydration.
 Strict physical rest is crucial, especially to avoid spleen rupture (a rare but serious complication).
 3. Avoidance of Contact Sports and Strenuous Activity
 The spleen may be enlarged and at risk of rupture, which can be life-threatening.
 No contact sports or intense physical activity for at least 3–4 weeks, or until a doctor confirms spleen size has returned to normal.
 4. Throat and Swallowing Relief
 Saltwater gargles or lozenges to soothe the throat.
 Cold foods (like ice cream or popsicles) can help relieve discomfort.
 Soft diet to reduce swallowing pain.
 5. Monitoring for Complications
 Watch for worsening symptoms, such as:
 Severe abdominal pain (possible spleen rupture).
 Difficulty breathing or swallowing (severe throat swelling).
 Persistent high fever (>10 days) or extreme weakness.

Treatment Task: My 10 year old daughter has had a cold and cough for a week. She was actually feeling better until she suddenly developed a high fever. In addition, she now complains of severe frontal headaches, which are particularly bad whenever she tilts her head forward. My doctor has diagnosed me with Acute sinusitis and rhinosinusitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute sinusitis and rhinosinusitis
 
  Model Output:1. Antibiotic Therapy (If Bacterial Sinusitis is Confirmed)
 Indicated if symptoms worsen after initial improvement ("double sickening") or last >10 days with severe symptoms.
 First-line treatment:
 Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Augmentin): 45–90 mg/kg/day in two doses for 10–14 days.
 If allergic to penicillin: Cefuroxime, Clindamycin, or Levofloxacin.
 Avoid unnecessary antibiotics if symptoms suggest a mild viral infection.
 2. Pain and Fever Management
 Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) or Ibuprofen for headache, facial pain, and fever.
 Ibuprofen (10 mg/kg every 6–8 hours) is especially effective for inflammation.
 3. Nasal Irrigation & Decongestants
 Saline nasal rinses (e.g., with a neti pot or saline spray) help clear mucus and reduce sinus pressure.
 Decongestant nasal sprays (Oxymetazoline or Xylometazoline)
 Used for no more than 3 days to avoid rebound congestion.
 Steam inhalation (with warm water or essential oils like eucalyptus) can help open sinuses.
 4. Rest, Hydration, and Humidified Air
 Ensure plenty of fluids to thin mucus and support recovery.
 Use a humidifier in the room to keep the air moist.
 Encourage rest and elevated sleeping position to ease congestion.
 5. Monitoring for Complications
 🚨 Seek urgent medical care if:
 
 Severe worsening headache, swelling around the eyes, or vision changes (possible orbital cellulitis).
 Persistent high fever >39°C for more than 3 days despite antibiotics.
 Neck stiffness or neurological symptoms (could indicate meningitis).

Treatment Task: My 14-year-old daughter has lost 16kg of weight within the last year. Her body weight is only 40kg at a height of 160cm. She started her diet because she was called a "fat boy" at school. Her friends all have model figures. Even now she still feels too fat. She keeps withdrawing and the girl no longer participates in meals together, preferring to prepare her own healthy low-calorie food (e.g., half an apple or 1 carrot for breakfast, 1 lean yogurt for lunch, salad for dinner). I suspect my daughter is secretly sticking her finger down her throat. She also exercises several times a day on the family exercise bike. It is no longer possible to have a conversation, everything the daughter understands as an attack on her healthy lifestyle. My doctor has diagnosed me with Anorexia nervosa. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Anorexia nervosa
 
  Model Output:1. Multidisciplinary Treatment (Medical, Psychological, and Nutritional Care)
 A team approach with pediatricians, psychiatrists, psychologists, and dietitians is essential.
 Medical monitoring: Regular check-ups for malnutrition, heart issues (bradycardia), and electrolyte imbalances.
 Psychological therapy: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Eating Disorders (CBT-E) is the most effective treatment.
 2. Nutritional Rehabilitation & Supervised Meal Plans
 Gradual weight restoration with a structured meal plan.
 Supervised, family-based therapy (FBT, Maudsley Approach) encourages healthy eating at home with parental guidance.
 Avoid extreme refeeding (risk of refeeding syndrome).
 3. Psychotherapy (CBT-E & Family-Based Therapy)
 CBT-E (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Eating Disorders)
 Helps correct distorted body image and unhealthy eating behaviors.
 Family-Based Therapy (FBT/Maudsley Method)
 Involves parents in meal planning and encourages a supportive home environment.
 4. Addressing Excessive Exercise and Purging Behavior
 Limit excessive exercise (set clear restrictions).
 Medical evaluation for purging behaviors (vomiting can cause dangerous electrolyte imbalances and heart issues).
 Psychological support for compulsive exercise (e.g., anxiety-driven movement).
 5. Monitoring and Possible Hospitalization (If Severe Malnutrition)
 🚨 Urgent hospitalization is needed if:
 
 BMI drops dangerously low.
 Heart rate is below 50 bpm (bradycardia).
 Severe dehydration, fainting, or electrolyte imbalances.
 Ongoing refusal to eat or rapid weight loss.

Treatment Task: My 10-year-old son has upper abdominal pain, nausea and loss of appetite. He feels listless and tired, has a slight fever (38.4 degrees Celsius). In addition, he has yellow eyes. 3 weeks ago we were still in Turkey visiting relatives. My doctor has diagnosed me with Hepatitis a. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hepatitis a
 
  Model Output:Rest and Hydration – Encourage your son to rest and drink plenty of fluids (water, herbal teas, electrolyte solutions) to prevent dehydration, especially if he has nausea or vomiting.
 
 Nutritional Support – Offer small, frequent meals that are easy to digest (e.g., soups, rice, fruits) while avoiding fatty, fried, or heavy foods that can burden the liver.
 
 Symptom Management – Paracetamol (acetaminophen) should be avoided or used cautiously, as it can strain the liver. If fever or pain is present, discuss safer alternatives with a doctor.
 
 Avoid Alcohol and Medications That Affect the Liver – Although your son is unlikely to consume alcohol, ensure he avoids unnecessary medications, especially those metabolized by the liver (e.g., certain antibiotics, NSAIDs like ibuprofen).
 
 Good Hygiene and Isolation Precautions – Since Hepatitis A is highly contagious, ensure proper handwashing and avoid sharing personal items (e.g., cups, utensils) to prevent spreading to other family members.

Treatment Task: My 4-year-old daughter suffers increasingly from facial swelling in the last few days. In particular, the eyelids are really swollen shut in the morning after waking up. My doctor has diagnosed me with Nephrotic syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Nephrotic syndrome
 
  Model Output:1. Corticosteroids (First-line Treatment)
 Prednisolone or Prednisone is the standard treatment to reduce protein loss in urine and control inflammation.
 The usual course involves daily steroids for several weeks, followed by a gradual taper.
 Regular monitoring for side effects (e.g., weight gain, mood changes, infections) is necessary.
 2. Salt and Fluid Restriction
 Reducing salt (sodium) intake helps manage swelling.
 Fluid intake may need to be adjusted if there is severe edema.
 Encourage fresh fruits, vegetables, and home-cooked meals while avoiding processed foods, fast food, and salty snacks.
 3. Diuretics (If Severe Edema)
 Furosemide (Lasix) may be prescribed if swelling is severe, but it must be used cautiously under medical supervision to avoid dehydration and electrolyte imbalances.
 Potassium supplements might be needed if diuretics are used.
 4. Albumin Infusions (If Severe Protein Loss)
 In cases of severe hypoalbuminemia (low blood albumin levels), an intravenous albumin infusion can help pull fluid back into the bloodstream, reducing swelling.
 5. Preventing Infections and Complications
 Nephrotic syndrome weakens the immune system, so vaccinations (like pneumococcal and flu vaccines) may be recommended.
 Monitor for signs of infections (fever, abdominal pain) and seek medical help promptly.
 Blood pressure and kidney function monitoring are essential.

Treatment Task: Our 4-year-old son has been suffering from abdominal pain, fever (39 degrees Celsius) and vomiting since the night before (more than 12 hours). The fever does not go down despite calf compress and paracetamol. The stomach pain is getting worse. Our son is otherwise not sensitive at all and is hardly ever sick. My doctor has diagnosed me with Acute appendicitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Acute appendicitis
 
  Model Output:1. Emergency Surgery (Appendectomy)
 Laparoscopic appendectomy (minimally invasive) or open appendectomy is the gold standard treatment to remove the inflamed appendix.
 Surgery is needed as soon as possible to prevent rupture (perforation), which can cause peritonitis (a life-threatening abdominal infection).
 2. Intravenous (IV) Antibiotics
 Pre- and post-operative antibiotics are given to reduce the risk of infection.
 If the appendix has already perforated, stronger or longer antibiotic treatment may be necessary.
 Common antibiotics used: Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole.
 3. Pain Management
 Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) or Ibuprofen can be used to relieve pain, but stronger IV pain medications (e.g., morphine) may be needed before or after surgery.
 Pain that persists despite medication may indicate a worsening condition.
 4. IV Fluids and Electrolyte Management
 Vomiting and fever can lead to dehydration, so IV fluids are given in the hospital.
 Electrolytes (sodium, potassium) are monitored and balanced as needed.
 5. Postoperative Care and Recovery
 Most children recover quickly after surgery, typically discharged within 1–3 days if there are no complications.
 Activity restrictions for a few weeks (avoid running, jumping).
 Soft diet for a few days, then normal food as tolerated.
 When to Seek Urgent Care?
 Severe pain spreading across the abdomen (possible rupture).
 High fever (>39.5°C) or worsening vomiting (signs of peritonitis).
 Distended belly, no stool/gas passing (signs of bowel obstruction).
 Lethargy, difficulty waking up (sepsis risk).

Treatment Task: My 10-year-old son has had sudden visual disturbances for about 1 hour with eye flickering and numbness first in his right arm, then in his right leg. In addition, he speaks somewhat slurred. This has subsided, but instead he now complains of a severe left-sided headache with nausea. He finds bright light unpleasant. We have never noticed anything like this in him before. He used to have febrile convulsions as a toddler, but otherwise he grew up normally. My doctor has diagnosed me with Migraine with aura. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Migraine with aura
 
  Model Output:Acute (Abortive) Therapies
 Pain Relief with Ibuprofen or Acetaminophen (Paracetamol)
 
 First-line treatment for mild to moderate migraine attacks.
 Best taken early in the attack for maximum effectiveness.
 Triptans (e.g., Sumatriptan, Rizatriptan) – for Moderate to Severe Attacks
 
 Approved for use in children (Sumatriptan nasal spray, Rizatriptan orally).
 Can help stop the migraine early if taken at the onset of headache.
 Supportive Measures
 Rest in a Dark, Quiet Room
 Helps reduce symptoms, especially during aura and headache phases.
 Minimize screen exposure and bright lights.
 Preventive Strategies (if Migraines Become Frequent)
 Lifestyle Modifications
 
 Ensure regular sleep, hydration, and meals (avoid skipping meals).
 Reduce stress and screen time.
 Identify and avoid triggers (e.g., certain foods, dehydration, lack of sleep).
 Preventive Medications (if Migraines Are Frequent or Severe)
 
 Topiramate or Propranolol may be considered if migraines occur frequently and impact daily life.
 Magnesium or riboflavin (Vitamin B2) supplements might also help reduce attack frequency.

disease_GT: Diabetes mellitus type one, insulindependent diabetes mellitus
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: My son is 8 years old, he has been having stomach pain and nausea for 2 days. Last night he vomited several times. He does not have diarrhea or fever. However, he is sleepy. The sleepiness along with grogginess has been going on for almost 2 weeks. He has also lost 3kg in that time, although he has been eating and drinking with appetite. Our son has to go to the toilet all the time and for 2 days he even wet the bed again. My doctor has diagnosed me with Diabetes mellitus type one, insulindependent diabetes mellitus. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Diabetes mellitus type one, insulindependent diabetes mellitus
 
  Model Output:1. Insulin Therapy (Essential)
 Basal (long-acting) insulin (e.g., Glargine, Detemir) – Given once daily to maintain steady glucose levels.
 Bolus (rapid-acting) insulin (e.g., Lispro, Aspart) – Given before meals to control blood sugar spikes.
 Insulin pump may be an option for long-term management.
 2. Intravenous (IV) Fluids & Electrolyte Management (If DKA)
 If Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) is present (grogginess, vomiting, dehydration), immediate hospitalization is needed.
 IV fluids (saline) are given to correct dehydration.
 Electrolytes (potassium, sodium) must be monitored and balanced.
 3. Blood Glucose Monitoring & Ketone Testing
 Frequent glucose checks (before meals, bedtime, and as needed).
 Ketone testing (urine or blood) to monitor for ketoacidosis risk.
 Target glucose range: 4–8 mmol/L (70–140 mg/dL), adjusted by doctor.
 4. Nutrition & Carbohydrate Counting
 Balanced meals with controlled carbohydrates to prevent high sugar spikes.
 Carbohydrate counting is crucial for insulin dosing.
 Avoid sugary foods/drinks, but ensure steady glucose intake to prevent hypoglycemia.
 5. Diabetes Education & Long-Term Care
 Training on hypoglycemia symptoms (shakiness, sweating, confusion) and emergency treatment (glucose tablets, glucagon injection if unconscious).
 Learning insulin administration and adjusting doses based on activity levels.
 Follow-up with a pediatric endocrinologist for long-term diabetes care.

Treatment Task: My 3-year-old daughter has woken up from her sleep with a barking cough. She has difficulty breathing. Even if she doesn't exert herself, you can hear a whistling sound when she breathes in. I am afraid that my child might suffocate. She has no fever. My doctor has diagnosed me with Croup syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Croup syndrome
 
  Model Output:Calm and Comfort Your Child
 
 Panic can worsen symptoms. Keep your child calm and sit them upright.
 Humidified or Cool Air Therapy
 
 Take your child outside into cool night air or let them breathe in humidified air (e.g., from a steamy bathroom).
 Corticosteroids (e.g., Dexamethasone or Prednisolone)
 
 A single oral dose of Dexamethasone (0.15–0.6 mg/kg) is highly effective in reducing airway swelling.
 Alternatively, Prednisolone can be used but is less preferred.
 Nebulized Epinephrine (for Severe Cases)
 
 If your child has significant difficulty breathing, nebulized Racemic Epinephrine (0.05 mL/kg) or L-Epinephrine (1:1000, 5 mL) can reduce swelling in the airway rapidly.
 This requires medical supervision.
 Seek Emergency Medical Care if Symptoms Worsen
 
 If your child struggles to breathe, becomes lethargic, or their lips turn blue, immediate medical attention (call emergency services or go to the hospital) is necessary.

Treatment Task: My 12-month-old son has been crying continuously for 2 hours and simply cannot be calmed down. When I changed his diaper, I noticed that his scrotum is red and his right testicle is very swollen. He has no fever. My doctor has diagnosed me with Testicular torsion. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Testicular torsion
 
  Model Output:Immediate Emergency Evaluation
 
 Go to the emergency room (ER) immediately or call emergency services.
 Ultrasound with Doppler may be used to confirm the diagnosis, but if torsion is strongly suspected, immediate surgery is prioritized.
 Urgent Surgical Detorsion (Orchiopexy)
 
 Surgical detorsion is the only definitive treatment.
 The twisted testicle is untwisted and fixed (orchiopexy) to the scrotum to prevent recurrence.
 The opposite testicle is also fixed as a preventive measure.
 Manual Detorsion (Only in Certain Cases)
 
 In some cases, a doctor may attempt to manually untwist the testicle while waiting for surgery.
 However, surgery is still required even if manual detorsion provides temporary relief.
 Pain Management
 
 Intravenous (IV) or oral pain medications (e.g., ibuprofen or acetaminophen) may be given before and after surgery.
 Testicle Removal (Orchiectomy, if Necessary)
 
 If the testicle has been deprived of blood for too long (typically >6 hours), it may be nonviable and require removal.

Treatment Task: My 5-month-old son and I have only been living in Germany for a few months. We are originally from Bulgaria. My son has had an increasingly severe cough for a few weeks now. He is almost constantly tired and listless, drinks badly and vomits when he coughs. At night he is often soaked with sweat. My doctor has diagnosed me with Tuberculosis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Tuberculosis
 
  Model Output:Immediate Anti-Tuberculosis Therapy (ATT) with First-Line Medications
 
 The standard treatment consists of a combination of multiple antibiotics over 6 months:
 Intensive phase (first 2 months):
 Isoniazid (INH)
 Rifampicin (RIF)
 Pyrazinamide (PZA)
 Ethambutol (EMB) (sometimes used depending on severity and resistance risk)
 Continuation phase (next 4 months):
 Isoniazid (INH) + Rifampicin (RIF)
 Nutritional and Supportive Therapy
 
 TB can cause failure to thrive in infants.
 Adequate nutrition and hydration are essential.
 Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine) is often given with Isoniazid to prevent nerve damage.
 Hospitalization (if severe symptoms or complications)
 
 If your baby has severe respiratory distress, dehydration, or signs of TB meningitis, hospital admission is required for close monitoring.
 Contact Screening and Preventive Therapy for Family Members
 
 Since TB is contagious, all close contacts (including you) should be tested with a tuberculin skin test (TST) or IGRA (Interferon Gamma Release Assay) and a chest X-ray (for adults).
 If you or any household member has latent TB, preventive therapy (e.g., Isoniazid for 6-9 months) may be needed.
 BCG Vaccination (for Unvaccinated Infants in High-Risk Areas)
 
 If your child has not yet been vaccinated with BCG, it may still be considered in some cases, but not if active TB is already diagnosed.
 Key Takeaway:
 Your child needs urgent medical treatment and long-term monitoring.
 Strict adherence to the medication regimen is critical to prevent resistance.
 All close contacts should be screened and treated if necessary.
 Seek specialized pediatric TB care in a hospital or TB center.

Treatment Task: I am a 55-year-old woman. Unfortunately, I have not been able to sleep through the night for about 2 years. I am very tired during the day and have hardly been able to concentrate on my work at the office in recent months. I have been to several general practioneers who have prescribed me various sleeping pills and tranquillizers. However, after taking them for a longer time, they have made things worse rather than better. The whole situation is such a burden that I recently went to a psychiatrist who diagnosed me with “depressive syndrome” and started me on pills again. But even that has made things worse rather than better. In the meantime, I no longer sleep in the same bed as my husband. He feels disturbed by the strange, sweeping leg movements I make. I wake up more and more often at night. Then I feel a tingling sensation in my legs. If I massage them, it gets better, but as the night progresses, I become increasingly restless and have to walk around the room. I have noticed that this urge to move always occurs when I sit for long periods, especially towards the evening. My doctor has diagnosed me with Restless legs syndrome. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Restless legs syndrome
 
  Model Output:Most Appropriate Therapies for Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS):
 Iron Supplementation (if Ferritin < 75 µg/L)
 
 Iron deficiency is a common trigger for RLS, even if you do not have anemia.
 Check your ferritin levels (a storage form of iron in the body).
 If ferritin is low, iron supplements (oral or intravenous) can help.
 Dopamine Agonists (if Symptoms Are Severe)
 
 Medications like Pramipexole, Ropinirole, or Rotigotine (patch) can reduce RLS symptoms.
 However, long-term use can lead to augmentation (worsening over time), so they should be used cautiously.
 Alpha-2-Delta Ligands (e.g., Gabapentin or Pregabalin)
 
 If pain, tingling, or insomnia are dominant, Gabapentin or Pregabalin may be better than dopamine agonists.
 These drugs are often preferred for people with RLS and depression or anxiety.
 Lifestyle Adjustments
 
 Avoid caffeine, nicotine, and alcohol, as they can worsen RLS.
 Regular moderate exercise (but not too close to bedtime).
 Good sleep hygiene (consistent bedtime, a cool and dark room).
 Massage, warm baths, and stretching before bed.
 Magnesium or Vitamin D Supplementation (if Deficient)
 
 Magnesium and Vitamin D deficiencies are linked to RLS.
 If deficient, supplementation may improve symptoms.

Treatment Task: I am a 22-year-old woman. For at least 2 years, I have noticed an increasing clumsiness when walking. As I have had pronounced hollow feet since I was a child - I inherited them from my mother - which is why I have been wearing special shoes for a long time. But even with these, I feel increasingly unsteady. Unfortunately, I can't bend or stretch my toes. But I think I can feel physical touch on my body normally. I work as a waitress. This means I often have pain in my shoulder, but no other complaints. My doctor has diagnosed me with Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathies. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathies
 
  Model Output:Physiotherapy and Balance Training
 
 Strengthens weak muscles and improves coordination.
 Balance and gait training help prevent falls.
 Focus on ankle and foot stability (e.g., toe curls, resistance exercises).
 Custom Orthotics and Bracing (Ankle-Foot Orthoses, AFOs)
 
 Since your special shoes are not enough, custom orthotics or ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) can help stabilize your feet.
 These prevent ankle rolling and foot drop, which contribute to clumsiness.
 Pain and Fatigue Management
 
 Physical therapy for your shoulder pain (postural exercises, ergonomic adjustments at work).
 Non-opioid pain medications (e.g., acetaminophen, ibuprofen) if needed.
 Avoid neuropathic pain medications unless necessary (since you mainly report motor issues).
 Occupational Therapy (Work Adaptations)
 
 Since you work as a waitress, consider workplace adjustments like:
 Supportive shoes and shock-absorbing insoles.
 Strategic breaks to reduce fatigue.
 Lighter trays or assistive tools if needed.
 Genetic Counseling and Monitoring for Progression
 
 Since HMSN is hereditary, genetic counseling may help plan for future symptoms or family planning.
 Regular neurology follow-ups to monitor progression and adjust treatment.

Treatment Task: I am a 37-year-old man. I have noticed a deterioration in my walking for a few months now. I can't say exactly when it started. I also have increasing pain in my neck and both shoulders. Recently, I have been injuring my arms more frequently, but didn't even realize it at first. For example, I've burned myself several times on the stove when touching hot pots. But I did not realize it right away. My doctor has diagnosed me with Syringomyelia. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Syringomyelia
 
  Model Output:Neurosurgical Evaluation (for Surgical Treatment if Needed)
 
 If the syrinx is enlarging or causing significant symptoms, surgical intervention may be necessary.
 Options include:
 Syrinx drainage (shunt placement) to reduce fluid buildup.
 Decompression surgery (if Chiari malformation is present).
 Pain Management (Neuropathic Pain Medications & Physiotherapy)
 
 Neuropathic pain medications (e.g., Gabapentin, Pregabalin, or Amitriptyline) may help relieve nerve-related pain.
 Physical therapy to improve mobility and posture while minimizing strain on affected areas.
 Avoiding Activities That Worsen Symptoms
 
 Heavy lifting, straining, or sudden movements can increase spinal pressure and worsen symptoms.
 Proper posture and ergonomic adjustments (especially if you work at a desk or do manual labor).
 Regular Neurological Monitoring
 
 Regular MRI scans to track syrinx progression.
 Neurological exams to assess motor and sensory function.
 Protective Measures for Sensory Loss
 
 Since you don’t feel burns or injuries, take precautions:
 Use gloves for cooking or handling hot items.
 Check your skin regularly for unnoticed wounds.

Treatment Task: I am a 30-year-old woman. I am currently in hospital because I had my appendix removed two days ago. I now have pain in my right hand, where the cannula is inserted, and in my right forearm. My right hand is swollen, reddened and sensitive around the cannula. My doctor has diagnosed me with Thrombophlebitis
 
 Thrombophlebitis. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Thrombophlebitis
 
 Thrombophlebitis
 
  Model Output:Immediate Removal of the Cannula
 
 The IV cannula must be removed immediately to prevent further irritation or infection.
 Local Treatment with Warm Compresses
 
 Apply warm compresses (3-4 times daily) to the affected area to reduce pain and swelling and improve blood circulation.
 Anti-Inflammatory Medication
 
 NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen or diclofenac) can help reduce pain and inflammation.
 Topical anti-inflammatory gels (e.g., heparin gel or diclofenac gel) may also help.
 Compression and Elevation
 
 Keeping your hand elevated can help reduce swelling.
 In some cases, a light compression bandage may help improve blood flow and reduce discomfort.
 Monitoring for Infection or Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT)
 
 Watch for signs of infection (increasing redness, pus, fever). If suspected, antibiotics may be needed.
 If symptoms spread beyond the local vein, an ultrasound may be required to check for deeper vein involvement.

disease_GT: Sudeck's atrophy (Complex regional pain syndrome type 1)
Disease_description:
Treatment Task: I am a 60-year-old woman. I have been suffering from severe pain in my right forearm and right hand for about a week. I have a bluish swelling on my forearm. It hurts when I touch it and my whole arm feels warm. I broke my wrist about 2 months ago. The cast was removed 1 week ago. My doctor has diagnosed me with Sudeck's atrophy (Complex regional pain syndrome type 1). What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Sudeck's atrophy (Complex regional pain syndrome type 1)
 
  Model Output:Early and Intensive Physiotherapy
 
 Movement is key to prevent worsening stiffness and pain.
 Gentle mobilization and desensitization exercises can help restore function.
 Mirror therapy may be beneficial in severe cases.
 Pain Management (Multimodal Approach)
 
 NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen, diclofenac) for inflammation and pain.
 Neuropathic pain medications (e.g., gabapentin, pregabalin, or amitriptyline) for nerve-related pain.
 Topical lidocaine or capsaicin creams for local pain relief.
 Medical Therapy to Improve Circulation and Bone Health
 
 Bisphosphonates (e.g., alendronate or pamidronate) may help slow bone loss and reduce pain.
 Vasodilators (e.g., nifedipine) can improve blood flow to the affected limb.
 Physical and Occupational Therapy for Sensory Rehabilitation
 
 Contrast baths (alternating warm and cool water) to improve circulation.
 Tactile desensitization therapy (e.g., gentle brushing or touching different textures) can help with hypersensitivity.
 Occupational therapy to adapt daily activities.
 Nerve Blocks or Spinal Modulation in Severe Cases
 
 If pain is severe and does not respond to medication, sympathetic nerve blocks (e.g., stellate ganglion block) can help reduce symptoms.
 Spinal cord stimulation may be considered for persistent, disabling pain.

Treatment Task: I am a 22-year-old man. I injured my left forearm while gardening. The wound was very small, so I just put a plaster on it. I have had headaches and back pain for a few days now and feel increasingly restless. Noises and strong sunlight cause the muscles in my face to tense up, which I can't control. My doctor has diagnosed me with Tetanus. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Tetanus
 
  Model Output:Immediate Administration of Tetanus Immunoglobulin (TIG)
 
 Neutralizes circulating tetanus toxin.
 Given intramuscularly (IM), usually 3000–6000 IU.
 Wound Care and Antibiotics
 
 The wound must be thoroughly cleaned to remove any remaining bacterial spores.
 Metronidazole or Penicillin G is given to kill Clostridium tetani bacteria.
 Muscle Relaxation and Symptom Control
 
 Benzodiazepines (e.g., Diazepam, Midazolam) help reduce muscle spasms and stiffness.
 Magnesium sulfate may also be used for severe muscle rigidity.
 Tetanus Vaccination (if Not Up to Date)
 
 If you have not received a full tetanus vaccination series (or a booster in the last 10 years), a tetanus vaccine (Td or Tdap) is given after immunoglobulin.
 Supportive Intensive Care (if Severe Cases)
 
 In severe cases, patients may need mechanical ventilation if breathing muscles are affected.
 IV fluids and electrolyte management for systemic support.

Treatment Task: I am a 47-year-old man and work as a long-distance driver. However, I am constantly tired and sometimes fall asleep during the day while working. On two occasions I narrowly escaped a traffic accident. If I sleep at night, I don't feel refreshed afterwards. My wife often complains that I snore very loudly. I suffer from high cholesterol and high blood pressure. I am 176 cm tall and weigh 102 kg. My doctor has diagnosed me with Obstructive sleep apnea. What are the most appropriate therapies in my case? Name up to five.
 
  True Disease: Obstructive sleep apnea
 
  Model Output:Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) Therapy – This is the gold standard treatment for OSA. A CPAP machine delivers a constant stream of air through a mask to keep your airway open while you sleep. It significantly reduces daytime sleepiness and the risk of accidents.
 
 Weight Loss – Reducing body weight can improve OSA symptoms by decreasing fat deposits around the airway, which contribute to obstruction. Even a moderate weight loss of 5-10% can lead to significant improvements.
 
 Mandibular Advancement Device (MAD) – If CPAP is not tolerated, a custom-fitted oral appliance can be an alternative. It repositions the lower jaw and tongue to keep the airway open.
 
 Positional Therapy – Some people experience worse OSA when sleeping on their back. Using special pillows or wearable devices that encourage side-sleeping can help reduce symptoms.
 
 Lifestyle Modifications and Medical Management – Avoiding alcohol and sedatives before bed, maintaining a consistent sleep schedule, and managing high blood pressure and cholesterol can contribute to better sleep and overall health.

Treatment Task: I am a 48-year-old man. I have had persistent pulling pain in my upper abdomen for a few days. The pain radiates into my back like a belt. Medications: While medications are not a long-term solution, they can be used to provide temporary relief from achalasia symptoms. Calcium channel blockers and nitrates can help relax the LES muscles, making it easier to swallow.

Please consult with your doctor to discuss which treatment options are most appropriate for your specific case, as well as any risks or potential side effects associated with each therapy.

  Model Output:It sounds like you've been going through a very tough time with these widespread pains and exhaustion. It's understandable that you are looking for the best ways to manage your Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR). It's good that your doctor has diagnosed you, as this is the first step to getting appropriate treatment.
 
 Polymyalgia Rheumatica is indeed characterized by muscle pain and stiffness, particularly in the neck, shoulders, hips, and thighs, and it often affects people over 50. Your symptoms like pain in the neck, upper arms, back, knees, difficulty moving and stiffness, especially in the morning or after rest, are very typical of PMR.
 
 The primary goal of treatment for PMR is to relieve your pain and stiffness and improve your ability to function. Here are five of the most appropriate therapies for Polymyalgia Rheumatica:
 
 Corticosteroids (like Prednisone): This is the most effective and first-line treatment for PMR. Prednisone is a type of steroid medication that powerfully reduces inflammation in the body. It usually provides significant relief from pain and stiffness within just a few days, sometimes even within 24-48 hours.
 
 How it works: Corticosteroids work by mimicking hormones your body produces naturally to fight inflammation. In PMR, there's inflammation in the lining of joints and bursae (fluid-filled sacs near joints), and prednisone helps to calm this down.
 
 Important to know: While very effective, corticosteroids can have side effects, especially with long-term use. Your doctor will start you on a low dose and gradually reduce it over time (tapering) as your symptoms improve and blood tests indicate the inflammation is under control. Common side effects can include weight gain, increased appetite, mood changes, increased blood sugar, high blood pressure, bone thinning (osteoporosis), and increased risk of infection. Your doctor will monitor you for these side effects and take steps to minimize them.
 
 Calcium and Vitamin D Supplementation: Since long-term corticosteroid use can increase the risk of osteoporosis, it is often recommended to take calcium and vitamin D supplements.
 
 Why it's important: Calcium is essential for bone health, and vitamin D helps your body absorb calcium. These supplements help to protect your bones while you are on corticosteroids.
 
 How to use: Your doctor can advise on the appropriate dosage of calcium and vitamin D for you. You can get calcium through diet as well (dairy products, leafy green vegetables, fortified foods), but supplementation is often needed, especially when on steroids.
 
 Physical Therapy and Gentle Exercise: While PMR causes pain and stiffness, staying as active as possible is important. Once the initial pain is under control with medication, physical therapy and gentle exercise can be very beneficial.
 
 Benefits: Physical therapy can help improve your range of motion, flexibility, and muscle strength. Gentle exercises like walking, swimming, or water aerobics can help reduce stiffness, improve your overall fitness, and boost your mood. It can also help you regain strength lost due to inactivity during periods of pain.
 
 Tailored approach: A physical therapist can design a program specifically tailored to your needs and limitations, considering your farming activities and areas of stiffness.
 
 Regular Monitoring and Follow-up with your Doctor: PMR is a condition that needs ongoing management. Regular follow-up appointments with your doctor are crucial.
 
 Why it's important: Your doctor will monitor your symptoms, adjust your medication dosage as needed (especially tapering the corticosteroids), check for side effects of medications, and ensure the PMR is well-controlled. Blood tests (like ESR and CRP) are often used to monitor inflammation levels.
 
 Long-term management: PMR can sometimes last for 1 to 3 years or even longer. Regular follow-up helps ensure you are on the right treatment path and any flares or complications are addressed promptly.
 
 Pain Management Strategies (Beyond Corticosteroids, if needed): While corticosteroids are the primary treatment, sometimes additional pain management strategies can be helpful, especially in the initial stages or during flares, or if there are persistent symptoms.
 
 Over-the-counter pain relievers: In some cases, your doctor might suggest using mild pain relievers like acetaminophen (paracetamol) for additional pain relief, especially for breakthrough pain. However, it's important to discuss this with your doctor, especially considering your age and other health conditions.
 
 Heat and Cold Therapy: Applying heat packs or warm baths can help soothe stiff muscles. Cold packs can help reduce inflammation and pain.
 
 Mind-body techniques: Techniques like relaxation exercises, deep breathing, meditation, or gentle yoga can help manage pain and improve overall well-being.
 
 Important Considerations for You as a Farmer:
 
 Pacing yourself: Farming is physically demanding. It's crucial to pace yourself, take breaks when needed, and avoid overexertion, especially when you are experiencing pain or fatigue.
 
 Assistive devices: Consider using tools or assistive devices that can make farming tasks easier and reduce strain on your body.
 
 Listen to your body: Pay attention to your pain signals and rest when you need to. Don't push yourself too hard, especially in the beginning of treatment and during flares.
 
 It is absolutely essential to discuss these therapies and any other concerns you have with your doctor. They are the best person to determine the most appropriate treatment plan for you based on your individual health status, the severity of your PMR, and any other medical conditions you may have. They will guide you on the correct dosage of medication, monitor your progress, and help you manage any potential side effects.

 Given your age, pre-existing conditions (high blood pressure, coronary heart disease, stents), and current medications (Ramipril, Atorvastatin, ASS), the choice of asthma therapy needs to be carefully considered to ensure both effectiveness and safety.
 
 Here are up to five of the most appropriate therapies for bronchial asthma in your case, which your doctor may consider. It's important to discuss these options and any other potential treatments with your doctor to determine the best course of action for you personally.
 
 Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS): These are the cornerstone of asthma treatment and are usually the first-line controller medication. ICS are anti-inflammatory drugs that reduce swelling and mucus production in the airways, making them less sensitive and reducing asthma symptoms. They are inhaled directly into the lungs, minimizing systemic side effects. Examples include beclomethasone, budesonide, ciclesonide, fluticasone, and mometasone.  Short-Acting Beta-2 Agonists (SABA): These are bronchodilators used as "reliever" or "rescue" medications. They quickly relax the muscles around your airways, opening them up and making it easier to breathe. They provide rapid relief from acute asthma symptoms like coughing, wheezing, and shortness of breath. Salbutamol (also known as albuterol) and terbutaline are common examples. It's important to use SABAs as needed for symptom relief but not as a regular daily treatment if possible, as frequent use can indicate poorly controlled asthma.  Combination Inhalers (ICS/LABA): If inhaled corticosteroids alone are not enough to control your asthma symptoms, your doctor may consider a combination inhaler. These inhalers contain both an inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) and a long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA). LABAs are bronchodilators that work similarly to SABAs but have a longer duration of action (around 12 hours). Using a combination inhaler can improve asthma control, reduce symptoms, and decrease the risk of exacerbations. Examples include budesonide/formoterol, fluticasone/salmeterol, and fluticasone/formoterol. These can be very effective for asthma control, and the LABA component needs to be used regularly with an ICS, which is already present in these combination inhalers.
 
 Leukotriene Receptor Antagonists (LTRAs): These are oral medications that block the action of leukotrienes, which are inflammatory chemicals in the body that contribute to asthma symptoms. Montelukast is a common example. LTRAs can help to reduce inflammation and mucus production in the airways and can be used as an add-on therapy to ICS or as an alternative controller medication in some cases. They are generally well-tolerated and may be a good option, especially as an oral medication can be easier for some patients to manage. They also have a good safety profile in patients with cardiovascular disease.
 
 Patient Education and Asthma Action Plan: This is not a medication, but it is a crucial part of asthma therapy. Understanding your asthma, triggers, medications, and how to manage symptoms is vital. Your doctor should provide you with a personalized asthma action plan. This plan will outline:
 
 How to monitor your asthma symptoms.

 What to do if your asthma worsens, including when to seek urgent medical care.
 
 Strategies to avoid your asthma triggers (if known).

 Ramipril and Cough: Ramipril, an ACE inhibitor, is known to sometimes cause a dry cough as a side effect. While you have been diagnosed with asthma, it's worth discussing with your doctor whether your cough could be partly or fully related to Ramipril. Do not stop taking Ramipril without consulting your doctor first. They may consider switching you to a different type of blood pressure medication (like an angiotensin receptor blocker - ARB) if they suspect Ramipril is contributing to the cough, but this would be a decision your doctor needs to make based on your overall health picture.
 
 Heart Conditions and Asthma Medication: Most asthma medications, especially inhaled therapies, are safe for patients with heart conditions when used as prescribed. However, it's crucial that your doctor is aware of your coronary heart disease and stents when choosing and adjusting your asthma treatment. They will select medications and dosages that are appropriate for your specific situation and monitor for any potential interactions or side effects.
 
 Regular Follow-up: Asthma management is often ongoing and requires regular follow-up appointments with your doctor. This allows them to assess how well your asthma is controlled, adjust your medications if needed, and address any concerns you may have.
 
 It is essential to have an open and detailed discussion with your doctor about these and other potential asthma therapies. They will take into account your specific medical history, current medications, the severity of your asthma, and your personal preferences to create the most effective and safe treatment plan for you.  Prevent future strokes: This might involve antiplatelet drugs like aspirin or clopidogrel, or anticoagulants like warfarin or newer oral anticoagulants, depending on the cause of your stroke.
